Rising-edge trigger with hysteresis fires 0-3 times per trajectory regardless of dt.
AI.conclusion: Wall-clock-calibrated leaky-integrator monitors cannot serve as moment detectors on agent streams; transition detection escapes the trap but does not match human intervention timing.

title: ImageWAM: Do World Action Models Really Need Video Generation, or Just Image Editing?
summary: World Action Models (WAMs) commonly rely on video generation to bridge visual world modeling and robot control. However, video-based WAMs face three coupled limitations: dense multi-frame future tokens make inference costly, full video prediction spends capacity on action-irrelevant temporal and appearance details, and long-horizon future imagination may introduce errors that mislead action prediction. These issues raise a simple question: Does world action model really need video generation? We propose ImageWAM, a simple WAM framework that repurposes pretrained image editing models for robot action prediction. In contrast to video generation, image editing provides a better-matched prior: it only needs to model a target-frame transformation, focuses on action-relevant current-to-target visual differences, and grounds task instructions to localized visual changes through edit pretraining. In practice, ImageWAM does not decode the target frame at inference time; instead, it conditions a flow-matching action expert on the KV caches produced by image-editing denoising, using them as a compact world-action context. ImageWAM outperforms standard VLA baselines and matching competitive WAMs without additional policy pretraining across different simulator and real-world experiments. It also reduces FLOPs to 1/6 and latency to 1/4 of video-based WAMs. Attention analysis further shows that editing caches focus on task-relevant change regions, supporting image editing as an effective alternative to video-based world-action modeling.

AI.tldr: ImageWAM replaces video generation with image editing for world action models, achieving better performance, lower computational cost (1/6 FLOPs, 1/4 latency), and task-relevant visual focus by using editing-based KV caches to condition flow-matching action prediction.
AI.motivation: Video-based WAMs suffer from three coupled limitations: high inference cost due to dense multi-frame token prediction, wasted capacity on action-irrelevant details, and accumulated errors in long-horizon video prediction that mislead action decisions.
AI.method: ImageWAM repurposes pretrained image editing models for robot action prediction. It uses image-editing denoising to produce KV caches (compact world-action context) that condition a flow-matching action expert, without decoding the actual target frame at inference time.
AI.result: Outperforms standard VLA baselines and competitive WAMs across simulator and real-world experiments, reduces FLOPs to 1/6 and latency to 1/4 of video-based WAMs, and attention analysis shows editing caches focus on task-relevant change regions.
AI.conclusion: Image editing is an effective and efficient alternative to video generation for world-action modeling, as it provides a better-matched prior: target-frame transformation focus, action-relevant visual differences, and localized change grounding through edit pretraining.

title: Hidden Anchors in Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation
summary: Multi-agent LLM deliberation, where agents exchange and revise answers over several rounds, is increasingly used to improve reasoning and accuracy, yet how and why it works is rarely modelled. Such deliberation mirrors how humans reach decisions. As social animals we are pulled both by the group, the herd effect that classical opinion-dynamics models such as DeGroot and Friedkin--Johnsen capture, and by our own internal belief, which they do not. We model multi-agent deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system in which each agent carries a hidden internal belief, its anchor, that continually pulls its opinion regardless of its neighbours. We show this anchor can be recovered from the deliberation alone, and that it explains a behaviour classical consensus rules forbid: an agent's confidence in the correct answer can climb past where any agent started, escaping the space (convexhull) formed by the initial beliefs. Checking whether the recovered anchor also predicts held-out runs (generalizes) gives a simple test for when a model is truly driven bysuch an anchor. Across three open-weight model families this is a spectrum, not all-or-nothing. All anchors' influence are about equally strongly, but they differ in where the anchor sits, and only when it sits far from the initial opinions does deliberation escape the hull and need the full closed-loop model.

AI.tldr: This paper models multi-agent LLM deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system where each agent has a hidden internal belief (anchor) that constantly pulls its opinion. The anchor can be recovered from deliberation alone and explains why confidence in correct answers can exceed initial beliefs, escaping the convex hull. The model provides a test for anchor-driven behavior and shows that across LLM families, anchor influence is a spectrum.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM deliberation is increasingly used but lacks theoretical modeling of how and why it works. Existing opinion-dynamics models (DeGroot, Friedkin-Johnsen) capture group influence but fail to account for agents' persistent internal beliefs.
AI.method: The authors model deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system where each agent has a hidden anchor (internal belief) that continually pulls its opinion regardless of neighbors. They show the anchor is recoverable from deliberation traces and propose a generalization test to validate anchor-driven behavior.
AI.result: The anchor explains why confidence can escape the convex hull of initial beliefs. Across three open-weight LLM families, anchor influence is a spectrum (not binary). All anchors exert similar influence strength, but differ in anchor position; only when the anchor sits far from initial opinions does deliberation escape the hull.
AI.conclusion: The closed-loop anchor model is necessary when deliberation escapes the convex hull; the generalization test can identify when this dynamics applies. The work bridges opinion-dynamics theory and LLM multi-agent systems.

title: Performance Analysis and Optimization of 3D Generative Diffusion Models across GPU Architectures
summary: Diffusion models have become essential for high-fidelity 3D MRI synthesis, yet their deployment remains constrained by substantial GPU resource demands arising from hundreds of U-Net evaluations per sample and a highly heterogeneous kernel behavior. This paper performs a comprehensive performance analysis of the state-of-the-art medical diffusion model, Med-DDPM, across three generations of NVIDIA architectures to study kernel-level runtime breakdowns, instruction-mix characteristics, memory system utilization, warp-level activities, and profiler priority-score estimates. We show that training is overwhelmingly dominated by cuDNN convolution and implicit-GEMM kernels, with inefficiencies arising from memory-access patterns, tensor-layout conversions, and limited Tensor Core utilization. Guided by these insights, we evaluate two architecture-aware optimizations TF32 Tensor Core activation and a 3D channels-last layout and demonstrate that they reduce SM cycles by up to 100x, cut dynamic instructions by 100x, raise Tensor Core utilization from 1.45 to 9.98x, and increase IPC by 7% on A100, all without degrading synthesis quality.

AI.tldr: This paper profiles Med-DDPM across three NVIDIA GPU architectures, identifies cuDNN convolution/GEMM kernels as bottlenecks due to memory patterns and low Tensor Core utilization, and proposes TF32 and 3D channels-last optimizations that yield up to 100x reduction in SM cycles and instructions, 9.98x higher Tensor Core use, and 7% IPC gain on A100 with no quality loss.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models for 3D MRI synthesis demand excessive GPU resources due to hundreds of U-Net evaluations per sample and heterogeneous kernel behavior, hindering deployment.
AI.method: Comprehensive kernel-level performance analysis of Med-DDPM across three NVIDIA GPU generations, examining runtime breakdowns, instruction-mix, memory system, warp-level activities, and profiler priority scores; then evaluating TF32 Tensor Core activation and a 3D channels-last layout optimization.
AI.result: TF32 Tensor Core and 3D channels-last optimizations reduce SM cycles by up to 100x, cut dynamic instructions by 100x, raise Tensor Core utilization from 1.45 to 9.98x, and increase IPC by 7% on A100, with no synthesis quality degradation.
AI.conclusion: Architecture-aware optimizations significantly improve GPU efficiency for medical diffusion models without compromising output quality, enabling more practical deployment.

title: Exploring Pre-training Benefits on Phoneme Addition through Fine-tuning in Speech Synthesis
summary: Transfer learning is widely used for low-resource text-to-speech. When the target corpus contains phonemes unseen in pre-training, the model must expand its phoneme inventory during fine-tuning; we call the process "phoneme addition." However, it remains unclear whether the pre-trained ability to generate seen phonemes contributes to this process. This study investigates phoneme addition in two settings: (1) a simulation setup using LLM-generated phoneme-controlled corpora that enables investigation without considering confounding factors, and (2) a real-speech cross-lingual transfer setup (English to Japanese) to validate whether the findings hold in practice. Experiments in both settings showed that while fine-tuning achieved higher naturalness than training from scratch, it required as much or more data to achieve comparable PER for new phonemes. These results indicate that pre-training mainly contributes to naturalness improvement, but offers limited benefit for phoneme addition.

AI.tldr: Transfer learning with pre-trained TTS models does not significantly accelerate phoneme addition (learning unseen phonemes) compared to training from scratch, though it improves naturalness.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether pre-trained TTS models' ability to generate seen phonemes aids in learning new, unseen phonemes (phoneme addition) during fine-tuning, as this remains an open question.
AI.method: Two experimental setups: (1) simulation with LLM-generated phoneme-controlled corpora to isolate confounding factors, and (2) real-speech cross-lingual transfer from English to Japanese.
AI.result: Fine-tuning achieved higher naturalness than training from scratch, but required as much or more data to achieve comparable PER for new phonemes.
AI.conclusion: Pre-training primarily benefits naturalness improvement but offers limited advantage for phoneme addition.

title: Interpretable and Verifiable Hardware Generation with LLM-Driven Stepwise Refinement
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success in software development. However, they are susceptible to hallucinations, meaning that they can introduce subtle semantic and logical errors. Due to the high stakes in chip design and manufacturing, hardware engineers are still reluctant to rely on LLMs for register-transfer level (RTL) generation. In this paper, we propose a hardware generation framework that combines the creativity and broad knowledge of LLMs with the explainability and mathematical rigor of formal methods. Specifically, we devise a set of transformation rules that cover various design decisions and hardware features. By iteratively applying these rules, an LLM agent can convert a design specification into an RTL program with guaranteed correctness. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the framework.

AI.tldr: Proposes a hardware generation framework combining LLMs with formal methods to produce correct-by-construction RTL code through iterative application of transformation rules.
AI.motivation: LLMs hallucinate when generating RTL code, introducing subtle semantic/logical errors, which makes hardware engineers reluctant to trust them for chip design due to high stakes.
AI.method: Devises a set of transformation rules covering design decisions and hardware features; an LLM agent iteratively applies these rules to convert a design specification into a provably correct RTL program.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed framework.
AI.conclusion: Combining LLM creativity with formal method rigor enables trustworthy RTL generation, potentially increasing adoption of LLMs in hardware design.

title: PerceptionDLM: Parallel Region Perception with Multimodal Diffusion Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in visual understanding tasks. However, most existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive generation, which limits their efficiency for perception tasks that require captioning multiple regions. In this work, we propose PerceptionDLM, a multimodal diffusion language model optimized for efficient parallel region perception. Built upon PerceptionDLM-Base, a strong foundational baseline that achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source diffusion MLLMs, our architecture fully leverages the parallel decoding nature of DLMs. Specifically, we introduce efficient prompting and structured attention masking to enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions, allowing the model to generate region descriptions in parallel at both the sequence and token levels. This design significantly improves inference efficiency compared with existing approaches that process regions sequentially. To systematically evaluate the parallelism property of visual perception capability for DLMs, we construct a new Parallel Detailed Localized Captioning Benchmark (ParaDLC-Bench) by scaling the DLC-Bench to include multiple region masks per image, enabling joint evaluation of both caption quality and inference efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that PerceptionDLM maintains competitive performance in region captioning while achieving substantial speed improvements for multi-region perception tasks. Our results highlight the potential of multimodal diffusion language models for efficient, parallel visual perception. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to achieve parallel region caption and perception by leveraging the advantages of diffusion language models. Code, models, and datasets are released.

AI.tldr: PerceptionDLM is a multimodal diffusion language model that enables efficient parallel region captioning by leveraging diffusion language models' parallel decoding nature, outperforming autoregressive MLLMs on multi-region perception tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive generation, which limits efficiency for perception tasks requiring captioning of multiple regions sequentially.
AI.method: PerceptionDLM introduces efficient prompting and structured attention masking on top of a strong foundational baseline (PerceptionDLM-Base) to enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions at both sequence and token levels.
AI.result: PerceptionDLM achieves competitive region captioning performance while delivering substantial speed improvements for multi-region perception tasks.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal diffusion language models have strong potential for efficient parallel visual perception, with PerceptionDLM being the first to achieve parallel region caption and perception using diffusion language models.

title: DeXposure-Claw: An Agentic System for DeFi Risk Supervision
summary: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLM agents fit this setting poorly: they over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations offer no regulator-aligned way to measure the resulting false alarms. We introduce DeXposure-Claw, a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system that routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model, forecasts future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios then turn those forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; and (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation before DeXposure-Claw emits auditable supervisory tickets with rationales. We further develop DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis evaluation harness, whose decision axis scores tickets against a regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and an explicit false-intervention rate. Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support our system. Code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/DeXposure-Claw.

AI.tldr: DeXposure-Claw is a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system for decentralized finance that uses a graph time-series foundation model, deterministic monitors, and confidence gates to generate auditable supervisory tickets, along with DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis evaluation harness with regulator-aligned ground truth, demonstrating effectiveness on five years of real data.
AI.motivation: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLM agents over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions, and existing evaluations offer no regulator-aligned way to measure resulting false alarms.
AI.method: DeXposure-Claw routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model forecasting future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios producing typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; (3) data-health and confidence gates constraining escalation before emitting auditable supervisory tickets. DeXposure-Bench is a six-axis evaluation harness with a decision axis scoring tickets against regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and false-intervention rate.
AI.result: Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support the system.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system effectively addresses the limitations of general-purpose LLM agents in decentralized finance supervision by grounding LLM decisions through structured evidence, forecasts, and regulator-aligned evaluation metrics.

title: Information Lattice Learning as Probabilistic Graphical Model Structure Learning
summary: Information lattice learning (ILL) learns interpretable rules of a signal by alternately projecting the signal onto a partition lattice that encodes a hierarchy of abstractions and lifting selected rules back to the signal domain. When the signal is a probability mass function, we show the probabilistic rules learned by ILL admit a natural probabilistic graphical model (PGM) interpretation and develop this interpretation in detail. A partition in ILL induces a deterministic quotient variable, and a rule is the marginal law of that quotient variable. A rule set is therefore a collection of marginal constraints over interpretable abstractions. General lifting is the feasible family of all joint distributions satisfying those constraints, while special lifting chooses a maximum-ignorance reconstruction, implemented in ILL by an L2 uniformity principle closely related to maximum entropy. Under a Shannon-entropy lifting, the same constraints yield a log-linear factor graph whose factors are indexed by learned abstractions. The information lattice itself, however, is not a Bayesian network: its edges encode refinement and coarsening of abstractions, not conditional dependence. Thus ILL is best viewed as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables. This view clarifies how ILL relates to graphical models and maximum entropy models, while suggesting new directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a probabilistic graphical model interpretation for information lattice learning (ILL), showing that rules learned by ILL correspond to marginal constraints over quotient variables, and lifting corresponds to maximum-entropy reconstruction, yielding log-linear factor graphs indexed by learned abstractions—positioning ILL as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs rather than Bayesian networks.
AI.motivation: To bridge information lattice learning (ILL) with probabilistic graphical models (PGMs), providing a formal interpretation of ILL's learned rules and lifting mechanisms in probabilistic terms, and clarifying how ILL relates to graphical models and maximum entropy models.
AI.method: The paper formally connects ILL to PGMs by: (1) interpreting partitions as deterministic quotient variables and rules as marginal laws; (2) defining general lifting as the feasible family of joint distributions satisfying marginal constraints, and special lifting as a maximum-ignorance (L2 uniformity) reconstruction; (3) showing Shannon-entropy lifting yields log-linear factor graphs indexed by learned abstractions; (4) distinguishing ILL's refinement/coarsening edges from Bayesian network conditional dependence edges.
AI.result: ILL rules are equivalent to marginal constraints over interpretable quotient variables; lifting reconstructs a joint distribution satisfying those constraints; under Shannon entropy, this yields a log-linear factor graph; ILL is not a Bayesian network but rather structure learning for constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables.
AI.conclusion: ILL is best understood as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables, offering new directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

title: PolSeT: Polish Semantics of Timbre Dataset
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures. Data descriptor for the PolSeT dataset (Polish Semantics of Timbre), available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17830609 under CC BY 4.0
summary: This data report introduces PolSeT (Polish Semantic Timbre), a dataset designed to facilitate research in psychoacoustics and Music Information Retrieval (MIR) in Polish and cross-cultural contexts. The dataset contains data from two sequential experiments. Experiment 1 (N=60) was a free-verbalization task aimed at creating a lexicon of Polish semantic descriptors. Using 11 stimuli, a total of 1901 descriptors (701 unique) were gathered. Experiment 2 (N=105) utilized this lexicon to conduct a semantic differential study, where participants rated 18 instrument sounds on 8 bipolar scales, with repeated trials for reliability analysis. The released dataset includes raw listener responses, comprehensive demographics (experience, gender, age), audio stimuli, and extracted acoustic features with Python extraction code. This dataset addresses a gap in open timbre research data, providing both the qualitative linguistic groundwork and the quantitative ratings necessary for psychoacoustic research and the training of multilingual semantic embedding models.

AI.tldr: PolSeT (Polish Semantic Timbre) is a dataset of Polish semantic descriptors for timbre, collected via two experiments: free-verbalization (N=60, 1901 descriptors) and semantic differential ratings (N=105, 18 sounds, 8 bipolar scales). It includes raw responses, demographics, audio, and acoustic features for psychoacoustics and MIR research.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of open timbre research data in Polish and cross-cultural contexts, providing both qualitative linguistic descriptors and quantitative ratings for psychoacoustic research and multilingual semantic embedding model training.
AI.method: Two sequential experiments: (1) free-verbalization task with 60 participants using 11 stimuli to collect 1901 semantic descriptors (701 unique); (2) semantic differential study with 105 participants rating 18 instrument sounds on 8 bipolar scales with repeated trials for reliability.
AI.result: A dataset containing raw listener responses, demographics (experience, gender, age), audio stimuli, extracted acoustic features, and Python extraction code.
AI.conclusion: PolSeT fills a gap in open timbre research by providing both qualitative linguistic groundwork and quantitative ratings, enabling psychoacoustic research and multilingual semantic embedding model development for Polish.

title: Beyond the GUI Paradigm: Do Mobile Agents Need the Phone Screen?
summary: Recent advances in mobile agents are dominated by the GUI paradigm, in which agents perceive UI information and emit screen interactions. However, mobile platforms also expose a command-line interface (CLI) that provides direct access to device services and data. We argue CLI deserves first-class consideration alongside GUI. We evaluate three coding agents (Claude Code, Terminus-2, mini-swe-agent) across four model APIs on AndroidWorld and MobileWorld without any mobile-specific post-training, comparing against three reproducible GUI baselines (GUI-Owl-1.5-32B, MAI-UI, Qwen3-VL-32B). Claude Code (Opus 4.7) reaches 71.8\% and 51.9\%, outperforming every reproducible GUI baseline (69.3/68.1/57.8\% on AndroidWorld; 43.2/26.3/13.3\% on MobileWorld), while every other CLI configuration remains competitive. To establish the paradigm's ceiling, we provide oracle CLI solutions that reach 88.8\% on AndroidWorld (103/116 tasks CLI-solvable) and 86.3\% on MobileWorld (101/117 tasks CLI-solvable), indicating substantial room for future improvement. To cover everyday user intents beyond the GUI scope, we introduce the \textbf{CLI-Advantage Task Suite}, comprising 45 templates across five categories: bulk operations, multi-condition filtering, aggregation, cross-app workflows, and hidden device state. Every CLI agent outperforms every GUI baseline in all five categories, with substantially fewer steps per task (10.7 vs.\ 18.6). To support future research on mobile CLI agents, we will open-source agent implementations, oracle solutions, the CLI-Advantage suite, and evaluation infrastructure.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that command-line interface (CLI) agents deserve first-class consideration alongside GUI agents on mobile platforms. Experiments show that CLI-based agents (Claude Code, Terminus-2, mini-swe-agent) outperform all reproducible GUI baselines on AndroidWorld and MobileWorld, and the authors introduce the CLI-Advantage Task Suite covering tasks where CLI agents have inherent advantages. Oracle solutions demonstrate the paradigm's ceiling at ~87-89% success rate.
AI.motivation: Mobile agent research is dominated by the GUI paradigm, but mobile platforms also expose CLI interfaces that provide direct access to device services and data. The authors argue CLI deserves equal consideration as a viable paradigm for mobile agents.
AI.method: Evaluated three coding agents (Claude Code, Terminus-2, mini-swe-agent) across four model APIs on AndroidWorld and MobileWorld without mobile-specific post-training, comparing against three reproducible GUI baselines (GUI-Owl-1.5-32B, MAI-UI, Qwen3-VL-32B). Also created oracle CLI solutions to measure the paradigm ceiling and introduced the CLI-Advantage Task Suite covering five categories: bulk operations, multi-condition filtering, aggregation, cross-app workflows, and hidden device state.
AI.result: Claude Code (Opus 4.7) achieves 71.8% and 51.9%, outperforming all GUI baselines (best GUI: 69.3% and 43.2%). Oracle solutions reach 88.8% (AndroidWorld) and 86.3% (MobileWorld). CLI agents outperform GUI baselines in all five CLI-Advantage categories with fewer steps per task (10.7 vs 18.6).
AI.conclusion: CLI agents represent a highly effective paradigm for mobile task automation, outperforming GUI agents on existing benchmarks and having clear advantages for tasks involving bulk operations, filtering, aggregation, cross-app workflows, and hidden device states. The substantial gap between current CLI agent performance and oracle solutions indicates significant room for improvement.

title: Mix-QVLA: Task-Evidence-Aware Mixed-Precision Quantization of Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: We propose Mix-QVLA, a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision PTQ framework for VLA models. Mix-QVLA anchors each quantized variant to the full-precision action-token reference decision and evaluates whether quantization preserves task-relevant evidence across key VLA functional boundaries. It computes normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations and compares full-precision and quantized maps using evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion, capturing changes in both the strength and allocation of decision-supporting evidence. A soft-bottleneck objective aggregates boundary-level degradation into layer-wise sensitivity scores. Mix-QVLA further models sensitivity throughout task execution, capturing phase-dependent shifts in layer importance rather than assuming a fixed sensitivity profile. The resulting evidence- and time-aware scores guide mixed-precision bit allocation under model-size and BitOps budgets. Extensive evaluations on OpenVLA-style policies show that Mix-QVLA improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off of low-bit VLA deployment. On LIBERO, Mix-QVLA reduces OpenVLA-OFT memory from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB, retains 96.3 average success compared with 97.1 for the BF16 model, and achieves a 1.52x inference speedup.

AI.tldr: Mix-QVLA is a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision PTQ framework for VLA models that uses gradient-weighted task-evidence maps and soft-bottleneck objectives to guide bit allocation, achieving 4.1 GB memory (from 15.4 GB), 96.3% success rate, and 1.52x speedup on LIBERO.
AI.motivation: Existing PTQ methods for VLA models fail to preserve task-relevant decision evidence across functional boundaries and assume fixed layer sensitivity profiles, leading to suboptimal accuracy-efficiency trade-offs in low-bit deployment.
AI.method: Mix-QVLA computes normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations, compares full-precision and quantized maps using evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion, aggregates degradation via a soft-bottleneck objective into layer-wise sensitivity scores, and models sensitivity shifts throughout task execution phases to guide mixed-precision bit allocation under model-size and BitOps budgets.
AI.result: On OpenVLA-style policies evaluated on LIBERO, Mix-QVLA reduces memory from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB, retains 96.3% average success rate (vs. 97.1% for BF16), and achieves 1.52x inference speedup.
AI.conclusion: Mix-QVLA significantly improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for low-bit VLA deployment through evidence-aware and time-aware mixed-precision quantization.

title: LLM Doesn't Know What It Doesn't Know: Detecting Epistemic Blind Spots via Cross-Model Attribution Divergence on Clinical Tabular Data
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to structured clinical data, yet whether they can recognize the limits of their own knowledge on such tasks remains unexplored. We study this question through the lens of cross-model attribution divergence with the goal of reducing epistemic uncertainty for structured tasks, comparing Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost on a prediction task via attribution divergence analysis. We report four findings. First, LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous, it outputs a near-constant (0.856-0.937) regardless of whether accuracy is 49% or 75.3%, tracking prompt format rather than prediction quality. Second, the LLM exhibits an inverse difficulty effect: accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct, but matches XGBoost (73.8% vs. 73.1%) when it is moderately uncertain. Third, few-shot examples and SHAP-derived feature evidence are orthogonal, super-additive interventions: they reduce the Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS) from 1.54 to 0.38 and improve accuracy from 49% to 75.3% without training. Fourth, a cross-model calibrator that determined LLM reliability using attribution divergence signals reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080, replacing uninformative verbalized confidence with patient-specific reliability estimates, without accessing model internals or requiring repeated inference. We frame these findings as a cold start problem for LLMs on structured data and outline a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness.

AI.tldr: LLMs cannot reliably gauge their own knowledge limits on structured clinical tasks; their verbalized confidence is uninformative and decoupled from accuracy. However, cross-model attribution divergence analysis—comparing LLM and XGBoost attributions—reveals systematic uncertainty patterns, enables super-additive improvements via few-shot + SHAP evidence, and allows a calibrator to replace vacuous confidence with patient-specific reliability estimates (reducing ECE from 0.254 to 0.080).
AI.motivation: To investigate whether LLMs can recognize the limits of their own knowledge (epistemic self-awareness) when applied to structured clinical data, given their increasing deployment in such tasks.
AI.method: Cross-model attribution divergence analysis comparing Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost on a structured prediction task; analysis of LLM verbalized confidence, inverse difficulty effect, super-additive few-shot + SHAP interventions, and a cross-model calibrator using attribution divergence signals.
AI.result: (1) LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous (near-constant 0.856–0.937 across 49%–75.3% accuracy); (2) LLM exhibits inverse difficulty effect (64.8% accuracy when XGBoost is 99% correct); (3) few-shot + SHAP evidence reduces ADS from 1.54 to 0.38 and boosts accuracy from 49% to 75.3%; (4) cross-model calibrator reduces ECE from 0.254 to 0.080 without accessing model internals.
AI.conclusion: LLMs face a cold-start problem on structured data and lack genuine epistemic self-awareness, but attribution divergence signals offer a pathway toward reliable uncertainty estimation and improved performance without training.

title: Weibull Weight-Scale Parameter Evolution under AdamW Training Dynamics
summary: Building on a two-parameter Weibull framework for diagnosing transformer weight distributions, we study why the Weibull weight-scale parameter $λ$ grows, overshoots, and then relaxes during AdamW training. We derive a leading-order three-force decomposition of the squared weight norm from the AdamW update: an alignment force measuring the correlation between weights and the adaptive update direction, an injection force from adaptive step magnitude, and a decay force from decoupled weight decay. On self-trained Pythia-70M models with ground-truth optimizer moments, alignment dominates the rise phase, contributing 88-94% of the absolute force budget across four random seeds and remaining robust to super-weight removal. Near saturation, alignment and decay approach balance, explaining the transition from weight-scale growth to relaxation. These force dynamics directly govern the squared-norm component underlying $λ(t)$; the remaining RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset is measurable and decomposes into bridge and integration components, totaling approximately 5-6% in densely sampled regions. To extend the analysis to real models where optimizer moments are unavailable, we introduce a spline displacement method that recovers the alignment force from sparse checkpoints with approximately 92-94% accuracy, about twice the naive two-point baseline. We further observe that the peak value of $λ(t)$ varies with training-data coherence in our experiments, suggesting a data-dependent component of weight-scale growth that we leave to a controlled follow-up study. Code and data are available at https://github.com/tiexinding/NPM-Weibull-public.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the dynamics of the Weibull weight-scale parameter λ during AdamW training, identifying three forces (alignment, injection, decay) that govern its growth, overshoot, and relaxation. Alignment dominates the rise phase (88-94%), and a spline displacement method recovers alignment from sparse checkpoints with ~92-94% accuracy. The peak of λ(t) varies with training-data coherence, suggesting data-dependent weight-scale growth.
AI.motivation: To understand why the Weibull weight-scale parameter λ grows, overshoots, and then relaxes during AdamW training, by decomposing the underlying dynamics of squared weight norm.
AI.method: Derives a leading-order three-force decomposition (alignment, injection, decay) from AdamW updates; validates on self-trained Pythia-70M models with ground-truth optimizer moments; proposes a spline displacement method to recover alignment force from sparse checkpoints when optimizer moments are unavailable.
AI.result: Alignment force contributes 88-94% of absolute force budget during rise phase; alignment and decay approach balance near saturation; RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset is ~5-6% in densely sampled regions; spline displacement method recovers alignment force with ~92-94% accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The dynamics of λ(t) are governed by a three-force balance where alignment drives growth and decay drives relaxation; the peak varies with training-data coherence, indicating data-dependent influences on weight-scale growth that warrant follow-up study.

title: Segment-Level Mandarin Chinese Speech-Based Cognitive Impairment Detection via an Autoencoder with Contrastive Learning
summary:
oindent	extbf{Background and Objective:} Speech has emerged as a low-cost and non-invasive digital biomarker with considerable potential for cognitive impairment detection. However, limited labeled data and cross-dataset variability remain major challenges for robust speech-based screening systems.
  \par
oindent	extbf{Methods:} We developed a segment-level representation learning framework for speech-based cognitive impairment detection. Speech recordings were divided into short segments and converted into spectrogram representations. To improve robustness under limited-data conditions, offline and online augmentation strategies were combined with autoencoder-based representation learning and contrastive objectives to enhance discriminative latent representations.
  \par
oindent	extbf{Results:} Experiments conducted on four independent Mandarin Chinese speech datasets demonstrated stable and competitive performance in both binary and three-class classification tasks, with particularly notable improvements in the clinically challenging three-class setting. Ablation studies further supported the effectiveness of the proposed framework.
  \par
oindent	extbf{Conclusions:} The findings suggest that segment-level speech representation learning may provide a scalable and practical approach for cognitive impairment screening in resource-constrained clinical settings.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a segment-level representation learning framework for speech-based cognitive impairment detection that combines autoencoder-based learning with offline/online augmentation and contrastive objectives, achieving stable performance across four Mandarin Chinese datasets, particularly in challenging three-class classification tasks.
AI.motivation: Speech is a low-cost, non-invasive digital biomarker for cognitive impairment detection, but limited labeled data and cross-dataset variability hinder robust screening systems.
AI.method: A segment-level representation learning framework: speech recordings are divided into short segments, converted to spectrograms, and processed with offline/online augmentation, autoencoder-based representation learning, and contrastive objectives to improve discriminative latent representations.
AI.result: Experiments on four independent Mandarin Chinese speech datasets showed stable and competitive performance in binary and three-class classification, with notable improvements in the clinically challenging three-class setting. Ablation studies confirmed the framework's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Segment-level speech representation learning may offer a scalable and practical approach for cognitive impairment screening in resource-constrained clinical settings.

title: Execution-bound advisory automation for agentic AI: a reproducible AIBOM-driven CSAF-VEX framework
summary: A protocol driven framework is presented that binds SBOM and AIBOM artefacts to deterministic environment capture and structured runtime telemetry. Exploitability is computed from declared artefacts, observed activation conditions, and enforced execution policies. CSAF VEX advisories are generated from combined static and runtime evidence, cryptographically signed, and validated through deterministic replay. Evaluation uses approximately 10000 component entries across synthetic Agentic AI workloads 50 to 5000 components, incorporating OSV, GitHub Advisory, KEV, and EPSS datasets.

AI.tldr: A protocol-driven framework binds SBOM/AIBOM artefacts with runtime telemetry to compute exploitability, generate signed CSAF VEX advisories from static and dynamic evidence, and validate through deterministic replay; evaluated with ~10K components across synthetic AI workloads.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of assessing exploitability in Agentic AI systems by combining declarative software and AI bill-of-materials (SBOM/AIBOM) with deterministic environment capture and structured runtime telemetry.
AI.method: The framework integrates declared artefacts (SBOM/AIBOM) with runtime activation conditions and enforcement policies. Exploitability is computed from these combined sources. CSAF VEX advisories are generated from static + runtime evidence, cryptographically signed, and validated via deterministic replay.
AI.result: Evaluated on ~10,000 component entries across synthetic Agentic AI workloads (50–5000 components), using OSV, GitHub Advisory, KEV, and EPSS datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates a viable approach for generating signed, evidence-based vulnerability advisories for complex AI systems by merging static declarations with runtime observability under deterministic replay validation.

title: Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings for Controllable and Generalizable Perception
summary: Vision foundation models are typically trained as static feature extractors, placing the burden of task adaptation onto large downstream models. We propose an alternative paradigm: instead of solely feeding visual features into language models, we use language itself to dynamically guide the vision encoder. Our method, Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings (LIVE), leverages language as high-level guidance to produce task-centric embeddings at inference time, removing the need for task-specific retraining. This enables the encoder to focus on contextually relevant aspects of the input, yielding more controllable and generalizable representations. Empirically, LIVE reduces visual hallucinations (+34 points on MMVP), surpasses vision-language models with orders of magnitude more parameters on visual question answering, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks -- offering a direct path toward adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence.

AI.tldr: LIVE uses language to dynamically guide vision encoders at inference time, producing task-centric embeddings without retraining, reducing hallucinations by 34 points on MMVP and surpassing much larger vision-language models on VQA.
AI.motivation: Vision foundation models are typically static feature extractors that require large downstream models for task adaptation. The authors propose shifting the paradigm: using language itself to dynamically guide the vision encoder, removing the need for task-specific retraining.
AI.method: LIVE (Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings) leverages language as high-level guidance to dynamically guide the vision encoder at inference time, producing task-centric embeddings instead of static visual features.
AI.result: Reduces visual hallucinations by +34 points on MMVP; surpasses vision-language models with orders of magnitude more parameters on visual question answering; generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks.
AI.conclusion: LIVE offers a direct path toward adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence by producing more controllable and generalizable representations through language-guided vision encoding.

title: REVEAL++: Differentiable Phenotypic Grouping for Vision-Language Retinal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Risk
summary: The retina offers a noninvasive window into neurodegenerative disease, capturing subtle structural patterns associated with a risk of future cognitive decline. Vision-language alignment frameworks such as REVEAL have shown that pairing retinal fundus images with structured clinical risk narratives improves early prediction of Alzheimer's disease (AD). A key design choice in these approaches is the use of phenotypic grouping, where individuals with similar risk profiles are treated as multi-positive pairs during contrastive learning. However, existing methods operationalize phenotypic similarity as a discrete construct, relying on hard group assignments that impose rigid supervision and decouple group formation from representation learning. We propose a continuous formulation of phenotypic structure within contrastive learning. Rather than assigning samples to fixed clusters, we model inter-subject similarity as a differentiable weighting function derived from intra-modality embedding similarities in both retinal images and risk profiles. These weights define soft multi-positive relationships through a continuous aggregation operator, enabling graded supervision that reflects the spectrum nature of disease risk. We further introduce a soft-target contrastive objective that jointly learns cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure in an end-to-end manner. Evaluated on UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the proposed framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines. By treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal rather than a fixed grouping rule, our approach provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a continuous formulation of phenotypic similarity within contrastive learning for Alzheimer's disease prediction from retinal fundus images and clinical risk narratives, replacing discrete group-based methods with a differentiable weighting function that enables soft multi-positive relationships and end-to-end cross-modal alignment.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language alignment methods for AD prediction use discrete phenotypic groupings (hard group assignments) that impose rigid supervision and decouple group formation from representation learning, failing to capture the continuous spectrum of disease risk.
AI.method: Models inter-subject similarity as a differentiable weighting function from intra-modality embedding similarities (retinal images + risk profiles), defines soft multi-positive relationships via continuous aggregation, and introduces a soft-target contrastive objective for joint cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure learning.
AI.result: On UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the continuous framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines.
AI.conclusion: Treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal rather than a fixed grouping rule provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

title: Zero-Inflated Gaussian Distributions Enable Parameter-Space Sparsity in Estimation-of-Distribution Algorithms
summary: Estimation-of-distribution algorithms (EDAs) are a powerful class of evolutionary methods for black-box optimization, especially when little is known about the structure of the objective. Whereas classical evolutionary algorithms rely on hand-designed mutation and crossover operators, hard to devise for unknown problem structures, and a source of bias, EDAs sidestep operator design entirely: they fit a probability distribution to the best individuals and sample the next generation from it. EDAs are well established on continuous parameter spaces, but they have not previously been generalized to sparse ones, in which most coefficients of a good solution are exactly zero. Existing sparse black-box optimizers therefore reintroduce exactly what EDAs were designed to avoid: hand-crafted sparsity operators, bi-level schemes alternating between support set and active values, zeroing thresholds, and other baked-in assumptions. We close this gap by proposing multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as EDA sampling laws. A latent Gaussian model with separate indicator and value dimensions represents sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and the interactions between the two, so sparsity patterns and active values are optimized jointly, hierarchy-free. We show that the latent parameters of this model are identifiable from observed samples, unlike in the missing-data settings where related constructions originate, and introduce practical amortized inversion-based estimators for them. The estimators accurately recover latent correlation structures, and on the Lunar Lander benchmark the resulting ZIG-EDA converges faster and reaches higher final returns than a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA, while finding controllers with only a small fraction of parameters active.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as sampling laws for estimation-of-distribution algorithms (EDAs) to handle sparse black-box optimization, where most optimal solution coefficients are zero. This approach jointly optimizes sparsity patterns and active values without hand-crafted operators, and demonstrates faster convergence and better results on benchmarks than dense EDAs, hand-crafted sparse EAs, and ad-hoc sparse EDAs.
AI.motivation: Existing EDAs work well on continuous parameter spaces but have not been generalized to sparse spaces (where most coefficients of a good solution are exactly zero). Existing sparse black-box optimizers reintroduce hand-crafted sparsity operators, bi-level schemes, zeroing thresholds, and other baked-in assumptions—exactly what EDAs were designed to avoid.
AI.method: The paper proposes multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as EDA sampling laws. A latent Gaussian model with separate indicator and value dimensions represents sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and their interactions. The latent parameters are identifiable from observed samples, and practical amortized inversion-based estimators are introduced.
AI.result: The estimators accurately recover latent correlation structures. On the Lunar Lander benchmark, ZIG-EDA converges faster and reaches higher final returns than a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA, while finding controllers with only a small fraction of parameters active.
AI.conclusion: ZIG distributions successfully close the gap of EDAs not being generalized to sparse spaces, enabling joint optimization of sparsity and active values without hand-crafted operators or hierarchy, with strong empirical performance.

title: DevOps and General Developers: Insights from Stack Overflow's 2023 Survey
comment: 17 pages, 11 tables, research paper based on the 2023 Stack Overflow Developer Survey data analysis
summary:
Purpose: To investigate the distinct roles of DevOps specialists and general software developers, examining their varying use of tools, technologies, methodologies, and demographics in the current software development environment. In addition, to differentiate these two professional groups regarding their unique contributions and challenges in the field.
  Design/Methodology/Approach: The research uses a quantitative approach to analyze data from the Stack Overflow 2023 Developer Survey. It focuses on a comparative analysis of technological preferences, demographic information, and professional experiences between DevOps specialists and general developers, highlighting key trends and differences. The data analysis was conducted using Python's Pandas library for data analysis.
  Findings: The research indicates no significant difference in the tool and technology preferences between DevOps specialists and general software developers, highlighting their complementary roles. DevOps specialists and general software developers use tools like Docker and Kubernetes, emphasizing efficiency and automation. While general developers employ diverse tools for various role demands, demographic trends show younger general developers and mid-career DevOps professionals. This age range reflects growing experience in DevOps, and both groups are adapting to remote and hybrid work models in the evolving tech industry.
  Practical Implications: This research offers perspectives on the dynamic roles within software development, emphasizing the growing importance of DevOps. It is a valuable resource for academic and industry professionals to understand the evolving dynamics in software development roles.
  Originality/Value: This research fills a significant gap in the existing literature regarding the evolving dynamics of software development roles.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes Stack Overflow 2023 survey data to compare DevOps specialists and general software developers, finding no significant differences in tool/technology preferences, with both groups using tools like Docker and Kubernetes, while demographic trends show younger general developers and mid-career DevOps professionals adapting to remote/hybrid work models.
AI.motivation: To investigate the distinct roles of DevOps specialists versus general software developers in terms of tools, technologies, methodologies, and demographics, and to differentiate their unique contributions and challenges.
AI.method: Quantitative analysis of Stack Overflow 2023 Developer Survey data using Python's Pandas library, with comparative analysis of technological preferences, demographics, and professional experiences between DevOps specialists and general developers.
AI.result: No significant difference in tool/technology preferences between the two groups; both use Docker and Kubernetes for efficiency/automation; general developers use more diverse tools; demographic trends show younger general developers and mid-career DevOps professionals; both groups adapting to remote/hybrid work.
AI.conclusion: DevOps specialists and general developers play complementary rather than distinct roles in tool usage; the research fills a gap in literature on evolving software development role dynamics.

title: Emergent Alignment
summary: Can Large Language Models (LLMs) discern when their own outputs are misaligned with human ethics? And can they self-correct? We endow an LLM with a conscience step that reviews its own reasoning and outputs, and we extend the training loss with an alignment component using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to steer the model away from non-ethical outputs. The result is an online technique to align models in a wide range of applications: training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot learning. It does not require a weaker or stronger judge, relying instead on a frozen copy of itself. In previous work, the Emergent Misalignment scenario showed a range of emergent unethical behaviors from fine-tuning the model to hack code. Instead, we empirically show how to achieve Emergent Alignment: a single high-level introspective question steers training toward an ethical model under the same code hacking scenario.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an alignment technique where LLMs introspectively review their own outputs and use DPO-based training to self-correct unethical behavior, demonstrating Emergent Alignment in scenarios where prior work showed Emergent Misalignment.
AI.motivation: To enable LLMs to autonomously detect and correct misalignment with human ethics without relying on external judges or human feedback, addressing the problem of emergent unethical behaviors from fine-tuning.
AI.method: Introduces a "conscience step" for LLMs to review their own reasoning and outputs, combined with an alignment loss component using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). The model uses a frozen copy of itself rather than external judges, and applies a single high-level introspective question during training.
AI.result: Achieves Emergent Alignment in a code hacking scenario, where a single introspective question steers the model toward ethical behavior, in contrast to the Emergent Misalignment observed in prior work.
AI.conclusion: The proposed online alignment technique is effective across training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot learning, enabling self-alignment without external supervision.

title: Human-like autonomy emerges from self-play and a pinch of human data
summary: Self-play reinforcement learning has recently emerged as a way to train driving policies without any human data. It uses cheap, large-scale simulations to substitute expensive, large-scale human driving demonstrations. A key limitation of this approach is that policies trained through pure self-play can learn effective but alien driving conventions incompatible with people. Previous works attempt to mitigate such behavioral misalignments through extensive reward engineering and domain randomization, which are brittle and labor-intensive. Instead of completely discarding human demonstrations, our method treats them as a regularization objective on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward. Like the spice in a good stew, we find that a little human data goes a long way: our method uses only 30 minutes of human demonstrations, 2500x fewer than comparable imitation learning approaches. Resulting policies coordinate with held-out human trajectories and complete training in 15 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU. Videos and full source code are available at https://spiced-self-play.com/.

AI.tldr: Self-play RL for driving policies works better with just 30 minutes of human data (2500x less than imitation learning) as regularization, achieving human-compatible coordination in 15 hours on a single GPU.
AI.motivation: Pure self-play reinforcement learning for driving policies learns effective but alien driving conventions incompatible with humans. Previous mitigation via reward engineering and domain randomization is brittle and labor-intensive.
AI.method: Treats human demonstrations as a regularization objective on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward, using only 30 minutes of human data (2500x less than comparable imitation learning).
AI.result: Resulting policies coordinate with held-out human trajectories, complete training in 15 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU.
AI.conclusion: A small amount of human data, used as regularization in self-play RL, efficiently produces human-compatible driving policies without large-scale human demonstrations or extensive reward engineering.

title: Zero-VC: Zero-Lookahead Streaming Voice Conversion via Speaker Anonymization
summary: Streaming zero-shot voice conversion struggles to disentangle timbre from linguistic content without degrading utility or inflating latency. Current methods rely on information bottleneck (IB) or speaker perturbation. While IB filters out timbre, it discards prosody, forcing models to explicitly inject features like fundamental frequency. This often requires buffering future frames, creating algorithmic lookahead latency. On the other hand, existing perturbation methods largely overlook the crucial trade-off between timbre leakage and utility preservation. Recognizing this neglected trade-off, we find that the inherent objective of Speaker Anonymization (SA) aligns well with balancing these factors. Thus, we introduce SA as a novel perturbation mechanism to explicitly mitigate timbre leakage while retaining prosodic utility. Crucially, SA's robust representations significantly alleviate the generator's reliance on future context, enabling our strictly causal, zero-lookahead network. Audio samples are available at https://amphionteam.github.io/Zero-VC-demo/.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Speaker Anonymization (SA) as a perturbation mechanism for streaming zero-shot voice conversion, achieving zero-lookahead latency while balancing timbre leakage and prosodic utility retention.
AI.motivation: Streaming zero-shot voice conversion struggles with timbre-linguistic content disentanglement without degrading utility or increasing latency. Existing methods (IB and speaker perturbation) have limitations: IB discards prosody requiring explicit feature injection with lookahead latency; perturbation methods overlook the trade-off between timbre leakage and utility preservation.
AI.method: The authors propose using Speaker Anonymization (SA) as a novel perturbation mechanism to explicitly mitigate timbre leakage while retaining prosodic utility. SA's robust representations reduce the generator's reliance on future context, enabling a strictly causal, zero-lookahead network.
AI.result: SA-based perturbation effectively balances timbre leakage and utility preservation, and its robust representations allow for zero-lookahead streaming voice conversion.
AI.conclusion: Speaker Anonymization serves as an effective perturbation mechanism for streaming zero-shot voice conversion, addressing the critical trade-off between timbre leakage and utility while eliminating algorithmic lookahead latency.

title: JustDiag!: A Diagnostic Justification Engine for Accountable Root Cause Analysis
summary: Large language models can produce fluent root cause analyses, but fluent final answers alone are insufficient evidence for accountability in high-stakes operations. In real incident response, engineers need to know what evidence supported a diagnosis, which alternatives were considered, where contradictions remained, and whether the system resolved the case or preserved uncertainty. We address this gap with JustDiag, a diagnostic justification engine for RCA that maintains an explicit process state over evidence, findings, competing hypotheses, conflicts, and next checks. We evaluated the system on 66 real-world incidents using a two-layer protocol that separately scores final-answer quality and process quality. Relative to a matched control without diagnostic justification, JustDiag achieved stronger outcome and process scores, while accepting slightly lower terminal completion due to more calibrated non-closure. These results suggest that accountable RCA requires explicit diagnostic justification artifacts and process-aware evaluation, not only fluent final answers.

AI.tldr: JustDiag is a diagnostic justification engine for root cause analysis that maintains explicit process state over evidence, hypotheses, and conflicts, achieving better outcome and process scores than a control system without diagnostic justification, at the cost of slightly lower terminal completion due to more calibrated non-closure.
AI.motivation: Fluency in root cause analyses is insufficient for accountability in high-stakes operations; engineers need justification artifacts such as evidence, alternatives considered, contradictions, and uncertainty resolution, which current LLM-based RCA systems lack.
AI.method: JustDiag maintains an explicit process state tracking evidence, findings, competing hypotheses, conflicts, and next checks. Evaluated on 66 real-world incidents using a two-layer protocol that separately scores final-answer quality and process quality, compared against a matched control without diagnostic justification.
AI.result: JustDiag achieved stronger outcome and process scores than the control, while accepting slightly lower terminal completion due to more calibrated non-closure (i.e., appropriately preserving uncertainty rather than confidently producing incorrect answers).
AI.conclusion: Accountable RCA requires explicit diagnostic justification artifacts and process-aware evaluation, not only fluent final answers.

title: Learning When to Denoise: Optimizing Asynchronous Schedules for Latent Diffusion
summary: Multi-representation diffusion models can improve visual synthesis by denoising complementary views of an image, but their performance depends critically on the asynchronous schedule that determines when each representation is denoised. We propose to learn this schedule. Our method formulates asynchronous flow matching over multiple representation spaces and uses a schedule-corrected objective that keeps each representation's local noising-time weights fixed as the schedule changes. We instantiate the schedule with a flexible parametric class that is convex and monotone by construction, and learn it using a fast joint probe with less than 1% additional training compute. On ImageNet 256x256, the learned schedule substantially improves both convergence speed and final quality under a matched 675M-parameter XL backbone. With AutoGuidance, our 200-epoch model reaches FID 1.05, matching the 800-epoch SFD-XL baseline with 4x less training. Training to 600 epochs further improves to FID 1.02, outperforming the 1B-parameter SFD-XXL result of FID 1.04 while using a smaller model. In the unguided setting, our 200-epoch model reaches FID 2.37, already below the best 800-epoch SFD-XL result (2.54) at 4x less training, and improves to FID 2.14 at 600 epochs. Code is available at https://github.com/bsq532087/LWD

AI.tldr: The paper proposes learning the asynchronous denoising schedule for multi-representation diffusion models, using schedule-corrected flow matching and a flexible parametric schedule class, achieving 4x faster training and state-of-the-art FID scores on ImageNet 256x256.
AI.motivation: Multi-representation diffusion models rely on an asynchronous schedule that determines when each representation is denoised, but this schedule is critical to performance and has been hand-designed; the authors propose to learn it automatically to improve efficiency and quality.
AI.method: The method formulates asynchronous flow matching over multiple representation spaces with a schedule-corrected objective that keeps local noising-time weights fixed. The schedule is instantiated via a convex, monotone parametric class and learned using a fast joint probe requiring less than 1% additional training compute.
AI.result: On ImageNet 256x256 with a 675M-parameter XL backbone: with AutoGuidance, the 200-epoch model reaches FID 1.05 (matching 800-epoch SFD-XL with 4x less training); 600 epochs improves to FID 1.02, outperforming 1B-parameter SFD-XXL (FID 1.04). In the unguided setting, 200 epochs achieves FID 2.37 (beating 800-epoch SFD-XL 2.54), and 600 epochs reaches FID 2.14.
AI.conclusion: Learning the asynchronous schedule substantially improves both convergence speed and final quality, enabling smaller models to outperform much larger baselines with significantly less training.

title: ITNet: A Learnable Integral Transform That Subsumes Convolution, Attention, and Recurrence
summary: Convolutional networks, recurrent networks, and transformers each encode different inductive biases -- locality, sequential memory, and content-dependent pairwise interaction -- and have remained mathematically distinct since their inception. We show that this fragmentation reflects not a fundamental diversity in how signals should be processed, but rather incomplete views of a single underlying mathematical object: a learnable integral transform. We introduce the Integral Transform Network (ITNet), a unified architecture built around a learnable kernel that depends jointly on positions and features. This kernel is implemented as a small neural network, specifically an MLP, that models pairwise interactions, enabling the model to adapt its behavior from data. We show that convolution, self-attention (including multi-head), and autoregressive recurrence (including LSTM, GRU, S4, and Mamba) arise as special cases under appropriate parameterizations, and that ITNet is a universal approximator of continuous operators. To make this practical, we develop tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, and learned low-rank factorization, enabling efficient and scalable computation. A single ITNet architecture with a shared operator and lightweight modality-specific encoders matches or exceeds specialized baselines on ImageNet-1K , GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA\,v2 and NLVR2. The results demonstrate that a single learned interaction mechanism can recover the behavior of all three architectural families from data.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes the Integral Transform Network (ITNet), a unified architecture where convolution, self-attention, and recurrent models emerge as special cases of a learnable integral transform with a position- and feature-dependent kernel implemented as an MLP. ITNet matches or exceeds specialized baselines across vision, language, and 3D tasks.
AI.motivation: The fragmented landscape of deep learning architectures (convolutional, recurrent, transformer) suggests fundamentally different signal processing mechanisms. The paper challenges this by showing these are incomplete views of a single underlying mathematical object: a learnable integral transform.
AI.method: ITNet uses a learnable kernel (an MLP) that depends jointly on positions and features to model pairwise interactions. It recovers convolution, self-attention (multi-head), and recurrence (LSTM, GRU, S4, Mamba) as special cases under appropriate parameterizations. Efficiency is achieved via tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, and learned low-rank factorization.
AI.result: A single ITNet architecture with a shared operator and lightweight modality-specific encoders matches or exceeds specialized baselines on ImageNet-1K (vision), GLUE (language), ModelNet40 (3D), VQA v2, and NLVR2 (multimodal).
AI.conclusion: A single learned interaction mechanism can recover the behavior of all three major architectural families (convolutional, recurrent, transformer) from data, demonstrating that architectural diversity is unnecessary when a sufficiently general operator is used.

title: ProMUSE: Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty-guided Staged Evidential Alzheimer Disease Classification
summary: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a fatal disorder that destroys memory and cognitive skills in the elderly population. Most treatments for AD are effective in the early stage, leading to an increasing demand for early AD diagnosis. AD diagnosis increasingly relies on multimodal data such as clinical assessments, structural Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), and Positron Emission Tomography (PET) imaging. However, MRI and PET acquisition remain costly and not universally accessible, making full-modality inference impractical in real-world clinical workflows. We propose ProMUSE, a Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty Guided Staged Evidential Network that adaptively determines when additional modalities are necessary, helping reduce the overall cost of data acquisition while maintaining accuracy. ProMUSE first performs evidential classification using low-cost clinical data and quantifies uncertainty via a Dirichlet-based subjective logic model. When uncertainty exceeds a learned threshold, ProMUSE progressively incorporates MRI or PET features, fusing modality-wise belief and uncertainty through Dempster-Shafer theory to obtain a calibrated multimodal prediction. This staged acquisition strategy enables accurate diagnosis while minimizing reliance on expensive imaging. Experiments on ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS across CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks demonstrate that ProMUSE achieves competitive or superior accuracy compared to full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50-90%, yielding substantial cost savings. These results highlight ProMUSE as a practical, uncertainty-aware, and resource-efficient solution for real-world AD screening.

AI.tldr: ProMUSE is a progressive multimodal evidential network that uses uncertainty quantification to determine when expensive MRI/PET modalities are needed for Alzheimer's diagnosis, reducing imaging usage by 50-90% while maintaining diagnostic accuracy.
AI.motivation: Early AD diagnosis increasingly relies on multimodal data (clinical assessments, MRI, PET), but MRI and PET are costly and not universally accessible, making full-modality inference impractical in real-world clinical workflows.
AI.method: ProMUSE first performs evidential classification using low-cost clinical data with a Dirichlet-based subjective logic model to quantify uncertainty. When uncertainty exceeds a learned threshold, it progressively incorporates MRI or PET features, fusing beliefs via Dempster-Shafer theory for calibrated multimodal prediction.
AI.result: On ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS datasets across CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks, ProMUSE achieves competitive or superior accuracy compared to full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50-90%.
AI.conclusion: ProMUSE provides a practical, uncertainty-aware, and resource-efficient solution for real-world AD screening by adaptively determining when additional costly modalities are necessary.

title: MixProLAP: Mixture-Induced Uncertainty Modeling for Probabilistic Language-Audio Pretraining
summary: Acoustic environments often contain multiple overlapping sound events, and the same acoustic scene can be described using diverse textual expressions, making audio-text alignment inherently ambiguous. This paper proposes a probabilistic audio-language pretraining framework to model many-to-many correspondence ambiguity in audio-text alignment. Unlike conventional contrastive methods that learn deterministic point embeddings, our approach represents each modality as a distribution and learns uncertainty-aware cross-modal alignment. Rather than relying on masking-based uncertainty simulation, we mix audio-text pairs to create overlapping sounds that better reflect real acoustic mixtures and capture semantic inclusion relations among sound events. We further introduce a multi-level inclusion loss to enforce representations consistent with these relations. Experiments on audio-text retrieval benchmarks show that the proposed method outperforms deterministic baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a probabilistic audio-language pretraining framework that models many-to-many correspondence ambiguity by representing each modality as a distribution and using mixed audio-text pairs with a multi-level inclusion loss for uncertainty-aware cross-modal alignment, outperforming deterministic baselines on audio-text retrieval.
AI.motivation: Acoustic environments often contain multiple overlapping sound events, and the same scene can be described with diverse textual expressions, creating inherent many-to-many correspondence ambiguity in audio-text alignment that conventional deterministic contrastive methods fail to address.
AI.method: Represent each modality as a distribution (rather than deterministic point embeddings), mix audio-text pairs to create overlapping sounds reflecting real acoustic mixtures, and introduce a multi-level inclusion loss to enforce consistency with semantic inclusion relations among sound events.
AI.result: The proposed probabilistic framework outperforms deterministic baselines on audio-text retrieval benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Modeling cross-modal alignment with uncertainty-aware distributions and leveraging mixed audio-text pairs with inclusion-aware supervision is an effective strategy for handling many-to-many correspondence ambiguity in audio-language pretraining.

title: OpenRath: Session-Centered Runtime State for Agent Systems
summary: Modern agent systems often suffer from fragmented runtime state: transcripts, tool effects, memory events, workspace placement, branch provenance, and replay evidence are recorded separately and become difficult to inspect or reproduce. OpenRath addresses this issue with a PyTorch-like programming model for multi-agent, multi-session systems. The analogy concerns the role of a central first-class runtime abstraction, not tensor computation. Its core abstraction is Session, the runtime value passed between agents and workflows. A Session is branchable, inspectable, replayable, backend-aware, and composable. It records conversation chunks, sandbox placement, lineage metadata, token usage, pending work, and tool evidence, while defining where memory interactions enter the runtime record. Since this state is carried by the same value used in program execution, fork, merge, and replay become explicit runtime operations rather than states reconstructed from external traces. OpenRath further defines Sandbox, Tool, Agent, Memory, Workflow, and Selector, with Selector turning control flow into runtime-routed decisions. This report presents the programming model, architecture, audited milestones, and evidence protocol. Its claims are limited to controlled runtime properties, while broad quantitative comparisons, live-provider quality, optional-backend availability, and memory quality are left for follow-on evaluation. The central thesis is that Session provides agent systems with a first-class runtime value for auditable composition.

AI.tldr: OpenRath introduces Session as a first-class runtime abstraction for multi-agent systems, enabling auditable composition by unifying fragmented state (transcripts, tool effects, memory, lineage, replay) into a single branchable, inspectable, and replayable value that flows through agents and workflows.
AI.motivation: Modern agent systems suffer from fragmented runtime state where transcripts, tool effects, memory events, workspace placement, branch provenance, and replay evidence are recorded separately, making inspection and reproduction difficult.
AI.method: OpenRath proposes a PyTorch-like programming model centered on the Session abstraction — a first-class runtime value that is branchable, inspectable, replayable, backend-aware, and composable. It defines components including Sandbox, Tool, Agent, Memory, Workflow, and Selector, where Selector turns control flow into runtime-routed decisions and Session carries all runtime state (conversation chunks, sandbox placement, lineage metadata, token usage, pending work, tool evidence) to enable explicit fork, merge, and replay operations.
AI.result: The report presents the programming model, architecture, audited milestones, and evidence protocol. Claims are limited to controlled runtime properties; broad quantitative comparisons, live-provider quality, optional-backend availability, and memory quality are left for future evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Session provides agent systems with a first-class runtime value for auditable composition, making fork, merge, and replay explicit runtime operations rather than states reconstructed from external traces.

title: TeleMorpher: Toward Robust Simultaneous Motion-Location Editing
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in image and video generation and editing. While recent studies have extended these efforts toward motion editing, simultaneously transforming both motion and location-despite its practical importance-remains largely unexplored.
