  The measurement also serves as a targeted compression signal: recoverable blocks admit large single-layer replacements (GPT-2's early FFN at 8x fewer parameters for +0.77 perplexity), while low-recoverability blocks flag where this is unsafe. It further exposes a methodological pitfall: trained linear baselines can badly under-converge on ill-conditioned transformer activations, so we report the exact closed-form least-squares ceiling throughout.

AI.tldr: The paper measures how nonlinear transformer FFN blocks actually are by computing their linear recoverability (R²_lin) via closed-form least-squares approximation. Results show high heterogeneity across blocks (near-linear to strongly nonlinear), that recoverability is learned not architectural, that residual nonlinearity is higher-order/bilinear probes don't capture it, and that R²_lin serves as a compression signal and reveals optimization pitfalls in linear baselines.
AI.motivation: FFNs are commonly treated as nonlinear stores of computation, but how nonlinear trained FFN blocks actually are has rarely been quantified. The authors aim to measure this directly.
AI.method: Each FFN is treated as a position-wise input-to-output map, split into the exact least-squares linear approximation plus a residual. Linear recoverability (R²_lin) is computed as the held-out variance explained by the closed-form linear map. A low-rank bilinear probe is also applied to the residual.
AI.result: R²_lin is highly heterogeneous and non-monotone with depth across GPT-2, Pythia-160m, and llama-160m (ranging from >0.99 to <0.3). Same-width GELU models have different profiles. Bilinear probes recover only a few points of R², uncorrelated with residual nonlinearity. Recoverable blocks admit large single-layer replacements (8x fewer parameters for +0.77 perplexity in GPT-2 early FFN). Linear baselines under-converge on ill-conditioned activations.
AI.conclusion: Recoverability is a learned property of individual trained blocks, not an architectural one. R²_lin can serve as a targeted compression signal and identifies a methodological pitfall in prior linear baseline evaluations.

title: The Hidden Environmental Cost of Poor Coding Practices in TensorFlow and Keras Applications: A Study on Resource Leaks and Carbon Emissions
summary: Efficiency and sustainability are critical considerations in the development and deployment of machine learning (ML) applications. Among the factors influencing sustainability, resource leaks in ML code can introduce hidden inefficiencies that elevate energy consumption and CO2 emissions. Despite this, empirical evidence quantifying their environmental impact remains limited. This emerging results paper presents an initial empirical investigation of two common resource-leak smells, namely Improper Model Reuse (IMR) and Unreleased Tensor References (UTR), and their impact on energy consumption and CO2 emissions in TensorFlow and Keras workloads. Controlled experiments were conducted for each smell by executing identical training tasks while comparing against a smell-free baseline. Our preliminary results show that both smells consistently increase estimated electricity usage and carbon emissions. IMR and UTR increased electricity consumption by approximately 32% and 46%, respectively, with proportional increases in CO2 emissions. Paired statistical tests indicate that these differences are systematic and statistically significant, providing initial empirical evidence that resource-leak smells may degrade ML energy efficiency and environmental sustainability. These findings suggest that resource-leak smells pose measurable risks to both software quality and sustainability, emphasizing the importance of integrating resource-lifecycle management and energy-efficiency considerations into ML development.

AI.tldr: This emerging results paper investigates two common resource-leak smells (Improper Model Reuse and Unreleased Tensor References) in TensorFlow/Keras workloads and finds they increase electricity consumption by ~32% and ~46% respectively, with proportional CO2 emission increases, providing initial empirical evidence that resource-leak smells degrade ML energy efficiency and environmental sustainability.
AI.motivation: Despite the critical importance of efficiency and sustainability in ML applications, empirical evidence quantifying the environmental impact of resource-leak smells (such as hidden inefficiencies that elevate energy consumption and CO2 emissions) remains limited.
AI.method: Controlled experiments comparing identical training tasks with and without two resource-leak smells (IMR and UTR) in TensorFlow and Keras workloads, using paired statistical tests to evaluate differences.
AI.result: IMR increased electricity consumption by ~32% and UTR by ~46%, with proportional increases in CO2 emissions; paired statistical tests confirmed these differences are systematic and statistically significant.
AI.conclusion: Resource-leak smells pose measurable risks to both software quality and sustainability, emphasizing the need to integrate resource-lifecycle management and energy-efficiency considerations into ML development.

title: Occ-VLM: Occupancy Grounded Vision Language Model for Indoor Scene Understanding
summary: Recently, vision-language models (VLMs) have made significant progress in 3D scene understanding, driving advances in applications such as embodied intelligence and robotic vision. However, existing approaches typically either rely directly on explicit 3D inputs (e.g., point clouds or RGB-D sequences), or introduce an additional 3D geometry encoder to derive 3D-aware visual tokens from 2D images. Such designs structurally decouple 3D geometric perception from the rich 2D semantics learned via vision-language pre-training, hindering the development of a unified 3D vision-language representation. In this work, we propose Occ-VLM, a novel framework for 3D scene understanding that operates purely on posed RGB images and employs a single 2D vision encoder. Specifically, Occ-VLM reconstructs 3D scene occupancy as an auxiliary geometric prior, which is utilized to spatially associate foreground 2D tokens with 3D space. These tokens are then decoded by a Large Language Model (LLM) for unified scene understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Occ-VLM achieves both accurate geometric perception and robust vision-language reasoning: it attains state-of-the-art performance on multi-view occupancy prediction, while performing on par with 3D-input VLMs on 3D Visual Question Answering (VQA) and 3D dense captioning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Occ-VLM is a VLM framework for 3D scene understanding that operates solely on posed RGB images with a single 2D vision encoder, using 3D scene occupancy reconstruction as an auxiliary geometric prior to spatially associate foreground 2D tokens with 3D space, achieving state-of-the-art on multi-view occupancy prediction and competitive results on 3D VQA and dense captioning.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs for 3D understanding either rely on explicit 3D inputs (point clouds, RGB-D) or add a separate 3D geometry encoder, which structurally decouples 3D geometric perception from rich 2D semantics learned during vision-language pre-training, hindering a unified 3D vision-language representation.
AI.method: Occ-VLM uses only posed RGB images with a single 2D vision encoder, reconstructs 3D scene occupancy as an auxiliary geometric prior to spatially associate foreground 2D tokens with 3D space, and decodes these tokens via an LLM for unified scene understanding.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multi-view occupancy prediction, and performs on par with 3D-input VLMs on 3D VQA and 3D dense captioning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Occ-VLM demonstrates that a unified 2D-based pipeline with occupancy as geometric prior can achieve both accurate geometric perception and robust vision-language reasoning, eliminating the need for explicit 3D inputs or additional 3D encoders.

title: VERITAS: Verifier-Guided Proof Search for Zero-Shot Formal Theorem Proving
summary: LLM-based formal provers often collapse rich verifier signals (syntax errors, type mismatches, partial goal progress) into a binary pass/fail bit. We present VERITAS, a zero-shot framework that routes every verifier signal back into proof search through a two-phase protocol: Best-of-N sampling first, then a critic-guided MCTS pass that ingests Phase 1 failures as explicit negative examples. The protocol preserves every theorem solved by its own Phase 1 sweep, so Phase 2's additional solves are attributable to feedback-driven exploration. VERITAS reaches 40.6% on miniF2F (vs. an independently run Best-of-5 at 36.9%, Portfolio 26.2%) and 7.3% on VERITAS-CombiBench, a 55-theorem combinatorics benchmark we release on which Best-of-5 (1.8%) falls below Portfolio (3.6%), exposing that unguided sampling hurts when correct lemma names must be recovered iteratively from verifier feedback. Artifacts are available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: VERITAS is a zero-shot framework that routes verifier signals back into proof search via a two-phase protocol (Best-of-N sampling + critic-guided MCTS), achieving 40.6% on miniF2F and 7.3% on the new VERITAS-CombiBench, outperforming baselines by leveraging feedback-driven exploration.
AI.motivation: LLM-based formal provers discard rich verifier signals (syntax errors, type mismatches, partial progress) by reducing them to binary pass/fail; VERITAS aims to leverage these signals to improve proof search.
AI.method: Two-phase protocol: Phase 1 performs Best-of-N sampling; Phase 2 runs critic-guided MCTS that ingests Phase 1 failures as explicit negative examples to guide exploration.
AI.result: 40.6% on miniF2F (vs. Best-of-5 36.9%, Portfolio 26.2%); 7.3% on VERITAS-CombiBench (vs. Best-of-5 1.8%, Portfolio 3.6%).
AI.conclusion: Routing verifier signals back into proof search via feedback-driven exploration significantly improves performance, especially in tasks requiring iterative recovery of correct lemma names from verifier feedback.

title: CoRaCommit: A VS Code Extension for Commit Message Generation with Exemplar Retrieval
comment: 17 pages, 6 images, 3 tables, Manuscript submitted to a Journal (2026)
summary: Commit messages are essential textual artifacts that describe the intent behind code changes, and play a critical role in version control, code review, and historical tracking. However, in practice, commit messages are primarily authored manually, which is time-consuming and often results in inconsistent quality and non-uniform expression. Existing VS Code extensions for commit message generation typically directly invoke large language models based on the code diff, without leveraging similar commit exemplars as references, and rarely support user feedback-driven LLM recommendation. To address these limitations, this paper presents CoRaCommit, a VS Code extension that enhances commit message generation by retrieving similar commit exemplars as prompt context, invoking multiple LLMs in parallel for candidate commit message comparison, and dynamically recommending LLMs based on user feedback. Experimental results on 945 commits from the ApacheCM dataset show that CoRaCommit outperforms existing VS Code extensions across BLEU, CIDEr, METEOR, and ROUGE-L metrics, demonstrating the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented context for commit message generation.

AI.tldr: CoRaCommit is a VS Code extension that improves commit message generation by retrieving similar commit exemplars as context, using multiple LLMs in parallel, and dynamically recommending LLMs based on user feedback, outperforming existing extensions on the ApacheCM dataset.
AI.motivation: Commit messages are manually authored, time-consuming, and suffer from inconsistent quality. Existing VS Code extensions directly invoke LLMs based on code diff without using similar commit exemplars and rarely support user feedback-driven LLM recommendation.
AI.method: CoRaCommit retrieves similar commit exemplars as prompt context, invokes multiple LLMs in parallel for candidate comparison, and dynamically recommends LLMs based on user feedback.
AI.result: On 945 commits from the ApacheCM dataset, CoRaCommit outperforms existing VS Code extensions across BLEU, CIDEr, METEOR, and ROUGE-L metrics.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-augmented context with similar commit exemplars and feedback-driven LLM recommendation effectively enhances commit message generation quality.

title: HypOProto: Hyperbolic Ordinal Prototypes for Left Ventricular Filling Pressure Classification
summary: Echocardiography (echo) is a widely used imaging modality for assessing cardiac function, with Left Ventricular Filling Pressure (LVFP) serving as a critical physiological marker for conditions such as heart failure. Standard LVFP classification into normal \emph{vs} elevated categories relies on the Doppler-derived $E/e'$ ratio, which is operator-dependent and often unavailable in resource-limited settings, motivating methods that infer LVFP directly from B-mode echo. Existing deep learning approaches achieve high performance but remain largely black-box, limiting clinical interpretability. We propose HypOProto, a hyperbolic, ordinal prototype-based framework for interpretable LVFP classification using a frozen, explainable foundation model backbone. HypOProto arranges prototypes along the physiological $E/e'$ scale, placing borderline cases near the hyperboloid root where small angular differences separate similar cases, while normal and elevated cases occupy outward positions reflecting increasing diagnostic certainty. This hyperbolic geometry encodes clinically meaningful ordinal relationships and improves interpretability. We also introduce a novel Hyperbolic Prototype Angular Separation (HyperPAS) loss, enforcing inter-class prototype separation in hyperbolic space. HypOProto achieves SOTA performance while maintaining transparency, and highlights clinically relevant regions in visualizations. This work represents the first prototype-based framework for LVFP classification in echo. Our code can be found at https://github.com/DeepRCL/HypOProto.

AI.tldr: HypOProto is a hyperbolic, ordinal prototype-based framework for interpretable Left Ventricular Filling Pressure (LVFP) classification from B-mode echocardiography, using an explainable foundation model backbone. It arranges prototypes along the E/e' scale in hyperbolic space, achieving state-of-the-art performance while maintaining clinical interpretability.
AI.motivation: LVFP classification via standard Doppler-derived E/e' ratio is operator-dependent and often unavailable in resource-limited settings. Existing deep learning methods for direct LVFP inference from B-mode echo are black-box, limiting clinical interpretability and trust.
AI.method: HypOProto uses a frozen explainable foundation model backbone with hyperbolic, ordinal prototype-based learning. Prototypes are arranged along the physiological E/e' scale in hyperbolic space, with borderline cases near the hyperboloid root and normal/elevated cases outward. A novel Hyperbolic Prototype Angular Separation (HyperPAS) loss enforces inter-class prototype separation.
AI.result: HypOProto achieves state-of-the-art performance on LVFP classification while maintaining transparency. It highlights clinically relevant regions in visualizations, providing interpretable predictions.
AI.conclusion: This is the first prototype-based framework for LVFP classification in echo, demonstrating that hyperbolic ordinal prototype learning can effectively balance performance and interpretability for clinical decision support.

title: Exit-and-Join Dynamics for Decentralized Coalition Formation
summary: This paper studies coalition formation as a decentralized dynamical process driven by unilateral exit-and-join decisions. Agents evaluate local moves using the Aumann-Dreze value, so payoffs are computed within the agent's current coalition rather than through a globally negotiated coalition structure. The resulting model links cooperative payoff allocation with noncooperative best-response behavior: a terminal partition is precisely a coalition structure with no admissible, individually profitable exit-and-join deviation. We establish equilibrium characterizations, identify conditions under which the dynamics admit scalar Lyapunov or exact-potential representations, and analyze how switching and acceptance costs shape local stability. Numerical experiments test finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and a special convex-game benchmark.

AI.tldr: This paper studies coalition formation as a decentralized dynamical process where agents use unilateral exit-and-join decisions based on the Aumann-Dreze value, establishing equilibrium characterizations, Lyapunov/potential representations, and the effects of switching/acceptance costs on stability.
AI.motivation: To model coalition formation as a decentralized, noncooperative dynamical process driven by local payoff evaluations (Aumann-Dreze value), rather than relying on globally negotiated coalition structures, bridging cooperative payoff allocation with noncooperative best-response dynamics.
AI.method: The paper models coalition formation as a dynamical process where agents make unilateral exit-and-join decisions. Payoffs are computed within the agent's current coalition using the Aumann-Dreze value. Terminal partitions are defined as coalition structures with no admissible, individually profitable deviations. The analysis includes equilibrium characterizations, conditions for scalar Lyapunov or exact-potential representations, and the impact of switching and acceptance costs on local stability. Numerical experiments test finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and a convex-game benchmark.
AI.result: Equilibrium characterizations are established; conditions for scalar Lyapunov and exact-potential representations are identified; switching and acceptance costs are shown to shape local stability; numerical experiments demonstrate finite-time stabilization and cost sensitivity.
AI.conclusion: The decentralized exit-and-join dynamics with Aumann-Dreze value provide a tractable framework linking cooperative and noncooperative approaches to coalition formation, with stability properties influenced by adjustment costs.

title: Thermodynamic Signatures of Reasoning: Free-Energy and Spectral-Form-Factor Diagnostics for Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models
summary: Hallucination detection in large language models (LLMs) is deployment-critical, and recent work shows that the spectrum of attention-derived graph Laplacians carries strong signal about reasoning quality. Prior spectral diagnostics, however, summarize the Laplacian spectrum by a handful of eigenvalues or hand-picked scalars, leaving most of its structure unused. We propose Free-Energy Signatures (Fes), a spectral descriptor that treats each layer's attention Laplacian as a Hamiltonian and extracts its thermodynamic potentials partition function, free energy, spectral entropy, heat capacity together with the random-matrix-theory (RMT) spectral form factor. We prove three results: (i)~Lipschitz stability of Fes under attention perturbation; (ii)~an expressiveness result showing that Fes enriches finite spectral summaries and approximates moment-derived spectral functionals under explicit regularity and grid-resolution assumptions; and (iii)~a finite-sample PAC bound on the AUROC of a training-free detector built from Fes. Empirically, across six open-weight LLMs and six benchmarks, a lightweight probe on Fes descriptors achieves the strongest aggregate AUROC among attention-spectral baselines, improving over LapEig by $+6.5$ AUROC points and over GoR-4 by $+2.4$ points on average, while requiring no update to the underlying LLM. In the fully unsupervised setting, an RMT-deviation score achieves mean AUROC $0.71$, providing a label-free but weaker detector. A complementary RMT analysis shows that correct generations exhibit more Wigner-Dyson like spectral statistics, whereas hallucinations exhibit more Poisson-like statistics. The anonymized code and config are provided in the supplementary material.

AI.tldr: Free-Energy Signatures (Fes) is a novel spectral descriptor for hallucination detection in LLMs that treats attention Laplacians as Hamiltonians to extract thermodynamic potentials and RMT-based statistics, achieving state-of-the-art AUROC across multiple benchmarks without modifying the underlying model.
AI.motivation: Prior spectral diagnostics for hallucination detection only use a handful of eigenvalues or hand-picked scalars from the attention graph Laplacian spectrum, leaving most of its structure unused, limiting detection accuracy.
AI.method: Fes extracts thermodynamic potentials (partition function, free energy, spectral entropy, heat capacity) and the RMT spectral form factor from each layer's attention Laplacian treated as a Hamiltonian; supported by Lipschitz stability, expressiveness, and PAC-bound theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: Lightweight probe on Fes achieves best aggregate AUROC among attention-spectral baselines: +6.5 over LapEig, +2.4 over GoR-4 across six LLMs and six benchmarks. Unsupervised RMT-deviation variant achieves 0.71 mean AUROC. Correct generations show Wigner-Dyson statistics; hallucinations show Poisson-like statistics.
AI.conclusion: Fes is a theoretically grounded, training-free spectral descriptor that significantly improves hallucination detection by fully utilizing the Laplacian spectrum's structure, with both supervised and unsupervised variants demonstrating strong performance.

title: JAMER: Project-Level Code Framework Dataset and Benchmark on Professional Game Engines
summary: Current AI-driven game development has made substantial progress in asset generation, gameplay design, and web-based game coding, yet project-level code engineering on professional game engines remains largely unexplored due to the absence of large-scale datasets and deterministic evaluation methods. We present JamSet and JamBench, the first project-level game code framework dataset and benchmark built on a professional game engine. Our key insight is that Game Jam competitions, community events where developers build complete games under tight time constraints, yield thousands of open-source projects suitable for this purpose. Building on the Godot engine's text-based format and headless execution mode, we design a deterministic verification pipeline from file integrity to runtime behavior collection, distilling 8,133 verified projects from over 240,000 repositories. Of these, 300 manually verified projects form JamBench; the rest constitute JamSet. JamBench defines theme-driven generation and code completion tasks, evaluated through a pipeline combining compilation pass rates, Structural Completeness Score (SCS), and Behavioral Alignment Score (BAS). Evaluation of 9 frontier models reveals a capability cliff as project scale increases, with runtime pass rates dropping from 80.4% on small projects to 5.7% on large ones (Task2a). Code Agents improve compilation rates yet yield no gains in runtime behavioral quality, indicating that the bottleneck lies in architectural design rather than syntactic correctness. Experiments validate JamSet as effective training data. All data and code are publicly available.

AI.tldr: JamSet and JamBench are the first project-level game code dataset and benchmark built on the Godot engine, containing 8,133 verified projects from Game Jams. Evaluation of 9 frontier models reveals a capability cliff as project scale increases (runtime pass rates drop from 80.4% to 5.7%), and Code Agents improve compilation but not runtime behavior, showing the bottleneck is architectural design rather than syntax.
AI.motivation: Current AI-driven game development lacks project-level code engineering on professional game engines due to the absence of large-scale datasets and deterministic evaluation methods.
AI.method: Leveraging Game Jam competitions and Godot engine's text-based format and headless execution mode, the authors built a deterministic verification pipeline from file integrity to runtime behavior collection, distilling 8,133 verified projects from over 240,000 repositories. 300 manually verified projects form JamBench; the rest constitute JamSet.
AI.result: Evaluation of 9 frontier models reveals a capability cliff as project scale increases (runtime pass rates drop from 80.4% on small projects to 5.7% on large ones). Code Agents improve compilation rates but yield no gains in runtime behavioral quality, indicating the bottleneck lies in architectural design rather than syntactic correctness.
AI.conclusion: JamSet and JamBench provide the first project-level game code benchmark on a professional engine, revealing that current models struggle with architectural design at scale, and JamSet is validated as effective training data.

title: ParaScale: Scale-Calibrated Camera-Motion Transfer via a Gauge-Invariant Parallax Number
summary: Transferring the camera motion of a reference video to a freshly generated one lets creators reuse cinematic moves. Yet reference and target often live at incompatible scales -- a sweep across a galaxy versus a nudge across a desk -- and naively reusing the recovered trajectory yields either imperceptible or violently exaggerated motion. We trace this to a geometric fact: translation-induced image motion scales as ||T||/Z, so a monocular trajectory is meaningful only up to a depth-scale gauge. We distill this into the Parallax Number Pi = ||Delta T|| / Zbar, a dimensionless, gauge-invariant descriptor of how strongly a camera move is felt, and prove that it -- not the raw trajectory -- is the quantity that scale-faithful transfer must preserve. ParaScale is a plug-and-play module that reads Pi off any reference video and re-realizes it against the target scene's own depth, per frame, leaving rotation untouched. Sitting between pose extraction and pose injection, it requires no retraining and drops into any pose-conditioned generator. We further introduce the Parallax Consistency Error (PCE), a scale-symmetric metric that -- unlike the similarity-aligned TransErr -- exposes scene-scale mismatch. Across scale regimes spanning four orders of magnitude and multiple backbones, ParaScale keeps the realized parallax on the identity line and cuts PCE by more than 3x over uncalibrated transfer with no loss of visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: ParaScale introduces the Parallax Number Π = ||ΔT||/Z̄, a scale-invariant descriptor of camera motion, and uses it to enable scale-faithful transfer of camera trajectories across scenes with vastly different depths (e.g., galaxy vs. desk). It is a plug-and-play module that preserves parallax rather than raw trajectories, reducing Parallax Consistency Error by 3x without retraining or fidelity loss.
AI.motivation: Camera motion transfer across scenes with incompatible scales (e.g., galaxy vs. desk) fails because raw trajectories are scale-ambiguous: translation-induced image motion scales as ||T||/Z, making recovered trajectories meaningful only up to a depth-scale gauge. Naively reusing recovered trajectories yields imperceptible or violently exaggerated motion.
AI.method: Proposes the Parallax Number Π = ||ΔT||/Z̄, a dimensionless gauge-invariant descriptor quantifying how strongly a camera move is felt. ParaScale reads Π from a reference video and re-realizes it against the target scene's own per-frame depth, leaving rotation untouched. It sits between pose extraction and pose injection as a plug-and-play module requiring no retraining.
AI.result: Across scale regimes spanning four orders of magnitude and multiple backbones, ParaScale keeps realized parallax on the identity line and cuts Parallax Consistency Error (PCE) by more than 3x over uncalibrated transfer with no loss of visual fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The Parallax Number Π is the quantity that scale-faithful camera motion transfer must preserve, not the raw trajectory. ParaScale effectively enables scale-invariant motion transfer as a drop-in module for any pose-conditioned generator.

title: Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents
summary: Agent benchmarks are growing fast, but no single benchmark touches more than four or five of the dimensions that deployment exposes. This paper aggregates the largest coordinated deep-dive of one MCP-based industrial-agent benchmark to date: fourteen parallel implementation studies covering new asset classes (including a multi-modal visual extension), alternative orchestrations, retrieval strategies, reasoning modes, infrastructure optimizations, and evaluation-methodology probes. Consolidating those studies with seven prior agent benchmarks, we argue that aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify deployed-agent evaluation. Rankings derived from aggregate scores do not transfer to out-of-distribution settings; recent public-to-hidden competition retrospectives provide direct empirical evidence of this rank instability. We propose ranking configurations by predictive validity, the correlation between in-sample and out-of-sample rank, rather than in-sample mean, and report a twelve-tier measurement apparatus that exposes the deployment-relevant dimensions HELM and its agent-era successors collapse. The position is operationalized through three falsifiable out-of-distribution criteria with explicit thresholds; existing evidence partly supports it but is too thin to confirm. We close with a pre-registered pilot design and a field-level vision for what the next generation of agentic benchmarks should report.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that aggregate-score leaderboards in agent benchmarks systematically fail to predict real-world deployment performance, proposing a shift to predictive validity ranking and a twelve-tier measurement framework, supported by fourteen parallel implementation studies and analysis of seven prior benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current agent benchmarks are growing but each covers only a few deployment-relevant dimensions, and aggregate-score leaderboards produce rank instability where rankings do not transfer to out-of-distribution settings.
AI.method: Conducted fourteen parallel implementation studies covering multi-modal visual extensions, alternative orchestrations, retrieval strategies, reasoning modes, infrastructure optimizations, and evaluation-methodology probes; consolidated with seven prior agent benchmarks; proposed ranking by predictive validity (in-sample vs. out-of-sample rank correlation) instead of in-sample mean; designed a twelve-tier measurement apparatus exposing deployment-relevant dimensions.
AI.result: Rankings from aggregate scores do not transfer to out-of-distribution settings; evidence from public-to-hidden competition retrospectives shows rank instability; existing evidence partly supports the position but is too thin to confirm.
AI.conclusion: Proposes three falsifiable out-of-distribution criteria with explicit thresholds, a pre-registered pilot design, and a vision for the next generation of agentic benchmarks that report deployment-relevant dimensions rather than aggregate leaderboard scores.

title: FlexLAM: Resolving the Bottleneck Trade-off in Latent Action Learning
summary: Latent actions provide a compact interface between action-free video and downstream decision-making, yet existing Latent Action Models (LAMs) force every transition through a fixed-capacity bottleneck. We identify a bottleneck trade-off: overly tight codes can discard transition cues needed for action alignment, while overly loose codes preserve additional transition variation that must be resolved when alignment labels are scarce or narrowly distributed. FlexLAM replaces this fixed capacity with variable-length latent actions trained by nested dropout, yielding prefix-valid codes that capture compact transition structure first and add detail only when needed, without new architectures or losses. A single FlexLAM matches or surpasses separately trained fixed-capacity LAMs at every evaluated token budget under standard scarce-label supervision and under a low-return single-task alignment stress test, indicating that FlexLAM is not merely adjustable at inference time but learns a better latent-action interface at the same token budgets. The same model supports inference-time token-budget adjustment without retraining, and FlexLAM improves Ego4D transition reconstruction. These results suggest that variable-length latent actions are an architecture-free, drop-in upgrade to the fixed-capacity bottleneck in latent action models, latent-action world models, and video-pretrained action interfaces.

AI.tldr: FlexLAM replaces fixed-capacity latent action bottlenecks with variable-length codes via nested dropout, improving transition alignment and reconstruction without architectural changes.
AI.motivation: Existing Latent Action Models (LAMs) suffer from a bottleneck trade-off: tight codes discard transition cues, loose codes preserve variation that hinders alignment with scarce labels.
AI.method: FlexLAM introduces variable-length latent actions trained with nested dropout, producing prefix-valid codes that capture compact structure first and add detail when needed, without new architectures or losses.
AI.result: Single FlexLAM matches or surpasses separately trained fixed-capacity LAMs at every token budget under scarce-label supervision and low-return single-task alignment stress test; improves Ego4D transition reconstruction; supports inference-time token-budget adjustment.
AI.conclusion: Variable-length latent actions are an architecture-free, drop-in upgrade to fixed-capacity bottlenecks in latent action models, world models, and video-pretrained action interfaces.

title: Repository-Level Solidity Code Generation with Large Language Models: From Prompting to Fine-Tuning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown strong capabilities in general-purpose code generation, but their effectiveness in specialized software domains remains underexplored. Solidity smart contracts represent a high-stakes domain where generated code must satisfy strict language-level, security, and software-engineering constraints. Existing benchmarks and metrics remain insufficient for repository-level Solidity generation, where models must synthesize complete contracts from natural language requirements. To address this gap, we introduce SolidityBench, a benchmark of 5,470 repository-level Solidity smart contracts paired with natural language descriptions. We also propose SolidityScore, a Solidity-aware semantic metric that emphasizes domain-critical constructs such as security modifiers, contract declarations, and Solidity-specific keywords. Using this benchmark, we evaluate representative code LLMs, including Qwen2.5-Coder, DeepSeek-Coder, and CodeLlama, across zero-shot prompting, Chain-of-Thought reasoning, in-context learning, retrieval-augmented generation, and supervised fine-tuning. The results show that general-purpose models exhibit systematic structural deficiencies in repository-level Solidity generation. Among non-parametric methods, retrieval-augmented generation performs best, while in-context learning degrades beyond two examples due to context saturation. Supervised fine-tuning achieves the largest improvement by internalizing Solidity-specific constraints into model parameters. Overall, our study provides a comprehensive benchmark for repository-level Solidity code generation and shows that high-quality domain data combined with supervised fine-tuning is the most effective strategy for improving the reliability of LLM-generated smart contracts.

AI.tldr: SoliddityBench introduces a benchmark of 5,470 repository-level Solidity smart contracts with natural language descriptions and SolidityScore, a semantic metric emphasizing domain-critical constructs. Evaluation shows general-purpose LLMs have structural deficiencies in Solidity generation; retrieval-augmented generation performs best among non-parametric methods, while supervised fine-tuning yields the largest improvements.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks and metrics are insufficient for repository-level Solidity code generation, where models must synthesize complete contracts from natural language requirements while satisfying strict language-level, security, and software-engineering constraints.
AI.method: Constructed SolidityBench (5,470 repository-level contracts with NL descriptions) and SolidityScore (a Solidity-aware semantic metric). Evaluated Qwen2.5-Coder, DeepSeek-Coder, CodeLlama across zero-shot, Chain-of-Thought, in-context learning, retrieval-augmented generation, and supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: General-purpose models show systematic structural deficiencies. RAG performs best among non-parametric methods; ICL degrades beyond two examples. Supervised fine-tuning achieves the largest improvement by internalizing Solidity-specific constraints.
AI.conclusion: High-quality domain data combined with supervised fine-tuning is the most effective strategy for improving reliability of LLM-generated smart contracts.

title: Training-Free Metrics for Synthetic Object Detection Data: A Proxy for Detector Performance
summary: With the recent advent of image generative models, synthetic data are increasingly being used to supplement limited real datasets for training computer vision models. However, not all synthetic datasets improve performance equally, and their effectiveness can only be assessed by training a downstream model, which is computationally expensive and time-consuming. This problem is pronounced in the task of object detection, where the required annotations are much more dense due to bounding boxes. In this paper, we propose a pre-computable metric family, dubbed Conditional-Composition Domain Match (CCDM), which serves as a proxy for the relative utility of candidate synthetic training sets for downstream detection. Experiments on the VisDrone-DET dataset show that the CCDM metric families achieve a Spearman correlation of 1.0 with the downstream performance of YOLOv8, clearly outperforming existing metrics for synthetic image evaluation.

AI.tldr: CCDM is a pre-computable metric family that predicts the utility of synthetic training data for object detection without needing to train downstream models, achieving perfect correlation (1.0 Spearman) with YOLOv8 performance on VisDrone-DET.
AI.motivation: Assessing synthetic dataset quality for object detection currently requires training downstream models, which is computationally expensive and time-consuming. A lightweight pre-computable metric is needed to quickly evaluate and select effective synthetic training data.
AI.method: Proposes Conditional-Composition Domain Match (CCDM), a pre-computable metric family that evaluates domain alignment between synthetic and real data at the conditional composition level, serving as a proxy for downstream detection performance without requiring model training.
AI.result: CCDM achieves a Spearman correlation of 1.0 with downstream YOLOv8 performance on the VisDrone-DET dataset, outperforming existing synthetic image evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: CCDM provides an efficient and highly predictive proxy metric for evaluating synthetic training data utility in object detection, eliminating the need for costly downstream model training during dataset selection.

title: GLARE: A Natural Language Interface for Querying Global Explanations
summary: While global explanations are crucial for understanding vision models across datasets, classes, and decision contexts, their complex and monolithic nature often hinders practical exploration. Because users typically seek targeted answers to specific questions rather than static artifacts, we present an LLM-based interactive interface that provides natural language access to global explanations for black-box image classifiers. The system's core LLM acts as a mediator, translating natural language questions into structured SQL queries over local explanation data. This enables flexible aggregation without exposing users to low-level representations. For each query, the interface outputs statistics-augmented natural language responses, supporting local explanations, and intent-aligned visualizations. We evaluate the system on intent interpretation, query mapping accuracy, generalization to novel queries and datasets, and robustness to linguistic errors. Our results demonstrate that LLM-mediated querying substantially improves the accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered XAI.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an LLM-based interactive interface that translates natural language questions into SQL queries over local explanation data, enabling flexible and accessible global explanations for black-box image classifiers without exposing users to low-level representations.
AI.motivation: Global explanations for vision models are often complex and monolithic, making practical exploration difficult. Users typically seek targeted answers to specific questions rather than static artifacts, motivating a more interactive and accessible explanation system.
AI.method: A core LLM acts as a mediator, translating natural language questions into structured SQL queries over local explanation data. The system outputs statistics-augmented natural language responses, supports local explanations, and provides intent-aligned visualizations.
AI.result: The system demonstrates strong performance on intent interpretation, query mapping accuracy, generalization to novel queries and datasets, and robustness to linguistic errors.
AI.conclusion: LLM-mediated querying substantially improves the accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered explainable AI (XAI).

title: Spectral DPPs via NEPv: A Scalable Continuous Relaxation of Determinantal MAP for Diversity-Aware Data Selection
summary: Selecting a small, diverse, high-quality subset from a massive pool of candidates is a recurring primitive in modern machine learning -- data curation and coreset selection for training and fine-tuning large models, active-learning batch acquisition, prompt and exemplar selection for in-context learning, retrieval diversification, and experimental design. Determinantal Point Processes (\DPP s) give a principled, well-calibrated notion of diversity for this task, but their \emph{MAP} objective -- pick a size-$k$ subset $S$ maximizing $\logdet(L_S)$ -- is NP-hard, and the standard greedy and sampling algorithms scale superlinearly in the ground-set size $n$. This cost is prohibitive precisely in the data-centric regime where diversity matters most, where $n$ ranges over millions to billions of candidate examples, features, or embeddings. We recast \DPP-MAP as a continuous optimization problem over the Stiefel manifold, and show that its first-order optimality conditions form a \emph{Nonlinear Eigenvalue Problem with eigenvector dependency} (\NEPv) of a previously unstudied form. This \NEPv\ admits a self-consistent field (\SCF) iteration with a spectral-gap-based local contraction guarantee, giving a principled iterative solver where the diversity objective drives an eigenvector-dependent operator. The resulting algorithm, \OurMethod, requires only matrix-vector products with the kernel and runs in time $O\!\big((ndk+nk^2)\,t\big)$ for a small number of iterations $t$, scaling near-linearly in $n$ and integrating directly with low-rank and feature-map kernels common in ML. This paper focuses on the relaxation, solver, and scaling analysis; full real-data benchmarking is left to a planned empirical study.

AI.tldr: The paper formulates DPP-MAP (selecting a diverse, high-quality subset) as a continuous optimization over the Stiefel manifold, leading to a Nonlinear Eigenvalue Problem (NEPv). They propose an SCF iteration solver (OurMethod) with near-linear time complexity O((ndk+nk^2)t), avoiding the prohibitive superlinear scaling of standard DPP algorithms.
AI.motivation: DPP-MAP is NP-hard and standard greedy/sampling algorithms scale superlinearly in the ground-set size n, which is prohibitive for massive-scale data-centric applications (millions to billions of candidates) where diversity matters most.
AI.method: The authors recast DPP-MAP as continuous optimization over the Stiefel manifold, derive first-order optimality conditions leading to a novel form of Nonlinear Eigenvalue Problem with eigenvector dependency (NEPv), and propose a self-consistent field (SCF) iteration with spectral-gap-based local contraction guarantee.
AI.result: The resulting algorithm, OurMethod, runs in O((ndk+nk^2)t) time requiring only matrix-vector products, achieving near-linear scaling in n. The SCF iteration enjoys a spectral-gap-based local contraction guarantee.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a principled, scalable relaxation of DPP-MAP with theoretical guarantees, enabling diversity-driven subset selection at unprecedented scale. Full empirical benchmarking is deferred to future work.

title: Beyond Static Endpoints: Tool Programs as an Interface for Flexible Agentic Web Services
summary: In the agentic web era, LLM-based agents increasingly invoke web services as tools, yet most interfaces remain \emph{static endpoints} that poorly express long-horizon workflows with loops, conditionals, joins, and retries. We present ToolPro, which represents an agent's tool intent as an \emph{executable tool program} that compactly encodes multi-step service interactions with explicit effect types. ToolPro combines constraint-guided program construction, effect-aware replay for exactly-once state-modifying calls, and a profile-driven policy that decides when program execution outperforms stepwise calling. We instantiate ToolPro over MCP-style services with WebAssembly sandboxing and evaluate it on diverse workflows of real-world applications. ToolPro reduces end-to-end latency by up to 53.4\% and client-side traffic by up to 96.1\%, with larger gains under higher network latency and workflow complexity.

AI.tldr: ToolPro represents LLM-based agent tool intents as executable programs encoding multi-step workflows with loops, conditionals, joins, and retries, reducing latency by up to 53.4% and traffic by up to 96.1% compared to stepwise calling.
AI.motivation: Current web service interfaces are static endpoints that poorly express long-horizon workflows involving loops, conditionals, joins, and retries, leading to inefficiency when LLM-based agents invoke them stepwise.
AI.method: ToolPro represents tool intent as an executable tool program encoding multi-step service interactions with explicit effect types, combining constraint-guided program construction, effect-aware replay for exactly-once state-modifying calls, and a profile-driven policy to decide when program execution outperforms stepwise calling. It is instantiated over MCP-style services with WebAssembly sandboxing.
AI.result: ToolPro reduces end-to-end latency by up to 53.4% and client-side traffic by up to 96.1%, with larger gains under higher network latency and workflow complexity.
AI.conclusion: Representing agent tool intents as executable programs with explicit effect types and profile-driven execution policies significantly improves efficiency over stepwise calling, especially under high network latency and complex workflows.

title: CSWinUNETR: Segmentation of Thin Anatomical Structures in Medical Images
summary: Accurate segmentation of thin, tortuous anatomical structures, such as retinal vessels, cerebral vasculature, and facial wrinkles, remains challenging due to low contrast, frequent discontinuities, and severe class imbalance. Although recent convolutional and Transformer-based models have improved performance, they often yield fragmented predictions and fail to recover fine branches. We propose CSWinUNETR, a general-purpose backbone for 2D and 3D thin-structure segmentation. It employs cross-shaped stripe self-attention to model long-range principal-axis context and incorporates cyclic shifts to enhance information exchange across stripes. To better preserve fine-grained details, we further introduce a detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention module that aggregates contextual features from multi-resolution representations. In addition, we propose sparse-control dynamic snake convolution, which reconstructs reliable dense curvilinear kernels from sparsely predicted control points to better follow tortuous geometry. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks across ophthalmology, neurovascular imaging, and dermatology demonstrate that CSWinUNETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods without task-specific post-processing or topology-aware losses. The code is available at https://github.com/labhai/CSWinUNETR.

AI.tldr: CSWinUNETR is a general-purpose backbone for segmenting thin, tortuous anatomical structures (e.g., retinal vessels, cerebral vasculature, facial wrinkles) using cross-shaped stripe self-attention with cyclic shifts, a detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention module, and sparse-control dynamic snake convolution, outperforming SOTA on four benchmarks without task-specific post-processing.
AI.motivation: Accurate segmentation of thin, tortuous anatomical structures remains challenging due to low contrast, frequent discontinuities, and severe class imbalance; existing convolutional and Transformer-based models often yield fragmented predictions and fail to recover fine branches.
AI.method: CSWinUNETR uses (1) cross-shaped stripe self-attention with cyclic shifts for long-range principal-axis context, (2) a detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention module aggregating multi-resolution features, and (3) sparse-control dynamic snake convolution that reconstructs dense curvilinear kernels from sparsely predicted control points to follow tortuous geometry.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on four benchmarks across ophthalmology, neurovascular imaging, and dermatology without task-specific post-processing or topology-aware losses.
AI.conclusion: CSWinUNETR provides a general-purpose, effective backbone for 2D and 3D thin-structure segmentation, achieving superior performance across diverse anatomical domains.

title: Interpreting Neural Combinatorial Optimization via Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks
summary: Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) achieves strong performance, yet its black-box nature remains a key roadblock to deployment and scientific diagnosis. Standard interpretability tools, such as Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs), are ill-equipped for NCO, whose decisions are dynamic, state-dependent, and lack proper concept vocabulary definition. To close this gap, we introduce Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks (EPB), to our knowledge, the first framework for interpreting NCO policies by distilling black-box NCO models into human-readable program portfolios. EPB employs an LLM to autonomously evolve a bank of programs, where each program's per-step action distribution serves as the bottleneck. EPB works through an iterative framework: Block I fixes program bank capacity and introduces a hybrid textual-numerical gradient descent scheme that couples numerical gradients for student router updates and textual gradients for LLM-based program revision; Block II dynamically adapts bank capacity via fault-targeted expansion and redundancy pruning. Extensive experiments demonstrate EPB's effectiveness and broad applicability, where the distilled program portfolios largely match original performance. EPB also reveals that NCO behavior shifts across optimization stages and can be approximated as a composition of classic heuristic variants. Our work advances interpretable NCO and establishes EPB as a promising tool for interpreting sequential decision-making models.

AI.tldr: EPB is a framework that interprets black-box Neural Combinatorial Optimization policies by distilling them into human-readable program portfolios using LLM-driven evolution, hybrid gradient descent, and dynamic capacity adaptation.
AI.motivation: NCO models are black-box and lack interpretability; standard tools like CBMs are unsuitable because NCO decisions are dynamic, state-dependent, and lack defined concept vocabularies.
AI.method: EPB uses an LLM to evolve a program bank with per-step action distributions as bottlenecks. Block I fixes bank capacity and uses hybrid textual-numerical gradient descent (numerical gradients for router, textual gradients for LLM-based revision). Block II dynamically adapts capacity via fault-targeted expansion and redundancy pruning.
AI.result: Distilled program portfolios largely match original NCO performance across extensive experiments. EPB reveals NCO behavior shifts across optimization stages and can be approximated as a composition of classic heuristic variants.
AI.conclusion: EPB advances interpretable NCO and serves as a promising tool for interpreting sequential decision-making models.

title: Spectral Retrieval-Augmented Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Time series forecasting leverages historical patterns to predict future values, but traditional methods face challenges when dealing with complex, non-stationary patterns that are difficult to memorize during training. Retrieval-augmented approaches have emerged as promising solutions by retrieving similar historical patterns to enhance predictions. However, existing retrieval methods suffer from two fundamental limitations: spectral blindness, which overlooks critical frequency-domain characteristics that capture underlying periodic structures, and temporal recency, which treats all historical data equally without emphasizing recent, more relevant patterns. In this paper, we propose SpecReTF, a novel retrieval method that addresses these issues by converting time series into windowed frequency representations, measuring similarity with a combined metric that captures both amplitude and phase information. To balance recency and historical context, we apply an exponential moving average weighting scheme that emphasizes recent windows. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that SpecReTF outperforms time-domain retrieval methods, achieving superior forecasting accuracy across diverse, non-stationary time series.

AI.tldr: SpecReTF is a novel retrieval-augmented time series forecasting method that uses windowed frequency representations and exponential moving average weighting to overcome spectral blindness and temporal recency limitations in existing retrieval approaches.
AI.motivation: Traditional time series forecasting struggles with complex, non-stationary patterns. Existing retrieval-augmented methods suffer from spectral blindness (ignoring frequency-domain periodic structures) and temporal recency (treating all historical data equally without emphasizing recent patterns).
AI.method: SpecReTF converts time series into windowed frequency representations, measures similarity using a combined metric capturing both amplitude and phase information, and applies an exponential moving average weighting scheme to balance recency and historical context.
AI.result: SpecReTF outperforms time-domain retrieval methods, achieving superior forecasting accuracy across diverse, non-stationary time series benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: Addressing spectral blindness and temporal recency via frequency-domain retrieval with recency-aware weighting significantly improves forecasting performance for non-stationary time series.

title: When Lower Privileges Suffice: Investigating Over-Privileged Tool Selection in LLM Agents
summary: As LLM agents increasingly select tools autonomously, their choices among tools with different privileges become safety-relevant. However, prior tool-selection studies focus on safety-agnostic metadata preferences, leaving privilege-sensitive choices underexplored. To address this gap, we study over-privileged tool selection, in which an agent selects or escalates to a higher-privilege tool despite a sufficient lower-privilege alternative. We introduce ToolPrivBench to evaluate whether agents choose higher-privilege tools despite sufficient lower-privilege alternatives, measuring both initial selection and escalation after transient tool failures. Across eight domains and five recurring risk patterns, we find that over-privileged tool selection is common among mainstream LLM agents and is further amplified by transient failures. We further find that general safety alignment does not reliably transfer to least-privilege tool choice, while prompt-level controls provide only limited mitigation under transient failures. We therefore introduce a privilege-aware post-training defense that teaches agents to prefer sufficient lower-privilege tools and escalate only when necessary. Our mitigation experiments show that this defense substantially reduces unnecessary high-privilege tool use while preserving general capabilities.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ToolPrivBench to study over-privileged tool selection in LLM agents, finding that agents commonly choose higher-privilege tools despite sufficient lower-privilege alternatives—especially under transient failures—and proposes a privilege-aware post-training defense that significantly reduces unnecessary high-privilege tool use.
AI.motivation: Prior tool-selection studies focus on safety-agnostic metadata preferences, leaving the safety-critical issue of privilege-sensitive tool choices (where agents select over-privileged tools despite sufficient lower-privilege alternatives) underexplored.
AI.method: The authors introduce ToolPrivBench, a benchmark evaluating over-privileged tool selection across eight domains and five recurring risk patterns, measuring both initial selection and escalation after transient tool failures. They then develop a privilege-aware post-training defense that teaches agents to prefer sufficient lower-privilege tools and escalate only when necessary.
AI.result: Over-privileged tool selection is common among mainstream LLM agents and is amplified by transient failures. General safety alignment does not reliably transfer to least-privilege tool choice; prompt-level controls provide limited mitigation under transient failures.
AI.conclusion: The proposed privilege-aware post-training defense substantially reduces unnecessary high-privilege tool use while preserving general capabilities.

title: 3D-PLOT-LLM: Part-Level Object Tokens for 3D Large Language Models
summary: 3D multimodal large language models (3D MLLMs) describe a 3D object as a whole but cannot address, name, or reason about its parts. Prior part-aware attempts add segmentation decoders, heavier 3D encoders, or bounding-box grammars at substantial parameter cost. We take a fundamentally different path: we reorganize the input token stream so that parts become directly addressable through the LLM's own vocabulary. Our model, 3D-PLOT-LLM, partitions the frozen point encoder's patches into K locally coherent regions and inserts, before each region's patch tokens, a learnable per-region marker and a reserved vocabulary token <part_k>; a Marker-Space Refinement (MSR) module then conditions each marker on its region's spatial statistics and adjacency neighbors. The model thus cites parts in its output and follows prompts that refer to parts by token, a capability absent from prior object-level 3D MLLMs. To probe this interface, we construct PartVerse-QA, a vocabulary-level part-QA benchmark adapted from PartVerse mesh annotations (77K training pairs and 588 held-out queries on disjoint object splits), on which 3D-PLOT-LLM reaches caption-to-slots Jaccard 0.459 and Exact-match 13.78%, with a slot-to-caption GPT-4o judge of 44.68. On the 3DCoMPaT-GrIn part-aware grounded description benchmark, 3D-PLOT-LLM outperforms PointLLM, Kestrel, PARIS3D, and SegPoint on every text-output metric, and ShapeLLM on 3 of 4, with up to +3.03 GPT-4o judge over PointLLM. On Objaverse whole-object captioning, adding PartVerse-QA at Stage 2 yields +0.65 SBERT and +1.85 GPT-4o over PointLLM, and tops PointLLM-PiSA on 4 of 5 traditional metrics (SBERT, SimCSE, BLEU-1, METEOR) despite targeting a different (part-grounded) objective. All with under 1M new trainable parameters on a frozen point encoder, an order of magnitude below prior part-aware 3D MLLMs, and no segmentation decoder or bounding-box head.

AI.tldr: 3D-PLOT-LLM introduces part-level understanding in 3D MLLMs by reorganizing the token stream with learnable markers and reserved vocabulary tokens <part_k>, enabling direct part addressing without segmentation decoders or bounding-box heads. It achieves strong results on PartVerse-QA, 3DCoMPaT-GrIn, and Objaverse benchmarks with under 1M new trainable parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D MLLMs describe 3D objects holistically but cannot identify, name, or reason about individual parts. Prior part-aware methods rely on heavy segmentation decoders, larger 3D encoders, or bounding-box grammars, incurring substantial parameter costs.
AI.method: The model partitions frozen point encoder patches into K locally coherent regions, inserts learnable per-region markers and reserved vocabulary tokens <part_k> before each region's patch tokens, and applies a Marker-Space Refinement (MSR) module that conditions each marker on its region's spatial statistics and adjacency neighbors.
AI.result: On PartVerse-QA: caption-to-slots Jaccard 0.459, Exact-match 13.78%, GPT-4o judge 44.68. On 3DCoMPaT-GrIn: outperforms PointLLM, Kestrel, PARIS3D, SegPoint on all text-output metrics, and ShapeLLM on 3 of 4. On Objaverse: +0.65 SBERT and +1.85 GPT-4o over PointLLM, tops PointLLM-PiSA on 4/5 traditional metrics.
AI.conclusion: 3D-PLOT-LLM achieves part-grounded reasoning in 3D MLLMs with under 1M trainable parameters—an order of magnitude fewer than prior part-aware models—and no segmentation decoders or bounding-box heads, demonstrating that token-stream reorganization is a lightweight and effective alternative.

title: A Comparative Study of Pretrained Transformer Models for Quranic ASR: Speech Representations, Label Formats, and Dataset Composition
comment: 30 pages, 9 figures, 5 tables, Submitted to International Journal of Speech Technology
summary: Quran Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) aims to convert Quranic recitation into text, enabling applications such as aided memorisation tools and Quranic search engines. However, existing ASR models often exhibit high Word Error Rates (WER) on user-recited verses and lack full coverage of the Quranic corpus. This paper presents a systematic empirical study of domain-specific fine-tuning of pretrained Transformer-based models for Quranic ASR, using advanced speech feature extraction methods: Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, and XLS-R. These models apply self-supervised learning by masking portions of input audio and using Transformer architectures to learn context-aware speech features. The pretrained models are fine-tuned on a filtered Quranic dataset exceeding 870 hours of professional and user recitations. Through comprehensive ablation studies across feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations, we identify the key factors that affect transcription accuracy in this domain. Our best-performing configuration achieves a WER of 0.08 on the EveryAyah subset and 0.11 on the combined EveryAyah+Tarteel setting, representing roughly a five-percentage-point gain over the Citrinet baseline (WER = 0.163) while reducing combined-model training time from 140 hours to 40 hours. Arabic text without diacritics yields the best fine-tuning results, and Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 provides the strongest overall representation. Future work includes improving dataset quality and developing phoneme-aware models to extract deeper speech feature representations for Tajweed-sensitive applications.

AI.tldr: This paper fine-tunes pretrained Transformer-based ASR models (Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, XLS-R) on a 870+ hour Quranic recitation dataset, achieving WER of 0.08 on EveryAyah and 0.11 on combined setting, a ~5-percentage-point gain over Citrinet baseline (WER=0.163), while reducing training time from 140 to 40 hours.
AI.motivation: Existing Quranic ASR models suffer from high Word Error Rates on user-recited verses and lack full coverage of the Quranic corpus, motivating the need for domain-specific fine-tuning of advanced pretrained models to improve transcription accuracy.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic empirical study by fine-tuning three pretrained Transformer-based models (Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, XLS-R) on a filtered Quranic dataset of 870+ hours, performing comprehensive ablation studies on feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations.
AI.result: The best configuration achieves WER of 0.08 (EveryAyah) and 0.11 (combined EveryAyah+Tarteel), roughly a 5-percentage-point improvement over the Citrinet baseline (WER=0.163), with training time reduced from 140 to 40 hours. Arabic text without diacritics works best, and Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 provides the strongest representation.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific fine-tuning of pretrained Transformer-based ASR models significantly improves Quranic transcription accuracy while reducing training time. Future work includes improving dataset quality and developing phoneme-aware models for Tajweed-sensitive applications.

title: Does Text Actually Help? Uncovering and Resolving Text Collapse in Multimodal Time Series Forecasting
summary: Multimodal time series forecasting, which pairs numerical sequences with domain-relevant textual reports, promises to inject world knowledge into forecasting pipelines. However, we uncover a critical failure mode in existing frameworks that we term text collapse: the text branch converges to a content-independent transformation, contributing negligible discriminative signal regardless of the input description. We argue that text collapse is a consequence of a fundamental asymmetry in time series forecasting: the numerical input is strongly autocorrelated with the output, making the numerical backbone inherently dominant, while the text branch, despite carrying complementary and often critical information, is insufficiently utilized, leading to its systematic underexploitation. To address this, we propose \textbf{REST-TS} (\textbf{R}esidual-\textbf{E}xclusive \textbf{S}upervision for \textbf{T}ext in \textbf{T}ime \textbf{S}eries), which turns the asymmetry into a design principle: the numerical backbone produces its own independent numerical forecast, and the text branch is exclusively supervised to predict the structured components of the residual, the prediction gap that numbers cannot explain. Because no numerical pathway can reduce these losses, the text branch must extract genuine content from the input description. Evaluated across diverse real-world domains and backbone architectures, REST-TS achieves state-of-the-art performance and consistently demonstrates greater text-branch utilization than existing frameworks, providing strong empirical evidence that supervising the text branch on the residual compels it to extract genuine content from the input.

AI.tldr: REST-TS solves text collapse in multimodal time series forecasting by supervising the text branch to predict residuals that numerical models cannot explain, forcing genuine content extraction and achieving SOTA performance.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal time series forecasting frameworks suffer from "text collapse," where the text branch degrades into a content-independent transformation and contributes negligible discriminative signal due to the inherent asymmetry between autocorrelated numerical inputs and underutilized textual inputs.
AI.method: REST-TS (Residual-Exclusive Supervision for Text in Time Series) turns the numerical-textual asymmetry into a design principle: the numerical backbone produces its own independent forecast, and the text branch is exclusively supervised to predict structured components of the residual (the gap numerical models cannot explain).
AI.result: REST-TS achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse real-world domains and backbone architectures, with consistently greater text-branch utilization than existing frameworks.
AI.conclusion: Supervising the text branch on the residual compels it to extract genuine content from the input description, providing strong empirical evidence for the effectiveness of this design principle.

title: The Correctness Illusion in LLM-Generated GPU Kernels
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, LNCS format. Companion papers to follow on arXiv next week; IDs will be added in a v2 replace
summary: Benchmarks for LLM-generated GPU kernels (KernelBench, TritonBench, GEAK) score correctness through fixed-shape, small-sample allclose-style checks. The number of inputs varies between benchmarks. The shape, dtype, and tolerance are fixed for each kernel. We test that oracle empirically. We construct a controlled corpus of 24 Triton and CPU stand-in kernels (15 correct controls and 9 LLM-style buggy variants seeded with documented transcription errors) and re-evaluate it under op-schema-aware seeded fuzzing with a high-precision (fp64) CPU reference and per-(op, dtype) absolute tolerances. The seeded oracle flags 9 of 9 buggy kernels and passes 15 of 15 correct controls, at zero precision cost on controls. We extend the corpus to 26 ops (adding a flash-attention pair) and re-run the same protocol on five GPU classes (RTX 3060, A10, L40S, A100 SXM4, H100 NVL). The verdicts are identical across all five GPUs: 10 of 10 illusions caught and 16 of 16 controls clean. The corpus result is about LLM-style transcription bugs that the allclose-on-one-shape oracle certifies as correct, not about the bug rate of any specific deployed LLM. Every flagged failure replays byte-for-byte from a stored seed.

AI.tldr: The authors demonstrate that current allclose-style benchmarks for LLM-generated GPU kernels miss systematic transcription bugs. They construct a corpus of 24 kernels with known buggy/correct labels and show that an op-schema-aware seeded fuzzing oracle with fp64 reference and per-(op, dtype) tolerances catches all 9 bugs (and 1 additional) while passing all correct controls, with identical results across five GPU classes.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks (KernelBench, TritonBench, GEAK) use fixed-shape, fixed-tolerance allclose checks which can certify buggy kernels as correct. The authors aim to empirically test this oracle weakness and propose a more rigorous evaluation methodology.
AI.method: (1) Construct a controlled corpus of 24 kernels (15 correct Triton/CPU stand-in controls + 9 buggy variants with documented LLM-style transcription errors). (2) Apply op-schema-aware seeded fuzzing with a high-precision (fp64) CPU reference and per-(op, dtype) absolute tolerances. (3) Extend corpus to 26 ops (add flash-attention pair) and repeat across 5 GPU classes (RTX 3060, A10, L40S, A100 SXM4, H100 NVL).
AI.result: The seeded oracle flags 9/9 buggy kernels and passes 15/15 controls at zero precision cost. Extended corpus: 10/10 illusions caught, 16/16 controls clean, verdicts identical across all 5 GPU classes.
AI.conclusion: Current allclose-on-one-shape oracles are insufficient for detecting LLM-style transcription bugs in GPU kernels; the proposed fuzzing methodology is more robust, hardware-agnostic, and reproducible via stored seeds.

title: Neural Events: Discrete Asynchronous Autoencoders for Event-Based Vision
summary: Event cameras capture dynamic scenes with exceptional temporal fidelity by representing them as a continuous stream of microsecond resolution \textit{events}. Each individual event, however, only carries minimal semantic value, merely signaling a localized brightness change. To derive meaningful signals, downstream algorithms need to quickly integrate cues from a potentially massive torrent of low-information events. Current architectures, however, are easily overwhelmed, struggling to balance capturing fine-grained temporal dynamics and maintaining a manageable data throughput. This paper proposes a framework to re-tokenize event streams into a small set of highly informative \textit{neural events}, each representing a local spatio-temporal context window with a discrete learnable code. Every time this code flips, a neural event is triggered, yielding a highly compressed data stream. We demonstrate that, across object detection and classification, networks trained on neural events are on par or surpass the performance of state-of-the-art approaches while reducing the event rate by a factor of 2.0.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes re-tokenizing event camera streams into sparse, learnable "neural events" that compress spatio-temporal context into discrete codes, achieving state-of-the-art performance on object detection and classification while reducing event rate by 2.0x.
AI.motivation: Event cameras generate massive streams of microsecond-resolution events, but each individual event carries minimal semantic meaning. Current architectures struggle to balance capturing fine-grained temporal dynamics with maintaining manageable data throughput.
AI.method: A framework that re-tokenizes event streams into a small set of highly informative "neural events," where each neural event represents a local spatio-temporal context window encoded as a discrete learnable code. A neural event is triggered whenever the code flips, producing a compressed data stream.
AI.result: Networks trained on neural events achieve performance on par with or superior to state-of-the-art approaches on object detection and classification, while reducing the event rate by a factor of 2.0.
AI.conclusion: Neural events offer an effective compression mechanism for event camera data, enabling efficient processing without sacrificing task performance, successfully balancing temporal fidelity and data throughput.

title: Benchmarking Agentic Review Systems
summary: A new class of agentic review systems are emerging as a remedy to the pressure placed on peer review systems by AI-assisted research, but it is unclear how they should be evaluated. We evaluate two open-source systems (OpenAIReview and coarse), one proprietary system (Reviewer3), and a zero-shot baseline, across six LLMs spanning frontier and efficient models. First, we study whether AI reviews on ICLR/NeurIPS papers track with papers' quality as approximated by external signals such as citations and acceptance decisions. Every system performs above chance in pairwise accuracy, and the best is OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5 at 83.0%. Second, to test whether systems can catch errors with known ground truth, we construct a perturbation benchmark that injects four categories of errors into papers across eight arXiv subject classes and measure detection recall. The strongest configuration (OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5) catches 71.6% of injected errors, leaving substantial room for improvement. The union of detections across six models reaches 83.3% recall, suggesting different models detect different errors and better harness design can potentially increase performance. Beyond these benchmarks, we study a public deployment of OpenAIReview with real users. Votes on its comments skew positive at 1.44 to 1, and the most common complaints are about false positives and minor nitpicks. Together, by evaluating full review systems backed by state-of-the-art models on real research papers, we show that while AI reviews still have room for improvement, they can already track human quality judgments well, catch important errors, and earn positive feedback from real users.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates AI-powered peer review systems (OpenAIReview, coarse, Reviewer3, and a zero-shot baseline) across six LLMs on ICLR/NeurIPS papers, finding that the best system (OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5) achieves 83.0% pairwise accuracy in tracking paper quality, catches 71.6% of injected errors (with ensemble recall of 83.3%), and receives positive user feedback (1.44:1 positive-to-negative vote ratio) despite room for improvement.
AI.motivation: AI-assisted research is overwhelming peer review systems, and agentic review systems are emerging as a potential remedy, but there is no clear methodology for evaluating their effectiveness, motivating the need for systematic benchmarking.
AI.method: The authors evaluate two open-source (OpenAIReview, coarse) and one proprietary (Reviewer3) review system plus a zero-shot baseline across six LLMs. They use pairwise accuracy against human quality signals (citations, acceptance decisions), a perturbation benchmark injecting four error categories across eight arXiv classes, and a real-user deployment study with vote analysis.
AI.result: Best system (OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5) achieves 83.0% pairwise accuracy and 71.6% error detection recall; ensemble of six models reaches 83.3% recall. Real-user votes skewed positive at 1.44:1, with common complaints about false positives and minor nitpicks.
AI.conclusion: AI reviews can track human quality judgments, catch important errors, and earn positive user feedback, but substantial room for improvement remains, particularly in error detection and reducing false positives.

title: MortarBench: Evaluating Mortgage Loan Origination Agents
summary: Loan origination is the process by which a lender creates a new loan, from application and underwriting through approval and funding. This process serves a critical role in evaluating the eligibility and level of risk posed by an applicant. Recently, firms have begun using mortgage loan agents to augment human loan officers, despite a lack of any public benchmark. To fill this gap, we present MortarBench, a loan origination agent benchmark. MortarBench uses a financial data synthesis and mutation pipeline to generate examples with broad edge case coverage that match real-world distributions and questions. We find that state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs) perform poorly, with closed-source models achieving at most 77.1\% exact match accuracy. We also discover systematic biases in LLM perception of foreignness related to non-English names. Noting these weaknesses, we introduce CRIT, a confidence calibration framework. Our method increases accuracy to 80.5\% while improving risk management steering and reducing bias.

AI.tldr: MortarBench is a benchmark for loan origination agents that reveals poor performance of LLMs (≤77.1% accuracy) and foreign-name biases. The proposed CRIT framework improves accuracy to 80.5%, enhances risk management, and reduces bias.
AI.motivation: Firms are deploying AI loan agents without public benchmarks to evaluate them. Existing LLMs lack domain-specific evaluation for loan origination, leaving their reliability, bias, and risk management capabilities unmeasured.
AI.method: The authors construct MortarBench using a financial data synthesis and mutation pipeline to generate realistic loan origination scenarios with broad edge-case coverage. They evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs and propose CRIT, a confidence calibration framework.
AI.result: Closed-source LLMs achieve at most 77.1% exact match accuracy. Systematic biases are found in LLM perception of foreignness related to non-English names. CRIT increases accuracy to 80.5% while improving risk management steering and reducing bias.
