AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are insufficient for reliable loan origination, but confidence calibration (CRIT) offers improvements in accuracy, risk management, and fairness. MortarBench provides a necessary evaluation standard for this domain.

title: Learning Critical Testing Literacy Through Puzzles: an Experience Report
summary:
In this paper, we report our experiences and takeaways from workshops using puzzles to learn CTL.
  Background: Software testing is important yet difficult to teach. We introduced a BoK of puzzle-based learning activities to teach CTL, based on a model of critical tester's cognition, leading to the pedagogical framework P4TEST. We conducted thirteen workshops with students, testers, teachers, and primary school pupils to assess puzzle-based teaching of critical testing literacy.
  Experience: Across eleven workshops, we used a semi-structured approach, varying puzzles, materials, and timing. In two additional workshops, we introduced workbooks and think-aloud sessions to gather more data on the learning experience.
  Observations: Participants consistently perceived themselves as experimenting while solving puzzles. Students tended to converge on solutions, while professionals continued exploring. Emotions were visible in behaviour but hard to surface through written reflection alone. Think-aloud sessions revealed immediate reasoning; written reflections elicited more meta-cognitive reflection. The theme Sensemaking / reflection-in-action captured how participants framed problems, navigated dead ends, and shifted strategies.
  Reflections: Puzzles are not the intervention: the entire sequence of solving, debriefing, and reflecting is. Designing that sequence more deliberately is the work ahead. We also developed an open-source web application with built-in analytics to customise workshops.

AI.tldr: The paper reports on thirteen workshops using puzzle-based learning to teach Critical Testing Literacy (CTL), finding that the full sequence of solving, debriefing, and reflecting constitutes the effective intervention, and that different participant groups (students vs. professionals) exhibit distinct problem-solving behaviors.
AI.motivation: Software testing is important but difficult to teach; the authors aimed to develop and assess a puzzle-based pedagogical approach to CTL based on a model of critical tester's cognition (P4TEST framework).
AI.method: Thirteen workshops were conducted (11 semi-structured, 2 with workbooks and think-aloud sessions) across diverse participant groups; data was gathered through observation, written reflection, and think-aloud protocols.
AI.result: Participants perceived themselves as experimenting; students converged on solutions while professionals continued exploring; emotions were visible behaviorally but hard to capture via written reflection; think-aloud captured immediate reasoning while written reflection yielded meta-cognitive insights.
AI.conclusion: The entire sequence of solving, debriefing, and reflecting—not just puzzles—is the intervention; future work involves designing this sequence more deliberately; an open-source web application with analytics was developed for workshop customization.

title: OTCHA: Optimal Transport-driven Confidence-aware Latent Hub Alignment for Multi-View Medical Image Classification
summary: Multi-view imaging, such as mammography and chest radiography, is a standard component of clinical practice. However, medical images are often unregistered and contain view-specific artifacts or irrelevant background cues that can obscure diagnostically relevant findings. Many existing methods directly fuse per-view representations, allowing such irrelevant content to contaminate the fused embedding and reducing robustness under varying view configurations. We propose OTCHA, a confidence-aware latent hub token alignment module based on optimal transport (OT) that refines patch tokens before fusion for multi-view classification. OTCHA introduces a set of learnable latent hub tokens shared across views. For each view, we compute an OT plan between patch tokens and hub tokens that jointly considers feature similarity and geometry, and augment the OT formulation with token-conditional dustbins to enable partial matching and discard irrelevant tokens. The resulting transport plan provides token-wise matching confidence, which gates hub-mediated message passing and weights a novel optimal-transport-based representation alignment loss to stabilize refinement. Experiments on three multi-view medical image datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over competing baselines across diverse anatomies and view configurations. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/OTCHA.

AI.tldr: OTCHA is a confidence-aware latent hub token alignment module using optimal transport that refines patch tokens before multi-view fusion, discarding irrelevant tokens via partial matching and improving multi-view medical image classification across diverse datasets.
AI.motivation: Multi-view medical imaging often suffers from unregistered views, view-specific artifacts, and irrelevant background cues that contaminate fused embeddings, reducing robustness under varying view configurations.
AI.method: OTCHA introduces learnable latent hub tokens shared across views, computes OT plans between patch tokens and hub tokens jointly considering feature similarity and geometry, augments OT with token-conditional dustbins for partial matching and irrelevant token discarding, uses transport-plan-based token-wise confidence for gated message passing and OT-based representation alignment loss.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over competing baselines on three multi-view medical image datasets across diverse anatomies and view configurations.
AI.conclusion: OTCHA effectively refines patch tokens before fusion via optimal transport-based partial matching with confidence gating, enhancing robustness and performance in multi-view medical image classification.

title: Grounded Inference: Principles for Deterministically Encapsulated Generative Models
summary: The incorporation of generative models into traditional computational systems presents both enormous opportunity and tremendous peril. Although many early adopters have realized these perils at great expense, the field still requires foundational frameworks to de-risk incorporation of AI into traditional systems. This manuscript establishes this foundation through the definition of four specific primitives of AI blended architecture, designed to enable deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models. It further establishes two overarching anti-patterns broadly represented across industry to serve as warnings for engineers in this field. This framework was designed to enable successful integration of AI into traditional systems while providing a foundation upon which generative model providers could build the next generation of generative model interfaces.

AI.tldr: This paper defines four primitives of AI-blended architecture for deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models and identifies two anti-patterns to guide safe integration of generative AI into traditional systems.
AI.motivation: The integration of generative models into traditional computational systems presents risks that have been realized at great expense by early adopters, yet the field lacks foundational frameworks to de-risk such incorporation.
AI.method: The paper establishes a framework by defining four specific primitives of AI-blended architecture designed to enable deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models, and identifies two overarching anti-patterns observed across industry.
AI.result: A foundational framework consisting of four architectural primitives for deterministic encapsulation and two identified anti-patterns serving as warnings for engineers.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables successful integration of AI into traditional systems and provides a foundation for generative model providers to build next-generation interfaces.

title: 3D-DLP: Self-Supervised 3D Object-Centric Scene Representation Learning
summary: We introduce 3D-DLP, a self-supervised object-centric representation learning model that decomposes scene-level RGB-D or voxel observations into a set of 3D latent particles. Building on the Deep Latent Particles (DLP) framework, each particle encodes disentangled attributes, including 3D keypoint position, bounding box dimensions, and appearance features, and represents a distinct entity in the scene. The model learns interpretable per-particle segmentation maps through an end-to-end self-supervised reconstruction objective. We demonstrate on both simulated and real-world datasets that the learned latent space is interpretable and controllable: by manipulating particle positions and decoding, we can generate novel scene configurations. Furthermore, we show that leveraging these compact 3D latent particles for downstream robotic manipulation improves performance over baselines that either lack explicit 3D information or rely on memory-intensive dense 3D inputs without object-centric structure. Code and videos are available at https://eubooks3003.github.io/3d-dlp.

AI.tldr: 3D-DLP is a self-supervised model that learns 3D object-centric representations by decomposing scene observations into latent particles (3D keypoints, bounding boxes, appearance), enabling interpretable segmentation, controllable scene generation, and improved downstream robotic manipulation.
AI.motivation: To learn interpretable, object-centric 3D representations from unlabeled RGB-D or voxel data without supervision, enabling controllable scene generation and efficient downstream manipulation.
AI.method: Extends the Deep Latent Particles (DLP) framework to 3D, where each latent particle encodes disentangled attributes (3D keypoint position, bounding box dimensions, appearance features). Learned via self-supervised reconstruction with per-particle segmentation maps, using RGB-D or voxel inputs.
AI.result: The learned latent space is interpretable and controllable (particle manipulation enables novel scene generation). Using these 3D latent particles for robotic manipulation outperforms baselines lacking explicit 3D structure or relying on memory-intensive dense 3D inputs.
AI.conclusion: 3D-DLP provides a compact, interpretable, object-centric 3D representation beneficial for both generative scene control and robotic manipulation tasks.

title: N-Version Programming with Coding Agents
summary: This paper revisits the classical concept on N-version programming in the setting of contemporary AI coding agents. Revisiting the seminal Knight-Leveson experiment, we study whether diversity across agent systems, models, and implementation languages creates diverse failure modes. Using the Knight-Leveson's, Launch Interceptor Program Specification, we evaluate 48 agent-generated implementations on a shared oracle and a campaign of 1,000,000 randomized test inputs. The results show substantial common-mode failure, along the findings of Knight-Leveson. Further analysis that many of those co-occuring failures can be traced to where is specification is particularly hard or ambiguous. We also demonstrate that diversity from coding agents provides practical benefit: across majority voting three-version units, the mean failure count drops from 387.44 for single versions to 130.99 for triples, and 11,844 N-version units exhibit zero observed failures. Our original results is the strongest evidence to date that N-Version Programming with coding agents is a useful engineering strategy.

AI.tldr: This paper revisits N-version programming with AI coding agents using the Knight-Leveson Launch Interceptor Program, finding common-mode failures from ambiguous specifications but demonstrating that majority voting across diverse agent-generated implementations significantly reduces failures, with many N-version units showing zero failures.
AI.motivation: To revisit the classical Knight-Leveson experiment on N-version programming in the context of modern AI coding agents, and to study whether diversity across agent systems, models, and implementation languages creates diverse failure modes that improve reliability.
AI.method: Used the Knight-Leveson Launch Interceptor Program Specification to generate 48 implementations via diverse coding agents (varying agent systems, models, and languages), then evaluated them on a shared oracle with 1,000,000 randomized test inputs, analyzing failure patterns and evaluating majority voting across three-version units.
AI.result: Substantial common-mode failures were found, often traceable to ambiguous/hard specification parts. Majority voting across three-version units reduced mean failure count from 387.44 (single versions) to 130.99 (triples), and 11,844 N-version units exhibited zero observed failures.
AI.conclusion: N-Version Programming with coding agents is a useful engineering strategy, providing the strongest evidence to date that diversity improves reliability in this setting.

title: ViCoStream: Streaming VideoLLMs Can Run Beyond 100 FPS with Stage-Wise Coordinated Inference
summary: Streaming VideoLLMs must continuously process incoming video while maintaining low query latency, making both video-ingestion throughput and query-time responsiveness critical for real-time deployment. Existing methods largely focus on accelerating individual modules, such as visual encoding, token pruning, or KV-cache compression, but provide limited insight into whether the resulting system can sustain real-time streaming performance. We formulate streaming VideoLLM inference as a coordinated pipeline spanning visual preprocessing, visual encoding, token dropping, and LLM prefilling/decoding. Building on this formulation, we propose ViCoStream (Video Coordinated Streaming), a stage-wise coordinated streaming framework that combines chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval to bound per-chunk computation and memory costs. We further provide a systematic study of bottleneck migration, revealing how chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope shape the throughput-accuracy trade-off. Experiments with Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct across multiple streaming benchmarks show that ViCoStream achieves 134 FPS video throughput and less than 50 ms TTFT on a single A100 GPU while maintaining accuracy close to full-history baselines.

AI.tldr: ViCoStream is a stage-wise coordinated streaming framework for VideoLLMs that achieves 134 FPS video throughput and <50ms TTFT on a single A100 by coordinating visual preprocessing, encoding, token dropping, and LLM prefilling/decoding via chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded attention, and query-side retrieval.
AI.motivation: Existing methods focus on accelerating individual modules (visual encoding, token pruning, KV-cache compression) but lack a holistic view of whether the overall system can sustain real-time streaming performance. Both video-ingestion throughput and query-time responsiveness are critical for real-time deployment.
AI.method: Formulates streaming VideoLLM inference as a coordinated pipeline (visual preprocessing → visual encoding → token dropping → LLM prefilling/decoding). Proposes ViCoStream with chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap for parallelism, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval to bound per-chunk computation and memory costs. Includes systematic study of bottleneck migration across chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope.
AI.result: Achieves 134 FPS video throughput and <50 ms TTFT on a single A100 GPU with Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct across multiple streaming benchmarks, while maintaining accuracy close to full-history baselines.
AI.conclusion: ViCoStream demonstrates that a coordinated, stage-wise approach to streaming VideoLLM inference can deliver real-time performance without sacrificing accuracy, and provides actionable insights into how pipeline design choices influence the throughput-accuracy trade-off.

title: Optimal Scheduling in a Question-Answering Forum of Knowledge Workers
summary: As individuals turn to the Internet to find answers to questions they may have, several Question Answering (QA) forums have evolved, where users knowledgeable in certain topics can contribute their expertise to answering these requests for information. While these are currently volunteer based, we consider a future version employing knowledge workers who are experts in certain topics. In such a system, the request-answer processes forming the queuing system may utilize schedulers that assign requests in different topics to the experts in the forum, who may be able to answer them according to their expertise levels in different topics. With this model, we calculate the capacity of the system for handling the requests while keeping the system stable, and design schedulers that achieve capacity. We also investigate how collaboration between experts in answering requests can potentially increase capacity.

AI.tldr: This paper models QA forums with expert knowledge workers as a queuing system, calculates system capacity for stable request handling, designs schedulers to achieve capacity, and explores how expert collaboration can increase capacity.
AI.motivation: To address the scalability and reliability challenges of volunteer-based QA forums by proposing a future model with paid knowledge workers, and to formally analyze the capacity and scheduling of such a system.
AI.method: Models the request-answer processes as a queuing system where schedulers assign topic-specific requests to experts based on their expertise levels; calculates system capacity for stability; designs schedulers that achieve this capacity; analyzes collaboration effects between experts.
AI.result: Derives the capacity of the QA forum system for handling requests while maintaining stability; proposes schedulers that achieve capacity; shows that collaboration between experts can potentially increase system capacity.
AI.conclusion: A formal queuing model can characterize and optimize expert-based QA forums, and collaboration among experts offers a promising avenue for enhancing system throughput.

title: Can In-Context Learning Support Intrinsic Curiosity?
summary: Effective machine learning depends not only on how we model data, but also on what data we choose to collect. While large sequence models have revolutionized data modeling, the problem of automated data selection, or "intrinsic curiosity", remains a significant challenge. Classic approaches incentivize exploration by rewarding an agent based on its "learning progress", which measures how much a newly acquired observation improves a world model's predictive ability. However, evaluating these rewards traditionally requires expensive inner loops of gradient descent updates within each trajectory, rendering them computationally impractical at scale. In this work, we investigate whether the emergent in-context learning (ICL) capabilities of sequence models can eliminate this bottleneck by serving as immediate, update-free world models. Specifically, we evaluate whether an exploration policy can be trained to maximize learning progress, using solely the prediction errors and counterfactual context manipulations of an in-context learner. We first prove that in general Markov decision processes, this is in fact impossible in an unbiased way: the resulting intrinsic rewards either suffer from nuisance terms that bias their estimation of true learning progress, or they cannot be implemented using an in-context learner's prediction errors. Conversely, we prove a positive result for a broad subclass of non-temporal settings, encompassing active learning and Bayesian Experimental Design: here, ICL-derived rewards successfully bound and asymptotically converge to the true learning progress. We corroborate our theory with controlled experiments across continuous and symbolic environments, demonstrating that our ICL-driven framework successfully trains curious data-collection policies that explore optimally.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether in-context learning (ICL) from sequence models can replace expensive gradient-based learning progress computation for intrinsic curiosity/exploration in reinforcement learning. The authors prove theoretically that unbiased estimation is impossible in general MDPs, but positive results hold for non-temporal settings like active learning. Experiments confirm ICL-driven exploration policies can train curious agents effectively.
AI.motivation: Automated data selection (intrinsic curiosity) for exploration is computationally expensive because traditional methods require inner-loop gradient descent per trajectory to measure learning progress. The authors aim to use emergent in-context learning capabilities of sequence models as update-free world models to eliminate this bottleneck.
AI.method: The authors theoretically analyze whether ICL-based prediction errors and counterfactual context manipulations can unbiasedly estimate learning progress. They prove an impossibility result for general MDPs (nuisance terms or implementation issues) and a positive result for non-temporal settings (active learning, Bayesian Experimental Design). Controlled experiments in continuous and symbolic environments validate the theory.
AI.result: (1) In general MDPs, unbiased estimation of learning progress via ICL is impossible due to nuisance bias or implementation constraints. (2) For non-temporal settings (active learning, BED), ICL-derived rewards bound and asymptotically converge to true learning progress. (3) Experimental results confirm ICL-driven frameworks can train curious data-collection policies that explore optimally.
AI.conclusion: ICL can serve as an efficient, update-free proxy for learning progress in certain settings, enabling scalable intrinsic curiosity mechanisms, though its applicability is fundamentally limited in general MDPs.

title: Qiskit Code Migration with LLMs
summary: The rapid evolution of Quantum Development Kits (QDKs) introduces a specific form of technical debt that compromises code maintainability and hinders software reuse. In the specialized domain of Quantum Software Engineering (QSE), this challenge is intensified by the scarcity of high-quality training data and the high volatility of emerging frameworks, which often lead general-purpose Large Language Models (LLMs) to produce unreliable or hallucinated results. This paper proposes a hybrid approach integrating LLMs with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to automate the migration of Qiskit code across versions. The proposed methodology enhances the precision and reliability of migration suggestions by leveraging an automatically generated taxonomy of migration scenarios as the structured, version-specific knowledge source to guide the models. The approach is implemented through an automated, extensible workflow evaluating LLMs (Google Gemini Flash-2.5 and OpenAI Gpt-oss-20b) under different retrieval schemes (unconstrained and restrictive). Results demonstrate that the taxonomy-based RAG architecture, particularly under the restrictive scheme, significantly reduces hallucinations and improves descriptive quality, with Google Gemini Flash-2.5 showing superior performance in detecting complex refactoring scenarios. These findings confirm the potential of this data-centric methodology to foster technological independence and provide robust, intelligent assistants that mitigate API obsolescence, ensuring the long-term availability of quantum algorithms within a rapidly shifting ecosystem and flattening the learning curve within Quantum Software Engineering (QSE).

AI.tldr: Proposes a hybrid LLM + Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) approach with taxonomy-based retrieval to automate Qiskit code migration across versions, reducing hallucinations and improving reliability in Quantum Software Engineering.
AI.motivation: Quantum Development Kits (QDKs) rapidly evolve, introducing technical debt that hinders maintainability and reuse. General-purpose LLMs produce unreliable/hallucinated results in QSE due to scarce training data and high framework volatility, necessitating a more robust migration approach.
AI.method: Hybrid approach integrating LLMs (Google Gemini Flash-2.5, OpenAI Gpt-oss-20b) with Retrieval-Augmented Generation, using an automatically generated taxonomy of migration scenarios as structured, version-specific knowledge. Evaluated under unconstrained vs. restrictive retrieval schemes.
AI.result: Taxonomy-based RAG (especially restrictive scheme) significantly reduces hallucinations and improves descriptive quality. Google Gemini Flash-2.5 outperformed in detecting complex refactoring scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The data-centric methodology fosters technological independence, provides robust intelligent assistants to mitigate API obsolescence, ensures long-term availability of quantum algorithms, and flattens the learning curve in QSE.

title: PSCT-Net: Geometry-Aware Pediatric Skull CT Reconstruction via Differentiable Back-Projection and Attention-Guided Refinement
summary: Computed Tomography (CT) is essential for diagnosing pediatric craniofacial abnormalities, yet poses radiation risks to developing anatomies. Reconstructing 3D CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays offers a low-dose alternative but is severely ill-posed. Existing methods employ geometry-agnostic feature lifting, naively projecting 2D features into 3D without explicit spatial modeling, causing depth ambiguity and degraded osseous boundaries. We present PSCT-Net, a geometry-aware framework with differentiable back-projection. Differentiable back-projection establishes a spatially faithful volumetric prior, alleviating depth ambiguity. An Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D) module then learns non-linear voxel-wise correspondences between 2D regions and 3D locations. A Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) module captures long-range volumetric dependencies with linear complexity. We further curate a private institutional pediatric skull CT cohort, PedSkull-CT, comprising normal and pathological cases for internal evaluation, addressing the gap in adult-centric, trunk-focused datasets.

AI.tldr: PSCT-Net reconstructs 3D CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays using geometry-aware differentiable back-projection, Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D), and Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) modules, evaluated on a new pediatric skull CT dataset (PedSkull-CT).
AI.motivation: CT is essential for diagnosing pediatric craniofacial abnormalities but poses radiation risks. Reconstructing 3D CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays offers a low-dose alternative, yet existing methods are geometry-agnostic, causing depth ambiguity and degraded osseous boundaries.
AI.method: PSCT-Net: (1) differentiable back-projection for spatially faithful volumetric prior; (2) Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D) for non-linear voxel-wise 2D-3D correspondences; (3) Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) for long-range volumetric dependencies with linear complexity.
AI.result: Curated a private institutional pediatric skull CT cohort (PedSkull-CT) with normal and pathological cases for evaluation, addressing the gap in adult-centric, trunk-focused datasets.
AI.conclusion: PSCT-Net provides a geometry-aware, low-dose alternative for pediatric craniofacial 3D CT reconstruction from sparse bi-planar X-rays.

title: Beyond Entropy: Learning from Token-Level Distributional Deviations for LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning; however, it faces a fundamental optimization instability: uniform token updates precipitate entropy collapse, leading to premature convergence to suboptimal strategies, whereas excessive Shannon Entropy maximization can cause entropy explosion, driving blind exploration toward incoherent reasoning chains. To resolve this dichotomy, we introduce the Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT) framework, which shifts the optimization focus from scalar uncertainty to the distributional properties of token logits. By leveraging the Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence between token logits distributions, ICT identifies tokens with distinctive distributional patterns as critical branching points for guiding effective exploration in LLM reasoning. Our theoretical analysis, grounded in both Shannon and second-order Rényi entropy, proves that selectively updating on these tokens regulates policy concentration: it reduces the overall distribution uncertainty measured by Shannon entropy, while controlling probability concentration captured by second-order Rényi entropy. This dual effect prevents over-concentrated token generation from weakening exploration and effectively stabilizes the training landscape. Empirical results demonstrate that updating only the top 10% of unique tokens on Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/7B) models yields an average pass@4 improvement of 4.58%, with a maximum gain of 14.9%, over GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines across seven benchmarks spanning math, commonsense, and Olympiad-level problems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT), a framework that uses Jensen-Shannon divergence between token logits to identify critical branching points for selective updates, resolving the entropy collapse vs. explosion dilemma in RLVR for LLM reasoning. It achieves 4.58% average pass@4 improvement (up to 14.9%) over baselines.
AI.motivation: RLVR faces a fundamental optimization instability dilemma: uniform token updates cause entropy collapse (premature convergence to suboptimal strategies), while excessive Shannon entropy maximization causes entropy explosion (blind exploration toward incoherent reasoning chains).
AI.method: ICT shifts optimization focus from scalar uncertainty to distributional properties of token logits, using JS divergence between token logits distributions to identify distinctive tokens as critical branching points, then selectively updating only these tokens.
AI.result: Updating only the top 10% of unique tokens on Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/7B) yields an average pass@4 improvement of 4.58% (max 14.9%) over GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines across seven benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Selective updating on distributionally distinctive tokens reduces Shannon entropy (overall uncertainty) while controlling Rényi entropy (probability concentration), preventing over-concentrated generation and stabilizing training.

title: Insulin4RL: Real-Time Insulin Management in the Intensive Care Unit for Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (ORL) offers the potential to improve the quality of clinical decision-making using historical electronic health record (EHR) data. Current training and evaluative practices in this field rely heavily on EHR datasets that have been temporally discretised into fixed, regular time intervals. Discretisation creates fictional representations of complex clinical scenarios and compromises the generalisability of retrospective model evaluations. In this paper, we introduce Insulin4RL, a healthcare ORL dataset featuring naturally irregular inputs and actions from real clinical trajectories. Derived from MIMIC-IV, Insulin4RL comprises over 375,000 labelled decisions across 12,209 patients requiring insulin infusion titration in the Intensive Care Unit. The dataset can thus be used for research into ORL model performance under realistic clinical sampling assumptions. We provide a description of the dataset's structure and characteristics, baseline performance metrics using model-free offline reinforcement learning, and a standardised evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation. We conclude with suggested areas for future research that could be addressed using this resource.

AI.tldr: Insulin4RL is a healthcare ORL dataset with naturally irregular clinical trajectories from MIMIC-IV, comprising 375k+ decisions across 12,209 ICU patients for insulin titration, with baseline metrics and evaluation protocols.
AI.motivation: Current ORL practices rely on temporally discretised EHR datasets that create fictional representations of clinical scenarios and compromise generalisability. There is a need for a dataset with naturally irregular inputs and actions from real clinical trajectories.
AI.method: Derived Insulin4RL from MIMIC-IV, featuring naturally irregular temporal sampling; provided baseline performance using model-free offline RL; established a standardised evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation.
AI.result: Created a dataset of over 375,000 labelled decisions across 12,209 ICU patients requiring insulin infusion titration, with accompanying baseline metrics and evaluation protocol.
AI.conclusion: Insulin4RL enables research into ORL model performance under realistic clinical sampling assumptions; the paper suggests future research directions using this resource.

title: SysML Modeling of Digital Twins for Renewable Energy Communities
comment: Presented at the Workshop on Digital Twin Experiences and Model-Based Testing Methods, 12 June 2026, Västerås, Sweden, co-located with the 30th Ada-Europe International Conference on Reliable Software Technologies (AEiC 2026)
summary: Renewable Energy Communities (RECs) are emerging as a key organizational model for local and global sharing of renewable generation, storage, and flexible loads. Engineering Digital Twins of RECs is made difficult by the heterogeneity of devices, contracts, and runtime data involved. In this paper, we take a first step toward a Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) workflow for REC's Digital Twins. Starting from an industrially-validated REC domain model, we re-express a representative house subset in SysML using the open-source Modelio tool, yielding two Block Definition Diagrams - a device taxonomy and a community organizational view. We then discuss four semantic gaps that plain SysML leaves open and sketch how the SAREF4ENER ontology could be imported as a reference package to close them. Combining SysML with SAREF-based semantics for smart-energy Digital Twins remains largely unexplored, and we position this paper as a first step along that line.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a first step toward a Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) workflow for Digital Twins of Renewable Energy Communities (RECs), using SysML to model a house subset and identifying semantic gaps that could be addressed by importing the SAREF4ENER ontology.
AI.motivation: Engineering Digital Twins of RECs is difficult due to the heterogeneity of devices, contracts, and runtime data involved.
AI.method: Re-express an industrially-validated REC domain model in SysML using Modelio, producing two Block Definition Diagrams (device taxonomy and community organizational view). Then discuss four semantic gaps in plain SysML and propose importing SAREF4ENER ontology to close them.
AI.result: Two SysML Block Definition Diagrams were created for a representative house subset; four semantic gaps in plain SysML were identified.
AI.conclusion: Combining SysML with SAREF-based semantics for smart-energy Digital Twins remains largely unexplored; this paper is positioned as an initial step along that research line.

title: Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Models
comment: Present at NeurIPS 2025 Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance the interpretability of deep learning networks by aligning the features extracted from images with natural concepts. However, existing CBMs are constrained in their ability to generalize beyond a fixed set of predefined classes and the risk of non-concept information leakage, where predictive signals outside the intended concepts are inadvertently exploited. In this paper, we propose Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Model (MM-CBM) to address these issues and extend CBMs into CLIP. MM-CBM utilizes dual Concept Bottleneck Layers (CBLs) to align both the image and text embeddings into interpretable features. This allows us to perform new vision tasks like zero-shot classification or image retrieval in an interpretable way. Compared to existing methods, MM-CBM achieves up to 51.26% accuracy improvement on average across four standard benchmarks. Our method maintains high accuracy, staying within ~5% of black-box performance while offering greater interpretability.

AI.tldr: MM-CBM extends Concept Bottleneck Models into the CLIP framework using dual Concept Bottleneck Layers for improved interpretability and generalization, achieving up to 51.26% accuracy improvement on benchmarks while staying within ~5% of black-box performance.
AI.motivation: Existing CBMs are limited by poor generalization beyond predefined classes and suffer from non-concept information leakage, where predictive signals outside intended concepts are exploited.
AI.method: Proposes Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Model (MM-CBM) that integrates dual Concept Bottleneck Layers (CBLs) into CLIP to align both image and text embeddings into interpretable features.
AI.result: Achieves up to 51.26% accuracy improvement on average across four standard benchmarks, maintaining accuracy within ~5% of black-box performance.
AI.conclusion: MM-CBM enables interpretable zero-shot classification and image retrieval, addressing key limitations of prior CBMs while preserving high accuracy.

title: AgentFinVQA: A Deployable Multi-Agent Pipeline for Auditable Financial Chart QA
summary: Financial chart question answering in regulated settings demands more than accuracy: practitioners must know which answers to trust before acting on them, and many institutions cannot send client data to external model providers. Yet existing chart-QA agents are accuracy-focused and opaque, and most assume proprietary API access; to our knowledge, none combines auditability with on-premise deployability without significant accuracy compromise. We present AgentFinVQA, a multi-agent pipeline that decomposes each query into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification, recording every step in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample. On FinMME, AgentFinVQA improves $+7.68$ pp over a primary-backbone matched zero-shot baseline with a proprietary backbone (Gemini-3 Flash; 71.24% vs. 63.56%, McNemar $p \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-16}$), and $+4.84$ pp with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 served locally. The verifier's verdict also serves as a useful confidence signal (68.2% vs. 55.6% exact accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers), enabling human-in-the-loop review routing. Error analysis shows that question misunderstanding, legend confusion and extraction error account for nearly two-thirds of failures and are the categories least detected by the verifier, identifying clear directions for future work. Together these results show that auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical and that the open-weights system keeps most of the accuracy gains while enabling full data residency. We release our code to support reproducible evaluation.

AI.tldr: AgentFinVQA is a multi-agent pipeline for financial chart QA that achieves auditable, on-premise deployment by decomposing queries into traceable sub-tasks, improving accuracy by +7.68 pp over zero-shot baselines (Gemini-3 Flash) and +4.84 pp with open-weights models while providing verifier-based confidence signals for human-in-the-loop review.
AI.motivation: Existing chart-QA agents are accuracy-focused, opaque, and assume proprietary API access; regulated financial institutions need both trustworthiness (auditability) and the ability to keep client data on-premise without significant accuracy loss.
AI.method: A multi-agent pipeline decomposing each query into five stages: planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification. Every step is recorded in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample.
AI.result: AgentFinVQA achieves +7.68 pp over zero-shot with Gemini-3 Flash (71.24% vs. 63.56%, p≈1.1×10⁻¹⁶) and +4.84 pp with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 served locally. Verifier confidence signal distinguishes 68.2% vs. 55.6% accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers.
AI.conclusion: Auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical; the open-weights system retains most accuracy gains while enabling full data residency. Error analysis identifies question misunderstanding, legend confusion, and extraction errors as key future improvement directions.

title: Concept Flow Models: Anchoring Concept-Based Reasoning with Hierarchical Bottlenecks
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance interpretability by projecting learned features into a human-understandable concept space. Recent approaches leverage vision-language models to generate concept embeddings, reducing the need for manual concept annotations. However, these models suffer from a critical limitation: as the number of concepts approaches the embedding dimension, information leakage increases, enabling the model to exploit spurious or semantically irrelevant correlations and undermining interpretability. In this work, we propose Concept Flow Models (CFMs), which replace the flat bottleneck with a hierarchical, concept-driven decision tree. Each internal node in the hierarchy focuses on a localized subset of discriminative concepts, progressively narrowing the prediction scope. Our framework constructs decision hierarchies from visual embeddings, distributes semantic concepts at each hierarchy level, and trains differentiable concept weights through probabilistic tree traversal. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that CFMs match the predictive performance of flat CBMs, while substantially mitigating information leakage by reducing effective concept usage. Furthermore, CFMs yield stepwise decision flows that enable transparent and auditable model reasoning with hierarchical class structures.

AI.tldr: Concept Flow Models (CFMs) replace flat concept bottlenecks with hierarchical decision trees to mitigate information leakage in Concept Bottleneck Models, achieving comparable performance while reducing spurious concept correlations and enabling transparent, stepwise reasoning.
AI.motivation: Flat Concept Bottleneck Models suffer from information leakage as the number of concepts approaches the embedding dimension, allowing models to exploit spurious or semantically irrelevant correlations, which undermines interpretability.
AI.method: CFMs replace the flat bottleneck with a hierarchical, concept-driven decision tree. Internal nodes focus on localized subsets of discriminative concepts, progressively narrowing prediction scope. The framework constructs decision hierarchies from visual embeddings, distributes semantic concepts at each hierarchy level, and trains differentiable concept weights through probabilistic tree traversal.
AI.result: CFMs match the predictive performance of flat CBMs while substantially mitigating information leakage by reducing effective concept usage, validated on diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: CFMs yield stepwise decision flows that enable transparent and auditable model reasoning with hierarchical class structures, improving interpretability without sacrificing performance.

title: Phoenix: Safe GitHub Issue Resolution via Multi-Agent LLMs
summary: We present Phoenix, a multi-agent LLM system that resolves GitHub issues from triage through pull-request creation, combining seven layered safety controls with a baseline-aware test evaluation strategy. Phoenix decomposes the work across six specialized agents. Planner, reproducer, coder, tester, failure analyst and Pull Request (PR) agent, all coordinated by a label-based GitHub webhook state machine. Every change is checked against a baseline test run before a pull request is opened. On a 24-instance slice of SWE-bench Lite. run on the production webhook path, Phoenix oracle-resolves 75% of instances with no pass-to-pass regressions on successful runs; this curated slice is not directly comparable to full-split leaderboard results, and we discuss the limits of the comparison. A complementary pilot on 42 real issues across 14 repositories yields 100% correctness preservation (CP; mean 122s on the hard tier). Manual inspection shows that about half of the resulting pull requests are well-targeted fixes. The other half place code at incorrect paths, a planner localization limitation we are addressing with retrieval. We also report the deployment failure modes (WAF filtering, token expiry, permission boundaries, flaky CI) that motivated each safety mechanism.

AI.tldr: Phoenix is a multi-agent LLM system that automates GitHub issue resolution from triage to PR creation using six specialized agents and seven safety controls, achieving 75% oracle-resolution on a curated SWE-bench slice and 100% correctness preservation on real issues, though with limitations in code localization and deployment failure modes.
AI.motivation: To automate the full lifecycle of GitHub issue resolution (triage through PR creation) using a multi-agent LLM system while ensuring safety and reliability through layered controls and baseline-aware test evaluation.
AI.method: Phoenix uses six specialized agents (Planner, Reproducer, Coder, Tester, Failure Analyst, PR Agent) coordinated by a label-based GitHub webhook state machine, with seven layered safety controls and a baseline test comparison strategy before PR creation.
AI.result: On a 24-instance SWE-bench Lite slice: 75% oracle-resolution with no pass-to-pass regressions. On 42 real issues across 14 repositories: 100% correctness preservation (mean 122s hard tier). ~50% of PRs are well-targeted fixes; remaining have incorrect code paths due to planner localization limitations.
AI.conclusion: Phoenix demonstrates effective automated issue resolution but faces challenges in code localization accuracy and deployment failure modes (WAF filtering, token expiry, permission boundaries, flaky CI), which motivated the safety mechanism design.

title: SurgVista: Long-Horizon Surgical World Modeling with Plausible Instrument-Tissue Dynamics
summary: Scaling robot policy learning for autonomous surgery is challenging, as expert demonstrations are expensive and in vivo exploration poses substantial safety risks. Surgical world models address this by generating realistic, action-conditioned future frames from an initial observation, but existing methods exhibit two persistent failure modes: spatial interaction incoherence, where visible instrument contact fails to induce spatially consistent tissue deformation, and temporal fidelity collapse, where prediction errors compound across autoregressive rollouts and progressively corrupt visual quality. We present SurgVista, a surgical world model that mitigates both failures through two training recipes. Deformation Consistency Regularization extracts scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforces cross-frame coherence through latent contrastive learning, strengthening physically consistent instrument-tissue dynamics. Drift Adaptation Training mitigates long-horizon drift by perturbing conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations calibrated to long-horizon drift statistics, sustaining visual fidelity over extended rollouts. To enable rigorous evaluation, we further introduce SurgWorld-Bench, featuring diverse procedure types, long-range rollouts, and decoupled metrics for instrument-motion accuracy and tissue-response fidelity. Extensive experiments show that SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, with gains widening as the prediction horizon grows.

AI.tldr: SurgVista introduces Deformation Consistency Regularization and Drift Adaptation Training to address spatial interaction incoherence and temporal fidelity collapse in surgical world models, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on the new SurgWorld-Bench benchmark.
AI.motivation: Scaling robot policy learning for autonomous surgery is challenging due to expensive expert demonstrations and safety risks of in vivo exploration. Surgical world models can generate realistic future frames but suffer from spatial interaction incoherence (instrument-tissue contact not producing consistent deformation) and temporal fidelity collapse (compounding prediction errors degrading visual quality over long rollouts).
AI.method: SurgVista uses two training recipes: (1) Deformation Consistency Regularization, which extracts scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforces cross-frame coherence via latent contrastive learning; (2) Drift Adaptation Training, which perturbs conditioning frames using online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations calibrated to long-horizon drift statistics.
AI.result: SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, with performance gains widening as the prediction horizon increases. The SurgWorld-Bench benchmark enables rigorous evaluation with diverse procedures, long-range rollouts, and decoupled metrics for instrument-motion accuracy and tissue-response fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Both failure modes in surgical world modeling can be effectively mitigated through trajectory-aware deformation regularization and drift-calibrated training, enabling more reliable long-horizon video prediction for autonomous surgical applications.

title: ORAgentBench: Can LLM Agents Solve Challenging Operations Research Tasks End to End?
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents for multi-step tasks in executable environments, yet their ability to perform realistic operations research (OR) work remains unclear. Existing OR evaluations often decouple modeling from solving, rely on pre-formalized or text-only instances, and rarely test the full workflow from operational artifacts to validated decisions. In this work, we introduce ORAgentBench, an execution-grounded benchmark for evaluating autonomous agents on challenging end-to-end operations research tasks. It contains 107 human-reviewed tasks across diverse operational scenarios, each packaged in an isolated environment with a natural-language brief, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and a required submission schema. Agents must write and run solution code, and their submissions are evaluated by hidden validators for schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality. Experiments with fourteen frontier agent-model configurations show that current agents remain far from reliable OR practice. The best agent passes only 35.51% of all tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks, and many feasible submissions still fall below the required quality threshold. Failure analysis further shows that errors are dominated by strategic weaknesses, including missed operational rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible-solution construction, and insufficient solution improvement. OR-specific procedural skills increase hard-task feasibility, but do not reliably improve solution quality or pass rate. These results suggest that progress in OR agents requires moving beyond plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

AI.tldr: ORAgentBench is a benchmark evaluating LLM agents on end-to-end operations research tasks; current agents pass only 35.51% of tasks, with failures mainly due to strategic weaknesses rather than coding issues.
AI.motivation: Existing OR evaluations decouple modeling from solving, rely on pre-formalized or text-only instances, and fail to test the full workflow from operational artifacts to validated decisions, leaving a gap in assessing LLM agents' realistic OR capabilities.
AI.method: The authors construct ORAgentBench with 107 human-reviewed OR tasks across diverse scenarios, each in an isolated environment with natural-language briefs, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and submission schemas. Agents write and run solution code, evaluated by hidden validators for schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality.
AI.result: Best agent passes only 35.51% of all tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks. Many feasible submissions fall below required quality thresholds. OR-specific procedural skills improve hard-task feasibility but not solution quality or pass rate.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM agents are far from reliable OR practice; progress requires moving beyond plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

title: Algebraic Dead Directions in LayerNorm Transformers: A Forward-Pass-Only Diagnostic at LLM Scale
comment: 34 pages, 7 figures, 6 tables. Empirical companion to arXiv:2606.05957
summary: Pretrained transformers sit near singular minima of the loss, where the Fisher information metric degenerates along dead directions: directions in parameter space along which the directional Fisher vanishes. Locating such a direction normally needs a forward pass and an eigendecomposition of activations, or a sampling-based complexity estimate; none returns a direction computable from the network's parameters alone. We give one, for LayerNorm transformers. The inverse-scale direction $γ^{-1}/\|γ^{-1}\|$ of the LayerNorm affine is an exact algebraic kernel of the post-final-norm centred activation covariance, for any input distribution, and induces a corresponding dead direction in parameter space. It is read from the LN scale parameter alone, with no forward or backward pass and no eigensolve: the cheapest dead-direction read, specific to LayerNorm. We test it on $14$ pretrained transformers ($9$ LayerNorm, $5$ RMSNorm; $160$M-$35$B; language and vision objectives). At random initialisation the predicted direction matches the measured bottom singular direction (one forward pass, direct SVD) to four decimal places on $9/9$ LayerNorm models, and is correctly absent on $5/5$ RMSNorm models, which lack the mean-subtraction projector that creates it. On the trained checkpoint the covariance eigenvalue along this direction deepens by ${\sim}10^3\times$ and further dead directions open; the random-init-to-trained gap is a one-forward-pass, per-checkpoint readout of singular structure along the predicted coordinate. Two consequences follow in closed form: the residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block on $13/14$ transformers measured on their own input distribution, the one exception (Gemma$4$-$31$B) a genuine dead direction the same read pinpoints; and the kernel direction's presence classifies a transformer's normalisation from the parameters alone.

AI.tldr: Pretrained LayerNorm transformers have a dead direction in parameter space that can be read directly from the LayerNorm scale parameter without any forward/backward pass or eigendecomposition. This direction matches the bottom singular direction with high precision, deepens ~1000x after training, and enables closed-form analysis of residual stream singular values and normalization type classification.
AI.motivation: To identify a dead direction (where Fisher information vanishes) in pretrained transformers that can be computed purely from network parameters without forward passes, backward passes, or eigendecompositions, which is normally computationally expensive.
AI.method: The authors mathematically derive that for LayerNorm transformers, the inverse-scale direction γ⁻¹/‖γ⁻¹‖ of the LayerNorm affine parameter is an exact algebraic kernel of the post-final-norm centered activation covariance for any input distribution. They validate this on 14 pretrained transformers (9 LayerNorm, 5 RMSNorm; 160M-35B parameters) across language and vision objectives.
AI.result: At random initialization, the predicted direction matches the measured bottom singular direction to four decimal places on all 9 LayerNorm models and is correctly absent on all 5 RMSNorm models. After training, the covariance eigenvalue along this direction deepens by ~1000x. The residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block on 13/14 transformers.
AI.conclusion: The LayerNorm scale parameter alone reveals a dead direction in parameter space, providing the cheapest possible dead-direction read (specific to LayerNorm). This enables closed-form analysis of singular structure in residual streams and allows classification of a transformer's normalization type from parameters alone.

title: Token-Operations-Oriented Inference Optimization Techniques for Large Models
summary: Large model inference optimization serves as a key foundation for supporting the scalable, low-cost, and highly stable operation of large model services. Centered on token-oriented inference optimization technology, this paper proposes for the first time a four-layer technical architecture consisting of Multi-model Fusion, Model Optimization, Compute-Model Fusion, and Compute-Network-Model Fusion. It systematically reviews the key technologies and current industry status across these four levels and analyzes the application value of related technologies in real-world business scenarios. This paper provides a practical technical path for reducing token production costs, improving token service efficiency, ensuring the stability of token supply, and driving the transition of large model services from being merely callable to being operable.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a four-layer technical architecture (Multi-model Fusion, Model Optimization, Compute-Model Fusion, Compute-Network-Model Fusion) for token-oriented large model inference optimization, systematically reviews key technologies and industry status, and demonstrates their value in reducing token costs, improving service efficiency, ensuring supply stability, and transitioning large model services from callable to operable.
AI.motivation: To address the need for scalable, low-cost, and highly stable large model services by providing a systematic technical path for inference optimization centered on token-oriented efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes a novel four-layer technical architecture (Multi-model Fusion, Model Optimization, Compute-Model Fusion, Compute-Network-Model Fusion) and systematically reviews key technologies and industry practices across these layers.
AI.result: Establishes a structured framework for token-centric inference optimization, identifies current industry status and key technologies at each of the four layers, and validates application value in real-world business scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture provides a practical path to reduce token production costs, improve token service efficiency, ensure stable token supply, and advance large model services from merely callable to fully operable.

title: Linear Recurrent Unit with Semantic Modulation for Image Super-Resolution
summary: Linear recurrent unit (LRU), designed with a principled formulation for stable linear recurrence, has demonstrated promising accuracy and robustness on long-range dependency tasks. However, its static parameterization and single-scan method limits its applicability to 2D vision tasks. In this study, we propose a LRU-based restoration network with a semantic modulating unit (SMU) to achieve a harmonious balance between performance and efficiency in single-image super-resolution. The SMU plays three key roles: LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement through learned prototype. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method quantitatively and qualitatively surpasses recent state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our approach achieves superior performance with computational complexity on par with existing methods. The source code and models are available at https://github.com/MingyuChoi-run/LSM

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Linear Recurrent Unit (LRU)-based super-resolution network enhanced with a Semantic Modulating Unit (SMU) that handles LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement, achieving state-of-the-art performance on single-image super-resolution with computational efficiency comparable to existing methods.
AI.motivation: LRU shows promise for long-range dependency tasks but its static parameterization and single-scan method limit its applicability to 2D vision tasks like image super-resolution. The authors aim to bridge this gap.
AI.method: The method proposes an LRU-based restoration network with a Semantic Modulating Unit (SMU) that performs three functions: (1) LRU modulation to adapt the recurrent dynamics, (2) spatial categorization to handle 2D spatial structure, and (3) feature enhancement via learned prototypes.
AI.result: The proposed method quantitatively and qualitatively surpasses recent state-of-the-art methods in single-image super-resolution, achieving superior performance with computational complexity on par with existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The LRU-based network with SMU achieves a harmonious balance between performance and efficiency, demonstrating the viability of recurrent architectures for 2D vision tasks like super-resolution.

title: CombEval: A Framework for Evaluating Combinatorial Counting in Large Language Models
summary: We present CombEval, a dynamic benchmark for evaluating combinatorial counting in large language models. CombEval represents each problem as a typed Cofola specification over entities, combinatorial objects, object dependencies, and constraints, enabling controlled generation of natural-language counting problems with exact solver-verified answers. Unlike static collections, CombEval supports systematic variation of object type, entity scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth. We evaluate 11 LLMs under direct and code-augmented settings and find that models remain brittle on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, relatively positional constraints, and nested object dependencies. Error analysis further identifies failures in constraint interpretation and counting principles. CombEval provides a diagnostic testbed for studying when and why LLMs fail at combinatorial reasoning. The code and generated benchmark suites are publicly available at \url{https://github.com/YuxuZhou-CN/combination-problem-generation}.

AI.tldr: CombEval is a dynamic benchmark for evaluating combinatorial counting in LLMs using typed Cofola specifications, revealing that models struggle with ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, relative positional constraints, and nested dependencies.
AI.motivation: Existing static benchmarks are insufficient for diagnosing LLM failures in combinatorial reasoning because they lack controlled variation of problem dimensions such as object type, entity scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth.
AI.method: CombEval represents each problem as a typed Cofola specification over entities, combinatorial objects, object dependencies, and constraints, enabling systematic generation of natural-language counting problems with exact solver-verified answers.
AI.result: 11 LLMs were evaluated under direct and code-augmented settings; models exhibit brittleness on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, relative positional constraints, and nested object dependencies. Error analysis reveals failures in constraint interpretation and counting principles.
AI.conclusion: CombEval serves as a diagnostic testbed for studying when and why LLMs fail at combinatorial reasoning, highlighting persistent weaknesses even with code augmentation.

title: Calibrating Generative Models to Feature Distributions with MMD Finetuning
summary: Generative models can produce individually plausible samples while deviating substantially from a target set in the distribution of key features. For example, a model pretrained on broad drug-like chemical space may generate molecules whose molecular features differ from those of a therapeutic class of interest, such as known antibiotics. Correcting such distributional miscalibration is challenging: direct finetuning on the target set can overfit and does not control which features are matched. To fill this gap, we introduce kernel Calibrating Generative Models (kCGM). kCGM minimizes a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between generated and target feature distributions using an unbiased score-function estimator, with KL regularization to remain close to the pretrained model. On a target set of 174 antibiotics, direct finetuning sacrifices chemical validity for feature-distribution matching, whereas kCGM improves target feature matching while increasing validity. We further demonstrate kCGM in protein and DNA generation tasks, showing it can adapt autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models using only feature-level supervision. Code is available at https://github.com/smithhenryd/cgm.

AI.tldr: kCGM is a method that uses maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) with KL regularization to calibrate generative models' feature distributions toward a target set, outperforming direct finetuning on tasks like drug, protein, and DNA generation.
AI.motivation: Generative models can produce plausible samples but deviate from target feature distributions (e.g., pretrained drug models generating molecules unlike known antibiotics). Direct finetuning overfits and cannot control which features are matched.
AI.method: Introduces kernel Calibrating Generative Models (kCGM), which minimizes MMD between generated and target feature distributions using an unbiased score-function estimator with KL regularization to stay close to the pretrained model.
AI.result: On a 174-antibiotic target set, direct finetuning sacrifices chemical validity for feature matching, whereas kCGM improves target feature matching while increasing validity. Also demonstrated on protein and DNA generation tasks across autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models.
AI.conclusion: kCGM effectively corrects distributional miscalibration in generative models using only feature-level supervision, generalizing across model types and domains without overfitting.

title: A Model-Driven Approach for Developing Families of Reinforcement Learning Environments
summary: Virtual training environments are software-intensive systems in which reinforcement learning (RL) agents learn, adapt, and demonstrate meaningful behavior. Virtual training environments offer a safe and cost-efficient alternative to training agents in real-world settings. However, to converge, most realistic RL problems require training in multiple, mostly similar but slightly different environments - i.e., families of environment variants. The typical development process of environment families is a labor-intensive and error-prone manual endeavor that does not scale well. To alleviate these issues, in this paper, we propose a model-driven approach for developing families of RL training environments. To obtain the family of environments, we develop an approach and prototype tool. In our approach, a hybrid genetic algorithm - a combination of population-based global search and heuristic local search - generates environment families. Mutations and constraints are expressed as model transformations and are operationalized into a search process by a state-of-the-art model transformation engine. We demonstrate the soundness of our approach in a wildfire mitigation scenario and curriculum learning - a particular learning paradigm that relies on environment families.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a model-driven approach with a hybrid genetic algorithm to automatically generate families of reinforcement learning training environments, reducing manual effort in developing environment variants needed for curriculum learning.
AI.motivation: Developing families of environment variants for RL training is labor-intensive, error-prone, and does not scale well, yet most realistic RL problems require training across multiple similar but slightly different environments to converge.
AI.method: A model-driven approach using a hybrid genetic algorithm combining population-based global search with heuristic local search. Mutations and constraints are expressed as model transformations and operationalized via a state-of-the-art model transformation engine.
AI.result: The approach demonstrated soundness in a wildfire mitigation scenario and curriculum learning paradigm, showing feasibility for generating environment families automatically.
AI.conclusion: The model-driven approach effectively alleviates scalability issues in developing RL training environment families, with demonstrated applicability to curriculum learning.

title: Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder for Endoscopic Videos
summary:
Endoscopic video analysis is essential for gastrointestinal diagnosis and computer-assisted interventions, but video sequences are routinely degraded by specular reflections, motion artifacts, and missing frames. These transient corruptions can distract clinicians, reduce image interpretability, and disrupt downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction and navigation. Effective restoration therefore requires methods that exploit temporal continuity rather than treating frames in isolation. We introduce a Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder (GPVAE) framework for endoscopic video restoration that replaces the standard factorized latent prior with a temporal Gaussian process prior, enabling interpolation of missing frames with uncertainty-aware reconstruction.
  The framework combines endoscopy-specific encoders, including a convolutional EndoVAE backbone and pretrained Vision Transformer encoders from GastroNet-5M, with two scalable GP approximations: Hierarchical Prior Approximation (HPA) and Sparse Precision Approximation (SPA). Specular reflections are handled using a DUCKNet-based masking pipeline that excludes corrupted pixels from the reconstruction objective. On the C3VDv2 colonoscopy dataset, the best GPVAE variants reduced image reconstruction RMSE by 21.9\% on average, and by up to 26.1\%, relative to matched VAE baselines. Downstream trajectory RMSE was reduced by 12.7\% on average across classical visual odometry and a pretrained PoseNet, at an average increase of 27.3\% in training time per epoch. Finally, the GP posterior provides per-frame uncertainty estimates that reflect temporal support and offer a confidence signal for restored frames.

AI.tldr: A Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder (GPVAE) framework for endoscopic video restoration that replaces standard factorized latent priors with temporal Gaussian process priors, achieving significant improvements in image reconstruction (up to 26.1% RMSE reduction) and downstream trajectory estimation (12.7% RMSE reduction) while providing uncertainty estimates, using DUCKNet-based masking for specular reflections and two scalable GP approximations (HPA and SPA).
AI.motivation: Endoscopic video sequences are routinely degraded by specular reflections, motion artifacts, and missing frames, which distract clinicians and disrupt downstream tasks. Existing methods treat frames in isolation rather than exploiting temporal continuity, motivating a restoration framework that models temporal dependencies with uncertainty-aware reconstruction.
AI.method: The framework introduces a GPVAE that replaces the standard factorized latent prior with a temporal Gaussian process prior. It combines convolutional EndoVAE backbone and pretrained Vision Transformer encoders from GastroNet-5M, uses two scalable GP approximations (HPA and SPA), and handles specular reflections via a DUCKNet-based masking pipeline that excludes corrupted pixels from the reconstruction objective.
AI.result: On C3VDv2 colonoscopy dataset, best GPVAE variants reduced image reconstruction RMSE by 21.9% on average and up to 26.1% relative to VAE baselines. Downstream trajectory RMSE reduced by 12.7% on average across visual odometry and PoseNet, with 27.3% increase in training time per epoch.
AI.conclusion: The GPVAE framework effectively restores endoscopy video by modeling temporal dependencies, outperforming frame-wise methods in reconstruction and downstream tasks, and provides per-frame uncertainty estimates that reflect temporal support as confidence signals for restored frames.

title: Think Again or Think Longer? Selective Verification for Budget-Aware Reasoning
summary: Test-time reasoning is increasingly used as a serving-time control knob, but extra reasoning is not uniformly valuable: it can repair failed attempts, waste compute on already-correct answers, or introduce harmful answer changes. We study this as a deployment allocation problem rather than a new-verifier problem. We introduce \sevra, Selective Verification for Reasoning Allocation, a serving-layer controller that decides whether to preserve a frozen solver's initial answer or invoke active verification. Using a frozen Qwen3-4B solver, we log intervention outcomes and train recoverability-aware gates from serving-visible attempt state. On \mathfive, selective verification reaches 76.3\% accuracy, compared with 75.5\% for always verifying, while reducing post-generation tokens by 26.8\% and harmful flips from 2.2\% to 1.0\%. However, an 8,192-token initial solve reaches 76.0\% accuracy with 28\% fewer total model tokens, showing that selective recovery is useful but not the best tested cost frontier. In frozen transfer to \gsm, the selective policy verifies only 3.0\% of examples, improves accuracy from 93.4\% to 94.5\%, and reduces verification tokens by 91.2\% relative to always verifying; again, a longer initial solve matches its accuracy with fewer realized tokens. On CommonsenseQA, always-on verification hurts, while Self-Consistency@5 improves accuracy at about five times the realized token cost. The resulting deployment rule is: tune the initial budget first, then use selective recovery when explicit checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression-risk control matter.

AI.tldr: SEVRA selectively decides when to verify a frozen solver's initial answer, reducing compute waste and harmful answer flips compared to always-on verification; however, investing more tokens in the initial solve often achieves better accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: Extra test-time reasoning is not uniformly valuable—it can repair failures, waste compute on already-correct answers, or introduce harmful changes. The problem is framed as a deployment allocation problem rather than designing a new verifier.
AI.method: SEVRA (Selective Verification for Reasoning Allocation) is a serving-layer controller that decides whether to preserve a frozen solver's initial answer or invoke active verification, using recoverability-aware gates trained from serving-visible attempt state.
AI.result: On MATH500, selective verification reaches 76.3% accuracy (vs. 75.5% for always verifying), reduces post-generation tokens by 26.8% and harmful flips from 2.2% to 1.0%. On GSM8K, it verifies only 3.0% of examples, improving accuracy from 93.4% to 94.5%, reducing verification tokens by 91.2%.
AI.conclusion: The deployment rule is: tune the initial budget first, then use selective recovery when explicit checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression-risk control matter.

title: Interactive Pareto navigation for deep multi-task learning
summary: In multi-task learning, handling an increasing number of objectives can quickly become challenging, both in terms of the computational resources and the decision maker's capacity to choose appropriate trade-offs. A widely used approach is thus to aggregate the individual losses in a single loss function by a weighted sum. This often fails to capture either the decision maker's preferences as a result of the shape of the Pareto front, or requires multiple adjustments and computations which becomes prohibitively expensive in deep learning applications. To address these issues, we introduce a novel framework, Preference Pareto Exploration (PPE), which enforces the decision maker's preferences while accounting for the geometry of the Pareto set in an interactive exploration process. PPE is based on a predictor-corrector method that performs predictor steps tangential to the manifold of Pareto-optimal solutions, following the decision maker's preference. The subsequent corrector step results in a new trade-off reflecting this preference. To avoid explicit Hessian computations when characterizing the tangent space of the manifold, we employ a Krylov subspace method that relies solely on matrix-vector products. These products can be efficiently obtained via automatic differentiation, ensuring both efficiency and robustness throughout the optimization process. The method's functionality and performance are demonstrated using both toy problems and examples from deep learning.

AI.tldr: PPE is a predictor-corrector framework that enables interactive Pareto front exploration in multi-task deep learning by enforcing decision maker preferences while using Krylov subspace methods to avoid expensive Hessian computations.
AI.motivation: Traditional weighted-sum aggregation in multi-task learning fails to capture decision maker preferences due to Pareto front geometry, and requires prohibitively expensive adjustments in deep learning applications.
AI.method: PPE uses a predictor-corrector approach: predictor steps move tangentially along the Pareto-optimal manifold following the decision maker's preference, then corrector steps project to a new trade-off. The tangent space is characterized via a Krylov subspace method using only matrix-vector products from automatic differentiation, avoiding explicit Hessian computations.
AI.result: The method's functionality and performance are demonstrated on both toy problems and deep learning examples.
AI.conclusion: PPE provides an efficient and robust interactive exploration framework that respects decision maker preferences and Pareto geometry without the computational burden of Hessian-based methods.

title: AutoPass: Evidence-Guided LLM Agents for Compiler Performance Tuning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise for code compilation tasks, but applying them to runtime performance tuning is difficult due to complex microarchitectural effects and noisy runtime measurements. We present AutoPass, a multi-agent framework for compiler performance tuning that uses compiler and runtime evidence to guide LLM-generated optimization decisions. Rather than treating the compiler as a black box like prior auto-tuning schemes, AutoPass opens up the compiler to the LLM, enabling it to query compiler-internal optimization states and analyze the intermediate representation to orchestrate compiler options. The search process iteratively refines optimization configurations using measured runtime feedback to diagnose regressions and guide latency-improving edits. AutoPass operates in an inference-only, training-free setting and requires no offline training or task-specific fine-tuning, making it readily applicable to new benchmarks and platforms. We implement AutoPass on the LLVM compiler and evaluate it on server-grade x86-64 and embedded ARM64 systems. AutoPass outperforms expert-tuned heuristics and classical autotuning methods, achieving geometric-mean speedups of 1.043x and 1.117x over LLVM -O3 on x86-64 and ARM64, respectively.
