AI.tldr: AutoPass is a multi-agent framework that uses LLMs to guide compiler optimization decisions by querying compiler-internal states and runtime feedback, achieving up to 1.117x speedup over LLVM -O3 without training or fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: LLMs show promise for code tasks but struggle with runtime performance tuning due to complex microarchitectural effects and noisy measurements. Prior auto-tuning treats the compiler as a black box, missing opportunities for informed optimization.
AI.method: A multi-agent framework that opens the compiler to the LLM, enabling queries of compiler-internal optimization states and IR analysis. The search iteratively refines configurations using runtime feedback to diagnose regressions and guide latency-improving edits, operating inference-only with no training required.
AI.result: Achieved geometric-mean speedups of 1.043x on x86-64 and 1.117x on ARM64 over LLVM -O3, outperforming expert-tuned heuristics and classical autotuning methods.
AI.conclusion: AutoPass demonstrates that exposing compiler internals to LLMs enables effective compiler performance tuning offline without training or fine-tuning, and generalizes readily to new benchmarks and platforms.

title: SpatialSV: Internalizing Interpretable 3D Spatial Awareness in MLLMs via Task-Oriented Visual Supervision
summary: Unlocking the spatial intelligence of multimodal large language model (MLLMs) is crucial for understanding and interacting with the 3D world. Prevailing approaches typically inject spatial priors via external tools, which impose significant inference overhead, or rely on latent feature distillation, which remains uninterpretable and lacks fine-grained geometric constraints. To address these issues, we propose SpatialSV, a framework designed to internalize robust 3D spatial awareness within MLLMs while simultaneously offering inherent interpretability. Deviating from passive feature imitation, SpatialSV employs task-oriented visual supervision, compelling the model to actively lift its 2D visual features into explicit 3D representations, including depth maps, camera poses, and point clouds. Crucially, this 2D-to-3D lifting process provides a transparent window into the model's representations: the resulting 3D reconstructions serve as an intuitive proxy for visualizing and diagnosing the quality of the model's intrinsic spatial knowledge. Extensive experiments across multiple models and benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of SpatialSV in enhancing and interpreting MLLMs' spatial intelligence. Furthermore, the framework exhibits strong generalization in semi-supervised settings, validating its potential to leverage unlabeled visual data for scalable, interpretable spatial representation learning.

AI.tldr: SpatialSV is a framework that enhances multimodal large language models' (MLLMs) 3D spatial intelligence by using task-oriented visual supervision to lift 2D features into explicit 3D representations (depth maps, camera poses, point clouds), offering both improved performance and inherent interpretability through the 3D reconstructions as diagnostic proxies.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for injecting 3D spatial priors into MLLMs rely on either external tools (high inference overhead) or latent feature distillation (uninterpretable, lacking fine-grained geometric constraints). The goal is to internalize robust 3D spatial awareness while maintaining interpretability.
AI.method: SpatialSV employs task-oriented visual supervision to compel the model to actively lift 2D visual features into explicit 3D representations, specifically depth maps, camera poses, and point clouds, instead of passive feature imitation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple models and benchmarks demonstrate effectiveness in enhancing and interpreting MLLMs' spatial intelligence. The framework also shows strong generalization in semi-supervised settings, leveraging unlabeled visual data.
AI.conclusion: The 2D-to-3D lifting process provides a transparent window into model representations, where 3D reconstructions serve as intuitive proxies for visualizing and diagnosing the quality of intrinsic spatial knowledge. The framework enables scalable, interpretable spatial representation learning.

title: Human-on-the-Loop Orchestration for AI-Assisted Legal Discovery
summary: Autonomous Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed in electronic discovery (e-discovery), where compounding errors across multi-step reasoning chains can constitute legal malpractice. Unlike single-turn retrieval, agentic workflows operating over privileged document corpora exhibit a class of failure we term "trajectory collapse": an early misclassification silently propagates, rendering an entire privilege review invalid. This paper makes three contributions. First, we propose a structured taxonomy of agentic failures in legal information retrieval, organized by functional stage. Second, we introduce a four-layer verification architecture -- spanning planning, reasoning, execution, and uncertainty quantification -- designed to intercept these failures before they compound. Third, we present a preliminary simulation study on a synthetic e-discovery corpus that demonstrates how mandatory Human-on-the-Loop (HOTL) escalation thresholds reduce privilege-waiver risk relative to fully autonomous baselines. Our results suggest that calibrated uncertainty thresholds can reduce privilege-waiver risk by up to 61% versus fully autonomous deployment, while routing fewer than one quarter of documents to attorney review.

AI.tldr: LLM agents in e-discovery exhibit "trajectory collapse" failures where early misclassifications compound. The paper proposes a failure taxonomy, a four-layer verification architecture, and shows via simulation that Human-on-the-Loop (HOTL) escalation thresholds reduce privilege-waiver risk by up to 61% while routing <25% of documents to attorney review.
AI.motivation: Autonomous LLM agents deployed in e-discovery face compounding errors across multi-step reasoning chains that can constitute legal malpractice, yet existing systems lack safeguards against "trajectory collapse" where early misclassifications silently propagate and invalidate entire privilege reviews.
AI.method: (1) A structured taxonomy of agentic failures in legal information retrieval organized by functional stage; (2) a four-layer verification architecture spanning planning, reasoning, execution, and uncertainty quantification; (3) a preliminary simulation study on a synthetic e-discovery corpus comparing mandatory Human-on-the-Loop (HOTL) escalation thresholds against fully autonomous baselines.
AI.result: Calibrated uncertainty thresholds reduce privilege-waiver risk by up to 61% versus fully autonomous deployment, while routing fewer than one quarter of documents to attorney review.
AI.conclusion: Human-on-the-Loop escalation with calibrated uncertainty thresholds offers a practical mechanism to mitigate trajectory collapse risks in agentic e-discovery workflows, balancing risk reduction against human review burden.

title: Techniques for Peak Memory Reduction for LoRA Fine-tuning of LLMs on Edge Devices
comment: Hassan Dbouk and Matthias Reisser contributed equally to this work
summary: Fine-tuning of Large Language Models (LLMs) using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on an end-user's data offers personalized experiences while keeping data private, but faces severe memory constraints on consumer hardware. Peak memory during fine-tuning often exceeds device limits, especially for models with billions of parameters and long-context training data. This paper introduces a suite of complementary techniques to reduce memory footprint without sacrificing model quality: (1) base model quantization with on-the-fly dequantization, (2) memory-efficient checkpointing combining selective activation caching and disk offloading, (3) softmax approximation using semantically relevant token subsets, and (4) logits masking. Experiments on Llama-3.2 3B and Qwen-2.5 3B demonstrate up to $26\times$ and $28\times$ reduction in peak memory, enabling fine-tuning on resource-constrained devices.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces four complementary techniques (base model quantization with on-the-fly dequantization, memory-efficient checkpointing with selective activation caching and disk offloading, softmax approximation using relevant token subsets, and logits masking) that reduce peak memory by up to 26-28x for LoRA fine-tuning of LLMs on consumer hardware without sacrificing model quality.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning LLMs with LoRA on end-user data enables personalization while preserving privacy, but faces severe memory constraints on consumer hardware, especially for billion-parameter models and long-context training data.
AI.method: The paper proposes a suite of four complementary techniques: (1) base model quantization with on-the-fly dequantization to reduce memory from weight storage, (2) memory-efficient checkpointing combining selective activation caching and disk offloading, (3) softmax approximation using semantically relevant token subsets, and (4) logits masking.
AI.result: Experiments on Llama-3.2 3B and Qwen-2.5 3B demonstrate up to 26× and 28× reduction in peak memory, enabling fine-tuning on resource-constrained devices.
AI.conclusion: The proposed techniques significantly reduce memory footprint for LoRA fine-tuning without sacrificing model quality, making personalized fine-tuning feasible on consumer hardware.

title: Probe-and-Refine Tuning of Repository Guidance for Coding Agents
summary: LLM-based coding agents need higher-level operational knowledge about a repository (which files house which subsystems, how to run the test suite, which workflows have historically led to wrong fixes) that does not exist in the code itself. Engineers typically maintain \texttt{AGENTS.md} files to supply this context as instructions for coding agents, but whether they help is contested: recent studies disagree on whether LLM-generated guidance improves or harms agent performance. In this paper we show that how the guidance is produced is the decisive variable, and introduce \emph{probe-and-refine tuning}: a procedure that uses synthetic bug-fix probes to iteratively diagnose and patch a repository's guidance file through single-shot LLM calls, with no agent loop or tool use during tuning. On SWE-bench Verified across four independent trials with Qwen3.5-35B-A3B at 200 steps, probe-and-refine achieves 33.0\,\% mean resolve rate vs.\ 28.3\,\% for the static knowledge base used to initialize it and 25.5\,\% for an unguided baseline ($p < 0.001$ for both probe-and-refine contrasts). The improvement comes from coverage rather than precision: refined guidance produces evaluable patches for 14.5 percentage points (pp) more instances while per-patch precision remains statistically constant ($\sim$59\,\%, $p = 0.119$), showing that improved guidance helps agents reach the correct file rather than improving the quality of the changes they make. Further, a step-budget experiment shows that guidance is what lets the agent use a larger step budget productively, and a cross-model experiment with NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B finds that the tuning loop degrades when the model cannot generate sufficiently diagnostic output, though per-patch precision remains constant even then.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces probe-and-refine tuning, a procedure that uses synthetic bug-fix probes to iteratively improve AGENTS.md guidance files for LLM-based coding agents. On SWE-bench Verified, this method achieves 33.0% resolve rate vs 28.3% for static knowledge base and 25.5% for unguided baseline. The improvement comes from increased coverage (14.5pp more instances patched) rather than precision (~59%, statistically constant).
AI.motivation: LLM-based coding agents lack higher-level operational knowledge about repositories (e.g., file structure, test procedures, historical failure patterns) that isn't in the code itself. Engineers maintain AGENTS.md files to provide this context, but prior studies disagree on whether LLM-generated guidance helps or harms performance. The paper aims to resolve this contradiction by showing that how guidance is produced is the decisive factor.
AI.method: The authors propose probe-and-refine tuning: a procedure that uses synthetic bug-fix probes to iteratively diagnose and patch a repository's guidance file through single-shot LLM calls, with no agent loop or tool use during tuning. The method involves generating guidance, probing it with synthetic bugs, diagnosing failures, and refining the guidance accordingly.
AI.result: Probe-and-refine achieves 33.0% mean resolve rate vs 28.3% for static knowledge base and 25.5% for unguided baseline (p < 0.001). Improvement comes from coverage (14.5pp more instances patched) rather than precision (~59%, p=0.119). A step-budget experiment shows guidance enables productive use of larger step budgets. A cross-model experiment with NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B shows the tuning loop degrades with less capable models, though per-patch precision remains constant.
AI.conclusion: The way guidance is produced is decisive for coding agent performance. Probe-and-refine tuning significantly improves resolve rates through increased coverage (helping agents reach the correct files) rather than improving patch quality. The approach works with capable models but degrades when the model cannot generate sufficiently diagnostic output.

title: CARE: Competence-Aware Reward Shaping for Adaptive Reasoning Length in Video-MLLMs
summary: In multimodal video reasoning, reinforcement learning-based methods typically rely on simplistic and inflexible reasoning-length control strategies that fail to adapt to the model's evolving competence. This mismatch may suppress necessary exploration at early stages, while encouraging redundant reasoning and inefficient decoding once the model becomes more competent. In this paper, we propose CARE, a competence-aware reward shaping framework for adaptive reasoning length optimization in multimodal reasoning. Specifically, CARE maintains a smoothed competence estimate via an exponential moving average of pass rates, and uses it to route training into progressive stages that shift the reward preference from exploration-oriented long-form reasoning to efficiency-oriented concise reasoning. To avoid conflating verbosity with intrinsic task complexity, CARE further normalizes reasoning effort with batch-level statistics, and introduces a posterior amplifier to strengthen reward signals for unexpectedly strong performance on historically difficult samples. The proposed mechanism is seamlessly integrated into the GRPO training pipeline and incurs no additional inference-time overhead. Extensive experiments on multiple video reasoning and general video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that CARE consistently improves reasoning accuracy, stabilizes reinforcement learning, and significantly enhances token efficiency. Moreover, CARE exhibits a characteristic inverted-U trajectory of reasoning length during training, and yields shorter yet more informative reasoning traces at convergence, indicating effective adaptive allocation of reasoning budget. We provide the source code for our proposed CARE framework and experiments at https://github.com/1Pansy/Video-CARE.

AI.tldr: CARE is a competence-aware reward shaping framework for adaptive reasoning length optimization in multimodal video reasoning. It uses an exponential moving average of pass rates to progressively shift training from exploration-oriented long-form reasoning to efficiency-oriented concise reasoning, improving accuracy, token efficiency, and RL stability without additional inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-based methods for multimodal video reasoning use simplistic and inflexible reasoning-length control strategies that fail to adapt to the model's evolving competence, suppressing necessary exploration early on while encouraging redundant reasoning and inefficient decoding as the model becomes more competent.
AI.method: CARE (1) maintains a smoothed competence estimate via exponential moving average of pass rates to route training into progressive stages; (2) normalizes reasoning effort with batch-level statistics; (3) introduces a posterior amplifier to strengthen reward signals for unexpectedly strong performance on historically difficult samples; (4) integrates seamlessly into GRPO training pipeline without additional inference-time overhead.
AI.result: CARE consistently improves reasoning accuracy, stabilizes reinforcement learning, and significantly enhances token efficiency on multiple video reasoning and general video understanding benchmarks. It exhibits an inverted-U trajectory of reasoning length during training, yielding shorter yet more informative reasoning traces at convergence.
AI.conclusion: CARE enables effective adaptive allocation of reasoning budget, outperforming prior methods in accuracy and efficiency for multimodal video reasoning tasks.

authors: V V N K Someswara Rao Koduri
title: TelcoAgent: A Scalable 5G Multi-KPM Forecasting With 3GPP-Grounded Explainability
comment: 6 pages, 6 figures. Submitted to IEEE GLOBECOM 2026
summary: Key Performance Measurement (KPM) forecasting is essential for proactive network management of 5G and next-generation telecom networks. However, existing machine learning (ML) approaches face significant limitations in scalability and explainability, restricting their effectiveness in real-world deployments. We propose TelcoAgent, a foundation model-based framework that enables accurate, scalable, and explainable forecasting of multiple KPMs across diverse network cells without the need for site-specific training. Specifically, the framework comprises three key components: (i) an automated three-agent pipeline that constructs a 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) knowledge graph directly from specification documents, (ii) a scalable, time-series foundation model (TSFM)-based prediction pipeline to deliver accurate, zero-shot forecasting, and finally (iii) a reasoning and explanation pipeline that provides actionable, domain-grounded diagnostics. Evaluated using a 3-month, real-world, city-scale 5G KPM dataset from a U.S.-based network operator, TelcoAgent demonstrates high forecasting accuracy for all 7 considered KPMs per cell across 200 cells, while delivering explainable insights and actionable instructions to address network degradations.

AI.tldr: TelcoAgent is a foundation model-based framework for accurate, scalable, and explainable KPM forecasting in 5G networks, using a 3GPP knowledge graph, a time-series foundation model for zero-shot prediction, and a reasoning pipeline for actionable diagnostics, tested on real-world city-scale data from 200 cells across 7 KPMs.
AI.motivation: Existing ML approaches for KPM forecasting in 5G/next-generation networks suffer from scalability and explainability limitations, hindering real-world deployment.
AI.method: TelcoAgent consists of three components: (i) an automated three-agent pipeline to build a 3GPP knowledge graph from specification documents, (ii) a zero-shot TSFM-based prediction pipeline for scalable forecasting, and (iii) a reasoning/explanation pipeline providing domain-grounded diagnostics and actionable instructions.
AI.result: Evaluated on a 3-month, real-world, city-scale 5G dataset from a U.S. operator across 200 cells and 7 KPMs, TelcoAgent achieved high forecasting accuracy with explainable insights.
AI.conclusion: TelcoAgent demonstrates that foundation models can deliver accurate, scalable, and explainable zero-shot forecasting for multi-cell KPMs without site-specific training, with actionable diagnostics for network management.

title: Tracking Representation Dynamics in Large Language Models with Persistent Homology
summary: Large language models are commonly aligned through supervised fine-tuning, yet little is known about how their internal representations evolve during this process. We study alignment dynamics using persistent homology by tracking the topology of activation spaces throughout fine-tuning. Across four transformer language models ranging from 1B to 7B parameters and three alignment objectives corresponding to helpful, harmless, and mixed training data, we find that the majority of topological reorganization occurs during the earliest stages of training. A dense checkpoint analysis reveals a transient peak in topological activity followed by rapid stabilization. We further show that different alignment objectives induce distinguishable topological trajectories, while instruction-tuned and pretrained models exhibit qualitatively different patterns of evolution. Our results suggest that persistent homology provides a complementary perspective on alignment, revealing representation-level changes that are not apparent from behavioral metrics alone.

AI.tldr: Using persistent homology, the paper analyzes how language models' internal activation spaces reorganize during alignment fine-tuning, finding that most topological changes happen early in training with distinguishable trajectories across different alignment objectives.
AI.motivation: Little is known about how internal representations evolve during supervised fine-tuning for alignment; the authors aim to study these dynamics using topological data analysis (persistent homology) to reveal changes not captured by behavioral metrics alone.
AI.method: Applied persistent homology to track topology of activation spaces across four transformer LMs (1B–7B params) during fine-tuning with three alignment objectives (helpful, harmless, mixed). Dense checkpoint analysis was used to capture temporal dynamics.
AI.result: Majority of topological reorganization occurs in earliest training stages; a transient peak in topological activity appears followed by rapid stabilization. Different alignment objectives induce distinguishable topological trajectories. Instruction-tuned vs. pretrained models show qualitatively different evolution patterns.
AI.conclusion: Persistent homology offers a complementary perspective on alignment, revealing representation-level changes invisible to behavioral metrics. Topological dynamics during alignment are highly concentrated at early stages and sensitive to alignment objective.

title: Spatial-Aware Reduction Framework: Towards Efficient and Faithful Visual State Space Models
summary: Mamba demonstrates strong efficiency in modeling long visual sequences. However, when token reduction is applied to structurally enhanced Mamba variants, these models exhibit a severe performance collapse. We attribute this degradation to the spatially agnostic nature of existing reduction methods, which violate the two-dimensional structural premise required by the selective scanning mechanism. In this work, we propose STORM, a spatial-aware token reduction framework designed to maintain structural integrity throughout the compression process. STORM reformulates reduction into a structured operation on spatial units, enforcing localized constraints to maintain both grid topology and neighborhood coherence. As a plug-and-play module, STORM equips existing reduction pipelines with explicit spatial awareness without any training. Empirical results demonstrate that STORM achieves state-of-the-art pruning accuracy across diverse vision Mamba backbones under training-free settings. Notably, STORM delivers a substantial accuracy recovery on VMamba, outperforming prior methods by up to 63.3\% in top-1 accuracy. Meanwhile, STORM incurs only a 1.0\% accuracy drop on PlainMamba, achieving performance comparable to ViT.

AI.tldr: STORM is a training-free, plug-and-play token reduction framework that preserves spatial structure in vision Mamba models, preventing the performance collapse caused by existing spatially agnostic reduction methods. It achieves state-of-the-art pruning accuracy, recovering up to 63.3% top-1 accuracy on VMamba with only 1.0% accuracy drop on PlainMamba.
AI.motivation: Existing token reduction methods cause severe performance collapse in structurally enhanced vision Mamba models because they are spatially agnostic and violate the 2D structural premise required by the selective scanning mechanism.
AI.method: STORM reformulates token reduction into a structured operation on spatial units, enforcing localized constraints to maintain grid topology and neighborhood coherence. It is a plug-and-play, training-free module.
AI.result: STORM achieves state-of-the-art pruning accuracy across diverse vision Mamba backbones under training-free settings, with up to 63.3% top-1 accuracy recovery on VMamba and only 1.0% accuracy drop on PlainMamba.
AI.conclusion: Spatial-aware token reduction via localized structural constraints effectively resolves the performance collapse in vision Mamba models, achieving ViT-comparable performance without training.

title: A Systematic Evaluation of Black-Box Uncertainty Estimation Methods for Large Language Models
summary: Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong capabilities across a wide range of tasks, their outputs often remain unreliable and may contain hallucinations, making uncertainty estimation (UE) essential for building trustworthy LLMs. In practice, many mainstream LLMs are only accessible through restricted APIs, where internal signals such as logits and hidden states are unavailable, making black-box UE especially important. However, existing work on black-box UE for LLMs remains fragmented in methodology and lacks a unified empirical comparison. To address this gap, we present a systematic review of black-box UE methods and organize them into five categories: verbalization-based, sampling-based, explanation-based, multi-agent, and hybrid methods. We further build a unified evaluation framework and benchmark 24 representative methods across 4 models and 4 dataset settings. Our results show that no single method consistently dominates across all settings. Nevertheless, methods that reason over and compare candidates in the answer space are generally effective, and hybrid methods that combine multiple uncertainty signals perform well under most conditions. By releasing the benchmark data and a unified evaluation framework, we aim to facilitate reproducible comparisons and support future research, while our empirical findings provide practical guidance for developing future black-box UE methods for LLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a systematic review and unified benchmark of black-box uncertainty estimation (UE) methods for LLMs, categorizing them into five types (verbalization-based, sampling-based, explanation-based, multi-agent, hybrid). Through benchmarking 24 methods across 4 models and 4 dataset settings, they find no single method dominates all settings, but answer-space comparison and hybrid methods perform well generally.
AI.motivation: LLM outputs are often unreliable and contain hallucinations, making uncertainty estimation essential for trustworthiness. Many mainstream LLMs are only accessible via restricted APIs without internal signals (logits, hidden states), making black-box UE crucial. However, existing black-box UE research is fragmented methodologically and lacks unified empirical comparison.
AI.method: Systematic literature review organizing black-box UE methods into five categories: verbalization-based, sampling-based, explanation-based, multi-agent, and hybrid methods. Built a unified evaluation framework and benchmarked 24 representative methods across 4 models and 4 dataset settings.
AI.result: No single method consistently dominates across all settings. Methods that reason over and compare candidates in the answer space are generally effective. Hybrid methods combining multiple uncertainty signals perform well under most conditions.
AI.conclusion: Answer-space comparison methods and hybrid approaches are practically effective for black-box UE. The paper releases benchmark data and a unified evaluation framework to facilitate reproducible comparisons and guide future research on black-box UE for LLMs.

title: Predicting Mergeability of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Updates
summary: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) makes it cheap to train many domain- and task-specific language model adapters, but whether two adapters can be merged is usually discovered only after both have been fully trained and evaluated. This late feedback is costly: adapters that are strong in isolation can interfere destructively once their updates are combined. We ask whether this outcome can be anticipated. We formalize adapter mergeability as the degree to which an adapter preserves its single-task utility after merging, and show that it can be forecast from signals measured in the first few percent of training -- chiefly how the low-rank updates and their gradients align across tasks and how much they disturb shared representations. We package these signals into MergeProbe, a lightweight predictor that estimates pairwise and set-level retention and turns the estimate into a concrete decision: merge directly, reweight, prune, or route. On MERGE-PEFT, a five-domain benchmark spanning math, code, science, instruction following, and safety, MergeProbe attains the best average and worst-case retention among strong interference-aware merge baselines while adding far less deployment overhead than full task routing. This turns LoRA merging from a post-hoc engineering step into an anticipatory measurement problem.

AI.tldr: MergeProbe is a lightweight predictor that forecasts whether LoRA adapters can be successfully merged by analyzing signals from the first few percent of training (low-rank update alignment, gradient alignment, and representation disturbance), achieving strong retention on a five-domain benchmark with less overhead than task routing.
AI.motivation: Current LoRA merging is a post-hoc process where destructive interference between adapters is only discovered after full training and evaluation, making it costly to iterate. The authors ask whether mergeability can be anticipated early.
AI.method: MergeProbe formalizes mergeability as the degree an adapter preserves single-task utility after merging. It predicts this from early-training signals: (1) alignment of low-rank updates across tasks, (2) gradient alignment, and (3) disturbance to shared representations. It outputs a decision (merge directly, reweight, prune, or route) for pairwise and set-level retention.
AI.result: On MERGE-PEFT (a five-domain benchmark: math, code, science, instruction following, safety), MergeProbe achieves the best average and worst-case retention among interference-aware merge baselines, with far less deployment overhead than full task routing.
AI.conclusion: LoRA merging can be transformed from a post-hoc engineering step into an anticipatory measurement problem, enabling early forecasting of adapter compatibility before full training completes.

title: Speeding up the annotation process in semantic segmentation industrial applications
summary: Current machine learning models commonly require large and well-annotated datasets. However, the annotation process often becomes a bottleneck, with increased complexity leading to higher chances of human errors. Within this context, our goal in this paper is to leverage unsupervised algorithms to improve data annotation efficiency for complex semantic segmentation problems in industrial materials science. Previous research has quantified labeling time and others explored unsupervised methods. However, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to quantify how much unsupervised algorithms accelerate the labeling process. We aim to validate the extent to which this laborious process can be accelerated, focusing on semantic segmentation tasks that involve annotating each pixel of high-resolution images, such as the microstructure characterization challenge in materials science. Specifically, we demonstrate that by using unsupervised computer vision algorithms, the time required for the labeling process can be reduced from 170 hours to 37 hours, achieving an approximate reduction of 78\%. The dataset we work with includes large images of dimensions 1280x959 and 960x703, which further increases the complexity of the annotation task. Despite these challenges, we create and share the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset to date, available under MIT License with permanent DOI, contributing a fully annotated, high-resolution dataset to the field. Additionally, this is the first work to compare the labeling time from scratch (a common approach in previous studies) to the labeling time when using these unsupervised algorithms as a pre-annotation step. Furthermore, we provide a Deep Learning model trained on this dataset, validated by field experts, and deployed in an industrial setting, serving as an initial benchmark for this public dataset.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that unsupervised algorithms can reduce pixel-level annotation time for semantic segmentation in materials science from 170 to 37 hours (78% reduction), while producing the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset.
AI.motivation: Data annotation is a bottleneck for machine learning models, especially for complex semantic segmentation tasks like pixel-level microstructure characterization in materials science, where human errors and labeling time are significant challenges.
AI.method: The authors employ unsupervised computer vision algorithms as a pre-annotation step to accelerate manual labeling, then compare labeling time from scratch versus labeling time using unsupervised pre-annotations. They create and publicly release a high-resolution steel microstructure segmentation dataset.
AI.result: Labeling time was reduced from 170 hours to 37 hours, achieving approximately 78% reduction. The dataset contains high-resolution images (1280x959 and 960x703) and constitutes the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset. A Deep Learning model trained on this data was validated and deployed industrially.
AI.conclusion: Unsupervised algorithms significantly accelerate the labeling process for complex semantic segmentation tasks; the study provides the first quantitative evidence of this acceleration and contributes a benchmark dataset and model to the field.

title: MetaResearcher: Scaling Deep Research via Self-Reflective Reinforcement Learning in Adversarial Virtual Environments
summary: Deep research agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in autonomous information gathering and synthesis, yet their training remains constrained by the static nature of simulated environments, the limits of fact-retrieval-only task designs, and the inefficiency of outcome-based reinforcement learning. In this work, we propose MetaResearcher, a novel framework that scales deep research agent training across four synergistic dimensions. First, we introduce an Evolving Virtual World that injects temporal dynamics and adversarial misinformation into the training environment, forcing agents to develop source credibility assessment and temporal conflict resolution skills. Second, we design Discovery-Oriented Tasks -- including hypothesis generation and contradiction resolution -- that transcend simple fact retrieval and push agents toward genuine research behaviors. Third, we propose a Self-Reflective Meta-Reward mechanism within the GRPO framework that jointly optimizes for answer correctness, search path efficiency, reflection depth, and tool call diversity, directly addressing the repetitive action loop problem observed in prior work. Fourth, we introduce a Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm architecture comprising specialized Scout, Filter, and Synthesizer models that learn collaborative research strategies through coordinated reinforcement learning. Built upon the LiteResearcher infrastructure, MetaResearcher requires zero marginal API cost for training while targeting substantial improvements in both benchmark performance (GAIA, Xbench-DS) and epistemic robustness under adversarial conditions. We present the complete framework design, training methodology, and planned experimental validation.

AI.tldr: MetaResearcher is a framework that scales deep research agent training across four dimensions: Evolving Virtual World (temporal dynamics + adversarial misinformation), Discovery-Oriented Tasks (hypothesis generation, contradiction resolution), Self-Reflective Meta-Reward mechanism within GRPO (optimizing correctness, efficiency, reflection depth, tool diversity), and Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm (Scout, Filter, Synthesizer models). Built on LiteResearcher, it requires zero marginal API cost for training and targets improvements on GAIA and Xbench-DS benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing deep research agents face three key limitations: (1) static simulated environments that lack temporal dynamics and adversarial challenges, (2) fact-retrieval-only task designs that fail to push agents toward genuine research behaviors, and (3) inefficiency of outcome-based reinforcement learning that leads to repetitive action loops.
AI.method: Proposes four synergistic dimensions: (1) Evolving Virtual World with temporal dynamics and adversarial misinformation for source credibility and conflict resolution training; (2) Discovery-Oriented Tasks including hypothesis generation and contradiction resolution; (3) Self-Reflective Meta-Reward within GRPO that jointly optimizes answer correctness, search path efficiency, reflection depth, and tool call diversity; (4) Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm with specialized Scout, Filter, and Synthesizer models trained via coordinated RL.
AI.result: Targets substantial improvements on GAIA and Xbench-DS benchmarks and epistemic robustness under adversarial conditions, with zero marginal API cost for training built upon LiteResearcher infrastructure. Results are planned experimental validation (framework proposed, not yet evaluated).
AI.conclusion: MetaResearcher provides a comprehensive framework to overcome static environment, shallow task design, and inefficient RL limitations in deep research agent training through four synergistic innovations, with planned empirical validation on standard benchmarks.

title: Displacement Is Not Direction: Evaluating Fidelity Metrics for Quantized LLM Deployment
summary: Fidelity metrics, such as per-token KL divergence (KLD) against a high-precision reference, are often used in practice as low-cost proxies for benchmark quality. We test this practice on a 28-quant cohort of Qwen3.6-35B-A3B and a 41-quant cohort of Devstral-Small-2-24B, evaluated across a suite of downstream benchmarks. We find that KLD is strongly correlated with benchmark score over the full cohort ($ρ=-0.72$ on Qwen and $ρ=-0.86$ on Devstral, both with $p<0.001$). However, this relationship collapses to non-significance in the near-baseline silent zone ($ρ=+0.00$ on Qwen and $ρ=-0.24$, $p=0.36$, on Devstral). This collapse persists across 14 measurement variants, including different KLD aggregations, perplexity formulations, top-1 agreement, calibration corpora, and context lengths. At the per-prompt level, KLD has only weak failure-prediction power on code, with failed-vs-passed geometric-mean ratios in $[1.08,1.22]$ across five models on LiveCodeBench, and fails as a cross-model router, achieving only $42.3\%-49.4\%$ accuracy on disagreement prompts. We trace the collapse to a structural decomposition: KLD primarily measures the volume of disagreement with the reference, with silent-zone composite $ρ=+0.94$ ($p<0.001$) on Qwen and $+0.55$ ($p=0.03$) on Devstral, while its relationship to the direction of those disagreements is weak and task-conditional.

AI.tldr: Per-token KL divergence (KLD) correlates strongly with benchmark scores across broad quant ranges but collapses to non-significance in near-baseline "silent zones" due to KLD primarily measuring disagreement volume rather than disagreement direction, limiting its utility as a fidelity proxy.
AI.motivation: To test whether commonly used fidelity metrics like KLD serve as reliable low-cost proxies for actual benchmark quality across quantized models.
AI.method: Analyzed KLD-benchmark correlations across a 28-quant Qwen3.6-35B-A3B cohort and 41-quant Devstral-Small-2-24B cohort, examined 14 measurement variants, per-prompt failure prediction on LiveCodeBench across 5 models, and cross-model routing accuracy.
AI.result: Strong full-cohort correlation (ρ=-0.72 to -0.86, p<0.001) collapses to non-significance in near-baseline silent zone (ρ=+0.00 to -0.24, p=0.36). Per-prompt failure-prediction power weak (geometric-mean ratios 1.08-1.22). Cross-model router accuracy only 42.3%-49.4%. Structural decomposition reveals KLD measures disagreement volume (silent-zone ρ=+0.94, p<0.001) but not direction.
AI.conclusion: KLD is unreliable as a fidelity proxy near baseline performance due to its inability to capture directional disagreement quality, limiting its practical use for model selection and failure prediction.

title: QMFOL: Benchmarking Large Language Model Reasoning via Quantifiable Monadic First-Order Logic Test Case Generation
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant progress in reasoning, particularly in deductive reasoning, which is crucial for high-stakes decision-making. As models improve, evaluation benchmarks should evolve to keep pace. However, existing benchmarks lack fine-grained control over logical complexity and struggle to balance semantic diversity with logical consistency.
  To address these issues, we propose QMFOL, an automated framework for generating monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with quantifiable and controllable complexity. It constructs formal logical structures using conjunction and disjunction patterns, enabling precise control over reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors. These structures are then translated into natural language via LLMs, with logical consistency ensured through round-trip verification using an external prover. Based on our framework, we build QMFOLBench, a benchmark comprising 2880 instances with 960 configurations across diverse logical and semantic dimensions. Evaluations on six large reasoning models (LRMs) and two LLMs show that performance degrades and computational overhead increases with rising logical complexity. Models perform better on True-labeled tasks than on False or Unknown ones, and exhibit sensitivity to semantic variation. Overall, QMFOL offers a scalable and reliable approach for constructing deductive reasoning benchmarks with controllable complexity, enabling more precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in modern language models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes QMFOL, an automated framework for generating monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with controllable complexity, and introduces QMFOLBench (2880 instances) to evaluate LLMs' deductive reasoning, finding that performance degrades with logical complexity and models are sensitive to semantic variation.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks lack fine-grained control over logical complexity and struggle to balance semantic diversity with logical consistency, making it difficult to precisely evaluate the deductive reasoning capabilities of increasingly advanced LLMs.
AI.method: QMFOL constructs formal logical structures using conjunction and disjunction patterns, controls reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors, translates them to natural language via LLMs, and ensures logical consistency through round-trip verification with an external prover. The framework produces QMFOLBench with 2880 instances across 960 configurations.
AI.result: Evaluations on 6 LRMs and 2 LLMs show: (1) performance degrades and computational overhead increases with rising logical complexity; (2) models perform better on True-labeled tasks than False or Unknown ones; (3) models exhibit sensitivity to semantic variation.
AI.conclusion: QMFOL provides a scalable and reliable approach for constructing deductive reasoning benchmarks with controllable complexity, enabling more precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in modern language models.

title: Triangular Consistency as a Universal Constraint for Learning Optical Flow
summary: We propose triangular consistency as a first-principled constraint for optical flow, which is agnostic to network architecture, supervision type, and dataset, and applies to both image-pair and multi-frame settings. This simple but powerful constraint is to compose two flows to induce a third flow and enforce consistency among the three. The composed flows may arise from (i) image pairs, yielding cycle consistency; (ii) multiple video frames, producing longer-range motion through temporal chaining; or (iii) image pairs combined with controlled synthetic transformations, which becomes data augmentation. This triangular consistency introduces negligible computational overhead and requires no additional annotations. Since it is derived directly from the geometry of optical flow, it does not rely on model-specific assumptions and serves as a ``universal'' plug-and-play component for optical flow training. Experiments show consistent improvement across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer learning settings.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes triangular consistency, a geometry-derived constraint for optical flow that composes two flows to induce a third and enforces consistency among them. It works across supervision types, network architectures, and datasets, serving as a universal plug-and-play training component with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: To introduce a first-principled, universal constraint for optical flow that is agnostic to network architecture, supervision type, and dataset, and can be applied in both image-pair and multi-frame settings without requiring additional annotations.
AI.method: Triangular consistency is implemented by composing two optical flows to induce a third flow and enforcing consistency among the three. It covers three scenarios: (i) cycle consistency from image pairs, (ii) longer-range motion via temporal chaining across multiple video frames, and (iii) data augmentation by combining image pairs with controlled synthetic transformations.
AI.result: Consistent improvement across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer learning settings, with negligible computational overhead and no need for additional annotations.
AI.conclusion: Triangular consistency is a universal, plug-and-play component derived directly from optical flow geometry that reliably improves optical flow training across diverse settings.

title: Multi-Agent Transactive Memory
summary: The decentralized deployment of LLM agents with diverse capabilities across diverse tasks motivates infrastructure for knowledge sharing across heterogeneous agent populations. Just as search engines index human-generated artifacts to support human problem solving, retrieval systems can organize agent-generated artifacts for reuse across agent populations. We extend retrieval-augmented generation - which demonstrates the value of human-authored artifacts to individual agents - to retrieval of agent-generated artifacts supporting a population of agents. In particular, agent trajectories encode reusable procedural knowledge, yet these artifacts are typically discarded after a single use or retained only by the producing agent, forcing newly instantiated agents to repeatedly rediscover existing solutions. We propose Multi-Agent Transactive Memory (MATM), a framework for population-level storage and retrieval of agent-generated trajectories, where producer agents contribute trajectories to a shared repository and consumer agents retrieve them to improve task execution. We focus on interactive environments (ALFWorld and WebArena), where trajectories are long and encode especially rich procedural structure. Our experiments demonstrate that retrieving trajectories from MATM improves downstream task performance and reduces interaction steps without coordination or joint training. These results position MATM as a design pattern for population-level experience sharing in open agent ecosystems.

AI.tldr: Multi-Agent Transactive Memory (MATM) is a framework for population-level storage and retrieval of agent-generated trajectories that improves task performance and reduces interaction steps in interactive environments without requiring coordination or joint training.
AI.motivation: Decentralized LLM agent deployment across diverse tasks lacks infrastructure for knowledge sharing across heterogeneous agent populations; agent trajectories (procedural knowledge) are discarded after single use or retained only by the producing agent, forcing repeated rediscovery of solutions.
AI.method: MATM extends retrieval-augmented generation to a multi-agent setting with a shared trajectory repository where producer agents contribute trajectories and consumer agents retrieve them to improve task execution, evaluated on ALFWorld and WebArena interactive environments.
AI.result: Retrieving trajectories from MATM improves downstream task performance and reduces interaction steps without coordination or joint training.
AI.conclusion: MATM serves as a design pattern for population-level experience sharing in open agent ecosystems.

title: Understanding Key Features of Time Series Foundation Models from Epidemic Forecasting
summary: Seasonal influenza infects millions of people and causes substantial morbidity and mortality in the United States each year, making accurate short-term forecasting a core public-health need. Reliable forecasts of epidemic time series can inform vaccination timing, hospital staffing, and resource allocation, yet the comparative behavior of modern forecasting architectures on infectious-disease surveillance data remains insufficiently characterized. We address this gap through a systematic evaluation of regional influenza forecasting using influenza-like illness surveillance and influenza-associated hospitalization time series under both temporal and spatial generalization settings for 1-4-week-ahead prediction. We compare classical neural network architectures, numerical transformer-based models, pretrained time series foundation models, and LLM-based forecasting approaches. Across tasks, we demonstrate that a mixture-of-experts model that fuses multiple pretrained forecasters achieves the strongest overall performance, indicating that heterogeneous pretrained representations provide complementary predictive information. Our results further show that numerical transformer-based models produce reliable forecasts, while pretraining provides the largest gains at longer horizons, particularly when the pretraining domain is mechanistically aligned with influenza dynamics. In contrast, LLM-based time series methods underperform relative to numerical forecasters in this setting. Finally, we examine hospitalization information as both an auxiliary covariate and a pretraining source. Hospitalization signals provide complementary improvements in selected settings and clarify when additional surveillance streams enhance the robustness of multi-horizon forecasting. These findings provide actionable guidance on model selection, pretraining strategy, and auxiliary-signal use for influenza preparedness.

AI.tldr: A systematic evaluation of influenza forecasting models (classical neural networks, transformers, pretrained foundation models, and LLM-based approaches) using ILI and hospitalization data shows that a mixture-of-experts model fusing multiple pretrained forecasters achieves the best performance. Numerical transformer-based models are reliable, pretraining benefits longer horizons when aligned with influenza dynamics, and LLM-based methods underperform. Hospitalization data provides complementary gains in select settings.
AI.motivation: Seasonal influenza causes substantial morbidity and mortality, making accurate short-term forecasting critical for public health decisions (e.g., vaccination timing, staffing, resource allocation). The comparative behavior of modern forecasting architectures on infectious disease surveillance data is insufficiently characterized, motivating this systematic evaluation.
AI.method: Evaluated influenza forecasting models using ILI surveillance and influenza-associated hospitalization time series under temporal and spatial generalization settings for 1-4-week-ahead prediction. Compared classical neural networks, numerical transformer-based models, pretrained time series foundation models, and LLM-based forecasting approaches. Also examined hospitalization information as both an auxiliary covariate and pretraining source.
AI.result: A mixture-of-experts model fusing multiple pretrained forecasters achieved the strongest overall performance. Numerical transformer-based models produced reliable forecasts. Pretraining provided largest gains at longer horizons when aligned with influenza dynamics. LLM-based methods underperformed numerical forecasters. Hospitalization signals provided complementary improvements in selected settings.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide actionable guidance on model selection, pretraining strategy, and auxiliary-signal use for influenza preparedness, highlighting that heterogeneous pretrained representations offer complementary predictive information and that hospitalization data can enhance multi-horizon forecasting robustness in specific contexts.

title: DiffMath: Symbol- and Graph-Aware Latent Diffusion Transformer for Handwritten Mathematical Expression Generation
summary: Handwritten Mathematical Expression Generation (HMEG) is challenging due to the complex two-dimensional layouts and long-range structural dependencies of mathematical expressions. Existing methods typically rely on explicit spatial supervision, such as symbol-level bounding boxes, which incurs high annotation costs and limits scalability. In this work, we propose DiffMath, a symbol- and graph-aware latent diffusion framework that leverages the hierarchical structure inherent in LaTeX as a structural prior, eliminating the need for positional supervision. First, we design a Relational Abstract Syntax Tree (RelAST), a generation-oriented representation that distills MathML trees into compact triplet sequences [S, R, D], where each token directly encodes a symbol identity, spatial relation, or nesting depth. Second, we introduce MathVAE, which learns structure-preserving latent representations through symbol-aware and relation-aware perceptual regularization, ensuring that the latent space captures both character semantics and spatial topology. Third, MathDiT performs conditional denoising in this structured latent space, further guided by a global symbol-count prior via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN) to improve structural coherence. Experiments show that DiffMath produces structurally consistent handwritten expressions, achieves superior performance over existing methods, and improves the accuracy of downstream OCR models through synthetic data augmentation.

AI.tldr: DiffMath is a symbol- and graph-aware latent diffusion framework for handwritten mathematical expression generation that eliminates the need for bounding box supervision by leveraging LaTeX's hierarchical structure via Relational Abstract Syntax Trees, a structure-preserving MathVAE, and a MathDiT denoiser with symbol-count conditioning.
AI.motivation: Handwritten Mathematical Expression Generation (HMEG) is challenging due to complex 2D layouts and long-range structural dependencies. Existing methods rely on expensive symbol-level bounding box supervision, which limits scalability. The goal is to generate high-quality handwritten expressions without explicit positional annotations.
AI.method: DiffMath has three components: (1) Relational Abstract Syntax Tree (RelAST) — converts MathML trees into compact triplet sequences [S, R, D] encoding symbol identity, spatial relation, and nesting depth; (2) MathVAE — learns structure-preserving latent representations via symbol-aware and relation-aware perceptual regularization; (3) MathDiT — performs conditional denoising in the latent space guided by a global symbol-count prior via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN).
AI.result: DiffMath produces structurally consistent handwritten expressions, outperforms existing methods, and improves downstream OCR accuracy through synthetic data augmentation.
AI.conclusion: By exploiting LaTeX's hierarchical structure as a prior, DiffMath successfully eliminates the need for positional supervision while generating high-quality handwritten mathematical expressions, demonstrating strong structural coherence and practical utility for data augmentation in OCR tasks.

title: eCNNTO: A Highly Generalizable ConvNet for Accelerating Topology Optimization
summary: This work proposes an element-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to accelerate density-based Topology Optimization (TO), termed eCNNTO. TO generally undergoes a large number of iterations, where finite element analysis is performed in every iteration, leading to the efficiency bottleneck especially when dense meshes are used to achieve high-resolution designs. To address this limitation, eCNNTO is proposed to build upon Kallioras et al. (2020), where a Deep Belief Network (DBN) was trained for every element to predict its near-optimal density from its early history, thereby skipping the great majority of iterations and significantly accelerating the TO procedure. However, the method lacks spatial correlations among neighboring elements and may lead to disconnected features in the final structure. The proposed method employs CNN with residual connections to address this issue. On top of it, a novel training strategy is introduced to further enhance the optimization efficiency, where the training dataset consists of the final stage density histories rather than early ones. This change can also help reduce the required training data size. eCNNTO requires only a small dataset to train and yet it can be generalized to problems with largely different boundary conditions, loading cases, design domain geometries, mesh resolutions, as well as non-design domains. In the end, the generalization capabilities and efficiency of eCNNTO are demonstrated through a variety of examples in two and three dimensions, achieving up to 90% and 97% reduction of iterations, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes eCNNTO, an element-based CNN approach to accelerate density-based Topology Optimization by predicting near-optimal densities from early iteration histories, using residual connections to maintain spatial coherence, and achieving up to 90% (2D) and 97% (3D) iteration reduction with strong generalization across different problem settings.
AI.motivation: Topology Optimization requires many iterations with costly finite element analysis (FEA) per iteration, creating an efficiency bottleneck especially for high-resolution designs. Prior work using Deep Belief Networks (DBN) per element accelerated the process but lacked spatial correlation between neighboring elements, leading to disconnected structural features.
AI.method: The method builds on element-wise prediction from Kallioras et al. (2020) but replaces DBN with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) equipped with residual connections to capture spatial correlations. A novel training strategy uses final-stage density histories instead of early ones, reducing required dataset size. The model predicts near-optimal element densities from early iteration histories, allowing the TO process to skip the majority of remaining iterations.
AI.result: eCNNTO achieves up to 90% reduction of iterations in 2D examples and 97% reduction in 3D examples. It requires only a small training dataset and generalizes to different boundary conditions, loading cases, domain geometries, mesh resolutions, and non-design domains.
AI.conclusion: The proposed eCNNTO effectively accelerates topology optimization while preserving structural connectivity through CNN-based spatial modeling, demonstrating strong generalization and significant computational savings across diverse problem settings.

title: Advances in Scientific Machine Learning for Coupled Fluid Flow and Transport
summary: This chapter reviews recent advances in Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) for modeling coupled fluid flow and transport phenomena governed by the incompressible Navier-Stokes and scalar transport equations. Such systems, found in applications like turbidity currents and thermal convection, feature strong nonlinear coupling and multiscale behavior that make high-fidelity simulations computationally expensive. To address this, the chapter surveys state-of-the-art SciML methods for building efficient surrogate models, including linear reduced-order techniques based on Singular Value Decomposition (such as Dynamic Mode Decomposition) and nonlinear neural network approaches like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and $β$-Variational Autoencoders ($β$-VAEs). It first covers the authors' work combining these models with High Performance Computing strategies, including Adaptive Mesh Refinement/Coarsening (AMR/C) and scientific floating-point data compression. It then presents two new contributions: surrogate modeling of turbidity currents via PINNs, and the extraction of disentangled nonlinear modes from thermal flows using $β$-VAEs. Governing equations and representative benchmarks, including lock-exchange flows and Rayleigh-Bénard convection, illustrate these methodologies. The chapter is intentionally long, covering both the mathematical and physical foundations of coupled fluid flow and the computational aspects of state-of-the-art modeling. Overall, it demonstrates how SciML enables fast, accurate approximations of complex coupled systems within the specific data regimes and modeling assumptions considered, while substantially reducing computational cost relative to full-order simulations. Broader capabilities such as real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification remain active research directions whose feasibility depends strongly on the problem at hand.

AI.tldr: This chapter reviews SciML methods—including SVD-based reduced-order techniques (e.g., DMD), PINNs, and β-VAEs—for building efficient surrogate models of coupled fluid flow and transport phenomena. It covers the authors' prior work integrating these with HPC strategies (AMR/C, data compression) and presents two new contributions: PINN-based surrogate modeling of turbidity currents and β-VAE-based extraction of disentangled nonlinear modes from thermal flows. Benchmarks include lock-exchange flows and Rayleigh-Bénard convection. Results show substantial computational cost reduction while maintaining accuracy, with real-time prediction and UQ remaining open challenges.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity simulations of coupled fluid flow and transport phenomena (e.g., turbidity currents, thermal convection) are computationally expensive due to strong nonlinear coupling and multiscale behavior. Efficient surrogate models are needed to reduce computational cost while preserving accuracy.
AI.method: Surveys linear reduced-order models (SVD-based, DMD) and nonlinear neural network approaches (PINNs, β-VAEs). Combines these with HPC strategies (AMR/C, scientific data compression). Presents two new contributions: PINN-based surrogate modeling for turbidity currents and β-VAE for disentangled nonlinear mode extraction from thermal flows.
AI.result: SciML methods enable fast, accurate approximations of coupled systems with substantially reduced computational cost relative to full-order simulations. Demonstrated on lock-exchange flows and Rayleigh-Bénard convection benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SciML provides efficient surrogate modeling for coupled fluid systems within specific data regimes and assumptions. Real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification remain active research areas with problem-dependent feasibility.

title: Timage: A Generative Text-in-Image Paradigm for Fine-Tuning Vision-Language Models
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) often lose track of the right image regions during fine-grained spatial reasoning, because a textual query rarely carries any explicit geometric anchor into the pixel domain. Prevailing remedies either rewire the model's weights or pad the prompt with verbose instructions, yet neither reliably pins the language to the correct visual coordinates without eroding the backbone's general competence. We introduce Timage, a paradigm that recasts multimodal understanding as an alignment problem solved at the input: the query is drawn, as a typeset overlay, onto the image itself. The placement and appearance of this overlay are produced by a Constrained Schrödinger Bridge (cSB), an entropic optimal-transport sampler that factorizes layout synthesis into two coupled stochastic stages. The first stage, Region Search, transports noise toward query-aligned image zones while obeying a hard occlusion barrier that protects salient foreground content; the second stage, Appearance Shaping, sizes the glyphs through an ``ink-budget'' regularizer so that the rendered text stays legible and visually balanced. The resulting overlay behaves as an explicit attention beacon that channels the model's focus along spatial semantics. On the VMCBench suite, Timage paired with a modest 7B backbone clearly overtakes far larger proprietary systems as well as parameter-tuned baselines. The study positions deliberate input reconstruction as a powerful, architecture-neutral lever for strengthening multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: Timage overlays a text query directly onto an image using a Constrained Schrödinger Bridge to optimize placement and appearance, enabling a 7B MLLM to outperform much larger models on fine-grained spatial reasoning without modifying the model architecture.
AI.motivation: MLLMs struggle with fine-grained spatial reasoning because textual queries lack geometric anchors to specific image regions; existing solutions (weight rewiring, verbose prompting) are unreliable and degrade backbone capabilities.
AI.method: Timage recasts multimodal understanding as an input-level alignment problem by drawing the query as a typeset overlay on the image. The overlay is generated by a Constrained Schrödinger Bridge (cSB) with two stages: (1) Region Search — transports noise toward query-relevant zones while respecting an occlusion barrier protecting salient foreground; (2) Appearance Shaping — applies an "ink-budget" regularizer for legible, balanced glyph sizing.
AI.result: On VMCBench, Timage with a 7B backbone outperforms far larger proprietary systems and parameter-tuned baselines.
AI.conclusion: Deliberate input reconstruction (overlaying the query onto the image) is a powerful, architecture-neutral mechanism for strengthening multimodal spatial reasoning.

title: The Tao of Agency: Autotelic AI, Embedded Agency and Dissolution of the Self
summary:
Most artificial intelligence systems are built on the assumption that goals are exogenous and specified by the designer. Exploring what happens when an agent begins generating its own goals opens the field of autotelic AI. Agents are expected not merely to pursue objectives but to discover them. In this article, we trace its consequences through intrinsic motivation, resource-driven priors, causal-interventional learning, homeostasis, and embeddedness; the last of which is found to be a necessary but not sufficient condition for autotelic agency. Embeddedness individuates the agent at the cost of revealing that the individuation is non-unique, such that the same dynamics admit many valid partitions, each defining a different candidate self.
  The deepest problem with autotelic AI is therefore not how the agent generates goals, but how it generates and relativizes the self to which the goals are assigned. The agent must believe in its own boundary in order to act, and see through that boundary in order to understand. We consolidate these developments into a single framework and extend it along three directions: a quantum formulation in which the agent-environment cut becomes physical, a philosophical reading against non-dual contemplative traditions, and a concrete LLM-based agentic instantiation.

AI.tldr: This paper explores autotelic AI—agents that generate their own goals—and identifies the core problem as how the agent constructs and relativizes the self to which goals are assigned. It synthesizes insights from intrinsic motivation, embeddedness, and homeostasis, and extends the framework into quantum formulations, non-dual philosophy, and LLM-based implementations.
AI.motivation: To move beyond the standard AI assumption of exogenous, designer-specified goals and investigate what happens when agents autonomously generate and pursue their own objectives, thereby opening the field of autotelic AI.
AI.method: The paper traces consequences across intrinsic motivation, resource-driven priors, causal-interventional learning, homeostasis, and embeddedness; consolidates these into a single framework; and extends the framework along three directions: a quantum formulation (physicalizing the agent-environment cut), a philosophical reading (against non-dual contemplative traditions), and a concrete LLM-based agentic instantiation.
AI.result: Embeddedness is identified as necessary but not sufficient for autotelic agency. It individuates the agent but reveals non-unique partitionings of the same dynamics into agent vs. environment. The deepest problem is not goal generation but how the agent generates and relativizes the self to which goals are assigned—requiring the agent to "believe in its own boundary to act, and see through that boundary to understand."
AI.conclusion: Autotelic AI's fundamental challenge is self-individuation under non-uniqueness. The paper offers a consolidated framework and three novel extensions (quantum, philosophical, LLM-based) to address this.

title: On the QUEST for Uncertainty Quantification via Highest Density Regions
comment: 27 pages, of which 10 are main text. Contains 7 figures, 4 tables, 1 algorithm in total
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is essential for reliable decision-making in safety-critical applications in probabilistic machine learning. For regression problems, dominant scalar UQ approaches - notably, those based on proper scoring rules - measure uncertainty via pointwise predictive risk. This can lead to counterintuitive results when the target statistic is not the conditional expectation. We propose an alternative framework, in which uncertainty is characterised by the volume of the most probable subset of a distribution's support. QUEST (Quantifying Uncertainty via highest dEnSiTy regions) is a novel approach to UQ based on the concentration of Lebesgue measure at a distribution's peak(s), evaluated at one or more values of a robustness parameter $α$. We establish connections between our measures and classical statistics from information theory and economics. We show that, unlike popular alternatives based on proper scoring rules, QUEST measures of epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty satisfy a set of axioms adapted from the UQ literature, including monotonicity under distributional spread and invariance to location shifts. Selective prediction benchmarks confirm that QUEST performs favourably against standard measures such as variance and differential entropy.

AI.tldr: QUEST proposes a new UQ framework based on highest density region volume, offering an alternative to standard pointwise predictive risk measures, with desirable axiomatic properties and competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Standard scalar UQ approaches based on proper scoring rules measure uncertainty via pointwise predictive risk, which can produce counterintuitive results when the target statistic is not the conditional expectation.
AI.method: QUEST quantifies uncertainty via the volume of the most probable subset (highest density region) of a distribution's support, using the concentration of Lebesgue measure at distribution peak(s) evaluated at one or more values of a robustness parameter α.
AI.result: QUEST measures satisfy a set of UQ axioms (monotonicity under distributional spread, location shift invariance) that popular proper-scoring-rule-based alternatives do not. Selective prediction benchmarks show QUEST performs favorably against variance and differential entropy.
AI.conclusion: QUEST offers a theoretically grounded alternative for UQ in regression, with connections to information theory and economics, better axiomatic properties, and strong empirical performance.

title: Confidence Calibration for Multimodal LLMs: An Empirical Study through Medical VQA
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) show great potential in medical tasks, but their elicited confidence often misaligns with actual accuracy, potentially leading to misdiagnosis or overlooking correct advice. This study presents the first comprehensive analysis of the relationship between accuracy and confidence in medical MLLMs. It proposes a novel method that combines Multi-Strategy Fusion-Based Interrogation (MS-FBI) with auxiliary expert LLM assessment, aiming to improve confidence calibration in Medical Visual Question Answering (VQA). Experiments demonstrate that our method reduces the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by an average of 40\% across three Medical VQA datasets, significantly enhancing MLLMs' reliability. The findings highlight the importance of domain-specific calibration for MLLMs in healthcare, offering a more trustworthy solution for AI-assisted diagnosis.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses confidence misalignment in Medical MLLMs by proposing MS-FBI combined with auxiliary expert LLM assessment, achieving 40% average reduction in Expected Calibration Error across three Medical VQA datasets.
AI.motivation: MLLMs in medical tasks often exhibit confidence that misaligns with actual accuracy, risking misdiagnosis or overlooking correct advice. No prior work had comprehensively analyzed this accuracy-confidence relationship in medical MLLMs.
AI.method: Multi-Strategy Fusion-Based Interrogation (MS-FBI) combined with auxiliary expert LLM assessment for confidence calibration in Medical VQA.
AI.result: Reduced Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by an average of 40% across three Medical VQA datasets.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific calibration is critical for MLLM reliability in healthcare; the proposed method provides a more trustworthy solution for AI-assisted diagnosis.

title: PhysDrift: Bridging the Embodiment Gap in Humanoid Co-Speech Motion Generation
summary: Humanoid robots require co-speech motions that are not only expressive and speech-aligned, but also physically executable under embodiment constraints. Existing co-speech generation pipelines are predominantly human-centric: motions are first generated in human-body representations such as SMPL-X and subsequently retargeted to humanoid robots. In this work, we identify a fundamental embodiment gap in this paradigm, where the mismatch between human motion manifolds and humanoid embodiment constraints disrupts embodiment consistency during motion transfer and physical execution. Through extensive analysis, we show that although retargeting can preserve coarse motion semantics, it significantly compresses motion diversity and weakens prosody-motion synchronization, limiting expressive humanoid behaviors. To address this problem, we first propose IK-EER, a prosody-preserving humanoid motion curation framework that jointly optimizes kinematic feasibility and speech-motion temporal alignment during retargeting. Building upon the curated robot-native motion dataset, we further introduce PhysDrift, an embodiment-aware co-speech motion generation framework that directly predicts executable humanoid joint trajectories from speech without relying on intermediate human-body representations. Unlike conventional human-centric pipelines, PhysDrift maintains embodiment consistency throughout both training and inference while incorporating physical regularization to stabilize robot motion dynamics. Extensive experiments and real-world humanoid deployment demonstrate that embodiment-aware robot-native generation substantially improves speech-motion alignment, physical plausibility, motion smoothness, inference efficiency, and real-time interaction capability.

AI.tldr: Humanoid co-speech motion generation suffers from an embodiment gap when using human-centric pipelines (SMPL-X → retargeting). The authors propose IK-EER for prosody-preserving retargeting and PhysDrift, an embodiment-aware framework that directly predicts executable humanoid joint trajectories from speech, improving speech-motion alignment, physical plausibility, and real-time capability.
AI.motivation: Identify the embodiment gap in existing human-centric co-speech generation pipelines, where retargeting from human motion manifolds to humanoid robots compresses motion diversity and weakens prosody-motion synchronization, limiting expressive and physically executable humanoid behaviors.
AI.method: 1) IK-EER: a prosody-preserving humanoid motion curation framework that jointly optimizes kinematic feasibility and speech-motion temporal alignment during retargeting. 2) PhysDrift: an embodiment-aware co-speech generation framework that directly predicts executable humanoid joint trajectories from speech, bypassing intermediate human-body representations, with physical regularization for stable dynamics.
AI.result: IK-EER produces a curated robot-native motion dataset. PhysDrift achieves superior speech-motion alignment, physical plausibility, motion smoothness, inference efficiency, and real-time interaction capability, validated through extensive experiments and real-world humanoid deployment.
AI.conclusion: Embodiment-aware robot-native generation (PhysDrift) significantly outperforms conventional human-centric pipelines by maintaining embodiment consistency throughout training and inference, directly addressing the embodiment gap for expressive and physically executable humanoid co-speech motions.

title: Unsupervised Causal Abstractions Discovery
summary: Causal abstractions formalize when a high-level structural causal model (SCM) captures the interventional behavior of a lower-level SCM. Existing applications of this notion largely follow a hypothesis-testing paradigm: an expert proposes a candidate high-level model and then evaluates if the low-level system implements it. We study the complementary problem of learning a high-level model directly from low-level measurements. Our contributions leverage hypotheses from low-rank causal discovery, and can be summarized as follows: (1) we show that observations generated by a low-rank graph induce latents that form a causal abstraction, (2) we provide identifiability results about these latents, and (3) we propose a practical objective to learn this high-level SCM.

AI.tldr: This paper studies learning high-level causal models from low-level data by leveraging low-rank causal discovery, showing that low-rank graphs induce latent causal abstractions with identifiability guarantees and proposing a practical learning objective.
AI.motivation: Existing causal abstraction work follows a hypothesis-testing paradigm where an expert proposes a candidate high-level model and evaluates if a low-level system implements it.
