The authors study the complementary problem of learning a high-level model directly from low-level measurements, which has been less explored.
AI.method: The authors leverage hypotheses from low-rank causal discovery: (1) they show observations from a low-rank graph induce latents that form a causal abstraction, (2) they provide identifiability results about these latents, and (3) they propose a practical objective to learn the high-level SCM.
AI.result: Observations generated by a low-rank graph induce latents that form a causal abstraction, with identifiability guarantees for these latents.
AI.conclusion: Low-rank causal discovery can be effectively leveraged to learn high-level structural causal models from low-level data, bridging the gap between hypothesis-testing and data-driven approaches to causal abstraction.

title: SketchKeyAnime: Reference-anchored Sparse Key-Sketch Animation Synthesis
summary: Traditional animation production relies heavily on manual drawing and iterative refinement, particularly for key-pose design, in-betweening, and character coloring. While existing animation and video generation methods have made notable progress, they typically depend on RGB boundary frames, dense frame-wise conditions, or complete sketch sequences, limiting their applicability under low-cost input conditions. We present SketchKeyAnime, a video diffusion framework for generating structurally controllable, appearance-consistent, and temporally coherent animations from sparse key-sketch inputs. Given a single reference RGB image and a few temporally indexed key sketches, SketchKeyAnime introduces a dual-branch conditioning mechanism to encode local geometric constraints alongside semantic-temporal context. It leverages Sketch Cross Attention to fuse reference image and sketch conditions with learnable gating, and incorporates an Adaptive Weighted Loss to strengthen supervision on key-sketch frames and line-art regions. Experimental results on the Aesthetic subset of Sakuga-42M show that our approach consistently outperforms representative animation interpolation and sketch-guided generation baselines. Compared to the best-performing baseline, SketchKeyAnime reduces EDMD by 31.9\% and FVD by 9.5\%, demonstrating superior sketch fidelity and temporal coherence, while achieving the best overall performance across most quantitative metrics. These results validate the proposed framework and highlight its potential for low-cost, highly controllable animation creation.

AI.tldr: SketchKeyAnime is a video diffusion framework that generates structurally controllable, appearance-consistent, and temporally coherent animations from sparse key-sketch inputs using a dual-branch conditioning mechanism, Sketch Cross Attention with learnable gating, and Adaptive Weighted Loss, outperforming baselines on Sakuga-42M with EDMD reduced by 31.9% and FVD by 9.5%.
AI.motivation: Traditional animation relies on manual drawing and iterative refinement for key-pose design, in-betweening, and coloring. Existing video generation methods require RGB boundary frames, dense frame-wise conditions, or complete sketch sequences, limiting applicability under low-cost input conditions. The goal is to enable animation creation from sparse key-sketch inputs with structural control, appearance consistency, and temporal coherence.
AI.method: SketchKeyAnime introduces a dual-branch conditioning mechanism encoding local geometric constraints alongside semantic-temporal context. It uses Sketch Cross Attention to fuse reference image and sketch conditions with learnable gating, and incorporates Adaptive Weighted Loss to strengthen supervision on key-sketch frames and line-art regions.
AI.result: On the Aesthetic subset of Sakuga-42M, SketchKeyAnime consistently outperforms animation interpolation and sketch-guided generation baselines. Compared to the best-performing baseline, it reduces EDMD by 31.9% and FVD by 9.5%, demonstrating superior sketch fidelity and temporal coherence, achieving best overall performance across most quantitative metrics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework validates its effectiveness for low-cost, highly controllable animation creation from sparse key-sketch inputs.

title: Advancing DialNav through Automatic Embodied Dialog Augmentation
summary: For embodied agents capable of physical interaction, the capability to create and understand dialog is crucial to ensure both safety and effectiveness. While DialNav~\cite{han2025dialnav} provides a framework for holistic evaluation of the dialog--execution loop in photorealistic indoor navigation, its performance remains limited by a critical scarcity of training data (2K episodes). To address this, we propose an automatic generation pipeline, and construct the \textbf{RAINbow} dataset, a large-scale training dataset with 238K episodes for DialNav. Our pipeline converts existing VLN datasets into multi-turn dialog and creates cost-efficient and high-quality dataset. Then, we introduce two additional complementary advances to unlock the data's full potential: (1) Dual-Strategy Training, a navigation training scheme to align the navigation training with the dynamic dialog-navigation loop, and (2) a localization model that leverages VLN knowledge. By combining these complementary solutions, our model substantially outperforms the baseline in success rate on both \textbf{Val Seen} (58.24, \textbf{+89\%}) and \textbf{Val Unseen} (29.05, \textbf{+100\%}) splits, establishing a new state of the art.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the data scarcity problem in DialNav by constructing RAINbow, a large-scale 238K-episode dataset via automatic generation from VLN datasets, and introduces Dual-Strategy Training plus a VLN-knowledge-based localization model, achieving +89-100% improvement in success rate.
AI.motivation: DialNav's performance is limited by critical data scarcity (only 2K episodes). The authors aim to overcome this bottleneck to improve dialog-driven embodied navigation.
AI.method: (1) An automatic pipeline converting existing VLN datasets into multi-turn dialog episodes to create RAINbow (238K episodes). (2) Dual-Strategy Training to align navigation training with the dynamic dialog-navigation loop. (3) A localization model leveraging VLN knowledge.
AI.result: Success rate on Val Seen: 58.24 (+89%); Val Unseen: 29.05 (+100%), establishing new state of the art.
AI.conclusion: Scaling dialog-navigation training data via automatic generation, combined with training alignment and VLN-knowledge injection, substantially improves dialog-driven embodied navigation performance.

title: IHBench: Evaluating Post-Interruption Recovery in Voice Agents with Structured Workflows
summary:
Voice agents deployed in structured workflows (customer service, healthcare scheduling, account management) must handle frequent user interruptions while maintaining progress through multi-step procedures. Existing benchmarks for speech-capable models focus on the timing of interruptions: barge-in detection, endpointing, and turn-taking dynamics. They leave unmeasured what happens after the interruption: does the agent resume the workflow at the correct step? Does it address the user's interjection? Does it avoid re-delivering content the user already heard?
  We introduce IHBench (Interruption Handling Benchmark), a benchmark that evaluates post-interruption recovery in voice agents executing state-machine-driven workflows across 10 enterprise domains. Six interruption types are injected at controlled points mid-utterance, with per-interruption evaluation rubrics generated alongside the data. Each interruption is scored on two axes: task fulfillment and recovery quality.
  We evaluate 27 audio-language model configurations from OpenAI, Google, and the open-weight community. Models vary widely, and recovery quality depends strongly on the interruption type. Across our experiments, closed-weight models are consistently more robust to interruptions than open-weight ones: they win far more often on task fulfillment, degrade roughly 3.3x more slowly as conversations grow longer, and show no audio-versus-text modality gap, whereas the open-weight models lose ground on all three. A human study validates the LLM judge against human annotators, and a cross-benchmark analysis against AudioMultiChallenge indicates that recovery quality is a largely distinct capability axis.

AI.tldr: IHBench evaluates post-interruption recovery in voice agents executing state-machine-driven workflows across 10 enterprise domains. It tests 27 audio-language model configurations and finds closed-weight models consistently outperform open-weight ones in task fulfillment, degradation resilience, and modality consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for speech-capable models focus only on interruption timing (barge-in detection, endpointing, turn-taking) but leave unmeasured what happens after the interruption — whether the agent resumes correctly, addresses the user's interjection, or avoids re-delivering content already heard.
AI.method: IHBench injects six interruption types at controlled mid-utterance points in state-machine-driven workflows across 10 enterprise domains, with per-interruption evaluation rubrics scoring on two axes: task fulfillment and recovery quality. 27 audio-language model configurations from OpenAI, Google, and open-weight community were evaluated.
AI.result: Closed-weight models consistently outperform open-weight ones: they win far more often on task fulfillment, degrade roughly 3.3x more slowly as conversations lengthen, and show no audio-versus-text modality gap, while open-weight models lose ground on all three. Recovery quality depends strongly on interruption type.
AI.conclusion: Recovery quality is a largely distinct capability axis from existing benchmarks (cross-benchmark analysis vs AudioMultiChallenge). A human study validates the LLM judge against human annotators.

title: Addressing Detail Bottlenecks in Latent Diffusion for RGB-to-SWIR Image Translation
summary: Latent diffusion models (LDMs) enable efficient image-to-image translation but discard fine spatial details during compression, degrading downstream perception tasks. We identify two bottlenecks: the autoencoder, which loses spatial information, and the conditioning pathway, which further degrades the source signal through naive downsampling. We propose two lightweight, backbone-agnostic fixes: a Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) that injects high-resolution source features into the decoder via skip connections, and a Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE) that replaces naive downsampling with a learned conditioning signal. Evaluated on RGB-to-SWIR translation for driving scenes with two denoiser backbones (U-Net and DiT), our approach improves detection mAP by up to 2x over the latent diffusion baseline, with up to 3.4x gains on small objects (COCO-small, <32^2 px^2), while achieving state-of-the-art FID. We further show that FID and detection performance are poorly correlated, motivating multi-axis evaluation. Results generalise zero-shot to the public RASMD benchmark. We will publicly release test data with annotations, all checkpoints, and training code.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two lightweight fixes (SCAE and LGE) to improve spatial fidelity in latent diffusion models for image-to-image translation, achieving up to 2x improvement in detection mAP and 3.4x gains on small objects in RGB-to-SWIR driving scene translation.
AI.motivation: Latent diffusion models discard fine spatial details during compression and conditioning, degrading downstream perception tasks like object detection.
AI.method: Two backbone-agnostic components: (1) Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) injects high-resolution source features into decoder via skip connections; (2) Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE) replaces naive downsampling with a learned conditioning signal.
AI.result: Improves detection mAP by up to 2x over latent diffusion baseline, with up to 3.4x gains on small objects (COCO-small, <32² px²), and achieves state-of-the-art FID on RGB-to-SWIR translation for driving scenes with both U-Net and DiT backbones. Results generalize zero-shot to RASMD benchmark.
AI.conclusion: FID and detection performance are poorly correlated, motivating multi-axis evaluation. The approach demonstrates that lightweight architectural fixes to both the autoencoder and conditioning pathway can significantly improve downstream perception without backbone changes.

title: ENPIRE: Agentic Robot Policy Self-Improvement in the Real World
summary: Achieving dexterous robotic manipulation in the real world heavily relies on human supervision and algorithm engineering, which becomes a central bottleneck in the pursuit of general physical intelligence. Although emerging coding agents can generate code to automate algorithm search, their successes remain largely confined in digital environments. We conjecture that the missing abstraction to automate robotics research is a repeatable feedback loop for real-world policy improvement: reset the scene, execute a policy, verify the outcome, and refine the next iteration. To bridge this gap, we introduce ENPIRE, a harness framework for coding agents that instantiates this physical feedback routine with four core modules: an Environment module (EN) for automatic reset and verification, a Policy Improvement module (PI) that launches policy refinement, a Rollout module (R) to evaluate policies with one or multiple physical robots operating in parallel, and an Evolution module (E) in which coding agents analyze logs, consult literature, improve training infrastructure and algorithm code to address failure modes. This closed-loop system transforms real-world manipulation learning into a controllable optimization procedure, minimizing human effort while allowing fair ablations across training recipe and agent variants. Powered by ENPIRE, frontier coding agents can autonomously train a policy to achieve a 99% success rate on challenging, dexterous manipulation tasks, such as organizing a pin box, fastening a zip tie, and tool use, a process that further accelerates when we dispatch an agent team on a robot fleet. Our results suggest a practical and scalable path toward deploying coding agents to autonomously advancing robotics in the physical world.

AI.tldr: ENPIRE is a harness framework that enables coding agents to autonomously train dexterous robotic manipulation policies through a closed-loop physical feedback system (Environment, Policy Improvement, Rollout, Evolution), achieving 99% success rates on complex tasks like pin organization, zip-tying, and tool use without human intervention.
AI.motivation: Real-world robotic manipulation research is bottlenecked by heavy reliance on human supervision and algorithm engineering; existing coding agents succeed in digital environments but lack a repeatable feedback loop for physical policy improvement.
AI.method: ENPIRE implements a four-module closed-loop system: Environment module (EN) for automatic scene reset and verification, Policy Improvement module (PI) for launching policy refinement, Rollout module (R) for parallel evaluation on physical robots, and Evolution module (E) where coding agents analyze logs, consult literature, and improve training infrastructure and algorithm code to address failure modes.
AI.result: Frontier coding agents using ENPIRE autonomously achieve 99% success rates on challenging dexterous manipulation tasks (pin box organization, zip tie fastening, tool use), with further acceleration when dispatching an agent team on a robot fleet.
AI.conclusion: ENPIRE provides a practical and scalable path toward deploying coding agents for autonomous advancement of robotics in the physical world, transforming real-world manipulation learning into a controllable optimization procedure with minimal human effort.

title: Comparing Linear Probes with Mahalanobis Cosine Similarity
summary: Linear probes are widely used in interpretability research and often compared by cosine similarity. The Mahalanobis cosine similarity (MCS) between two directions, which reweights the inner product by test data covariance, is a natural task-aware refinement. Ying et al. (2026) report that a probe's MCS to a reference probe trained on the out-of-distribution (OOD) data near-perfectly linearly predicts the probe's OOD AUROC (R^2 = 0.98). Here, we extend this empirical finding across models, layers, and concept domains, and prove this general phenomenon in closed form: For balanced classes whose projections are Gaussian, OOD AUROC and MCS to the reference probe are linear because both are sigmoid-shaped functions of the probe's signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) on the test data. The theory also predicts when this linearity fails, which we verify empirically. MCS offers a theoretically grounded and empirically effective alternative to Euclidean cosine similarity for comparing linear probes.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that Mahalanobis cosine similarity (MCS) between linear probes linearly predicts OOD AUROC (R²=0.98) because both depend on the probe's signal-to-noise ratio; MCS is superior to Euclidean cosine similarity for probe comparison.
AI.motivation: Linear probes are commonly used in interpretability but are typically compared using cosine similarity without accounting for data covariance. The authors aim to understand and generalize the empirical observation that MCS predicts OOD performance, and to provide theoretical grounding for this relationship.
AI.method: The authors extend empirical findings across models, layers, and concept domains, and provide a closed-form proof that for balanced classes with Gaussian projections, both OOD AUROC and MCS are sigmoid-shaped functions of the probe's signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), yielding a linear relationship. They also theoretically predict and empirically verify conditions where this linearity breaks down.
AI.result: The linear relationship between MCS and OOD AUROC is near-perfect (R²=0.98) across diverse settings, confirming the theoretical prediction. The theory also correctly identifies failure cases for the linearity.
AI.conclusion: Mahalanobis cosine similarity is a theoretically grounded and empirically effective metric for comparing linear probes, outperforming Euclidean cosine similarity for this purpose.

title: ROSE: Benchmarking the Perception-to-Action Gap in Multimodal Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly expected to act on visual information, yet the same scene may require different actions under different task contexts. How reliably can a model turn the same visual evidence into the action required by the current context? To answer this question, we introduce \textsc{ROSE} (\textbf{R}eference-conditioned \textbf{O}ddity and \textbf{S}ymbolic \textbf{E}xecution), a controlled benchmark that holds the visual scene fixed while varying region constraints and required symbolic outputs. Through coupled counting and coordinate-action tasks, \textsc{ROSE} tests whether models can infer an implicit majority reference and act on the resulting fine-grained visual evidence under changing contexts. Across nine recent MLLMs, performance drops by as much as 44.5 percentage points from counting-oriented tasks to region-conditioned action, despite 98.8\% human performance. The gap persists on paired scenes and regions for which the same model returns the correct count, while global-click and matched local controls show that coordinate grounding explains only part of the loss, revealing a distinct, model-dependent bottleneck in turning shared visual evidence into context-specific actions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ROSE, a benchmark that tests whether MLLMs can use the same visual information to perform different actions depending on task context. Results show a large performance gap (up to 44.5 percentage points drop) from counting to context-specific actions, with humans at 98.8%, revealing a distinct bottleneck in context-conditioned action execution beyond just visual grounding.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether MLLMs can reliably turn the same visual evidence into different actions required by varying task contexts, a capability that existing benchmarks do not isolate because they conflate visual changes with task changes.
AI.method: ROSE (Reference-conditioned Oddity and Symbolic Execution) benchmark holds visual scenes fixed while varying region constraints and symbolic outputs, using coupled counting and coordinate-action tasks with implicit majority reference inference.
AI.result: Across nine MLLMs, performance drops by up to 44.5 percentage points from counting to region-conditioned action tasks (vs. 98.8% human performance). The gap persists even when controlling for correct counting, global-click, and matched local controls, showing coordinate grounding explains only part of the loss.
AI.conclusion: There is a distinct, model-dependent bottleneck in MLLMs' ability to turn shared visual evidence into context-specific actions, beyond simple visual grounding failures.

title: Reward as An Agent for Embodied World Models
summary: While RL has become a promising tool for refining world models, existing methods largely rely on conservative rollouts near the training distribution, limiting exploration, behavioral diversity, and richer dynamic discovery. In this work, we challenge this conservative paradigm. We argue that the core limitation is not exploration itself, but the lack of reliable verification strategies to support broader exploration. Without reliable verification, expanded exploration becomes highly susceptible to reward hacking, where policies exploit imperfect rewards without achieving genuine improvement. To evaluate this motivation, we instantiate our method in embodied world models, where physical plausibility, and task completion provide a rigorous testbed for scalable RL under complex dynamics. On the verification side, we introduce Reward as an Agent, an agentic reward framework that actively evaluates generated behaviors to provide robust reward signals and mitigate reward hacking under distribution shifts. On the exploration side, we introduce Dynamic-Aware Rollout Diversification through DynDiff-GRPO, which explicitly expands action-space exploration to diversify trajectories, broaden state-action coverage, and encourage richer embodied behaviors beyond conservative rollout regimes. By unifying Reward as an Agent with DynDiff-GRPO, we enable RL on a more reliable reward foundation with substantially diversified sampling, effectively mitigating reward hacking while yielding significant accuracy gains across multiple open-source world models, thereby demonstrating that broader exploration can scale successfully when grounded in robust verification.

AI.tldr: The paper challenges conservative RL for world models by proposing Reward as an Agent (robust verification) and DynDiff-GRPO (diversified exploration) to enable broader exploration without reward hacking, achieving accuracy gains across multiple open-source world models.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods for refining world models rely on conservative rollouts near the training distribution, limiting exploration, behavioral diversity, and richer dynamic discovery. The core limitation is not exploration itself but the lack of reliable verification strategies to support broader exploration.
AI.method: Reward as an Agent (agentic reward framework for robust verification and reward hacking mitigation) + DynDiff-GRPO (Dynamic-Aware Rollout Diversification for explicit action-space exploration and trajectory diversification), unified to enable RL with reliable reward signals and diversified sampling.
AI.result: Significant accuracy gains across multiple open-source world models; broader exploration scales successfully when grounded in robust verification.
AI.conclusion: Broader exploration can scale successfully when supported by robust verification, effectively mitigating reward hacking and yielding substantial improvements in embodied world model refinement.

title: Latent Confounded Causal Discovery via Lie Bracket Geometry
summary: Recent work on Kan-Do-Calculus (KDC) has established that the boundary between passive observation and active intervention in causal inference is a category-theoretic bi-adjunction, with interventions modeled by left Kan extensions and conditioning by right Kan extensions. This paper introduces two causal discovery algorithms under latent confounding, building on the information-geometric and categorical consequences of KDC. In smooth statistical settings, Radon-Nikodym derivatives between observational and interventional measures induce local causal vector fields; failures of these fields to close under Lie brackets become computable Frobenius residuals, which we interpret as witnesses of failed visible integrability and possible latent or unmodeled structure. Our first algorithm, BRIDGE (Bracket Residuals for Interventional Discovery and Geometric Estimation), combines an interventional density or Radon-Nikodym-ratio engine with a geometric screen that proposes a high-recall family of admissible arrows, identifies non-closing visible pairs as latent-obstruction candidates, and passes the reduced family to downstream score-based or differentiable discovery routines. The second algorithmic contribution, Spectral Kan-Do Flow Matching (SKFM), learns amortized intervention fields and factors latent curvature spectrally, exposing the direct Lie-space endpoint toward which BRIDGE points. A detailed set of experiments show that both algorithms are capable of discovering causal models with latent confounders while collapsing the super-exponential space of possible DAGs by many orders of magnitude. This paper introduces a new paradigm in causal discovery, where latent structure is inferred directly from the geometry of intervention-induced flows.

AI.tldr: Two causal discovery algorithms (BRIDGE and SKFM) are introduced that leverage category-theoretic and information-geometric properties of the Kan-Do-Calculus to identify latent confounders by measuring non-closure of causal vector fields under Lie brackets, collapsing the super-exponential DAG search space.
AI.motivation: To extend causal discovery under latent confounding by exploiting the geometric structure of intervention-induced flows, moving beyond combinatorial DAG search toward continuous, curvature-based inference of hidden variables.
AI.method: BRIDGE uses Radon-Nikodym derivatives to construct local causal vector fields, detects latent obstructions via Frobenius residuals (failure of Lie bracket closure), then passes reduced arrow families to score-based discovery. SKFM learns amortized intervention fields and factors latent curvature spectrally to expose the Lie-space endpoint.
AI.result: Both algorithms discover causal models with latent confounders while dramatically collapsing the super-exponential space of possible DAGs.
AI.conclusion: A new paradigm in causal discovery is established, where latent structure is inferred directly from the geometry of intervention-induced flows rather than combinatorial search.

title: Semantic-Anchored Evidential Fusion for Domain-Robust Whole-Slide Survival Analysis
summary: Whole-slide images (WSIs) are widely used for computational cancer prognosis. However, most existing methods primarily focus on in-domain performance and fail to generalize across clinical centers. This limitation stems from their reliance on pixel-derived representations that are highly susceptible to domain-specific artifacts caused by staining protocols and scanner hardware. We hypothesize that high-level pathology semantics, such as tumor grade and micro-environmental architecture, provide a domain-invariant semantic representation that mirrors the robust diagnostic logic of human pathologists. Therefore, we propose a Semantic-Anchored Evidential Fusion Survival (SAEFS) framework, where SAEFS derives semantic anchors from WSIs via Visual Question Answering (VQA), employs a dual-stream WSI evidence extraction architecture, uses Dirichlet-based Subjective Logic to model uncertainty, and fuses semantic and visual evidence through a cautious conjunction rule to avoid overconfident fusion from correlated sources. Trained exclusively on one source domain and evaluated zero-shot across four unseen domains, SAEFS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models both in prediction accuracy and reliability, improving the average C-index by 10.2%. Quantitative analyses further show that VQA-derived semantic features exhibit significantly lower cross-center divergence than pixel-derived features, highlighting their robustness for cross-center clinical applications.

AI.tldr: SAEFS is a framework that uses VQA-derived semantic anchors from WSIs to achieve domain-invariant cancer prognosis, outperforming state-of-the-art models by 10.2% average C-index in zero-shot cross-center settings.
AI.motivation: Existing WSI-based cancer prognosis methods fail to generalize across clinical centers due to pixel-derived representations being highly susceptible to domain-specific artifacts (staining protocols, scanner hardware).
AI.method: SAEFS derives semantic anchors from WSIs via Visual Question Answering (VQA), uses a dual-stream WSI evidence extraction architecture, employs Dirichlet-based Subjective Logic for uncertainty modeling, and fuses semantic and visual evidence through a cautious conjunction rule to avoid overconfident fusion.
AI.result: Trained on one source domain and evaluated zero-shot across four unseen domains, SAEFS outperforms SOTA models by 10.2% average C-index improvement. VQA-derived semantic features show significantly lower cross-center divergence than pixel-derived features.
AI.conclusion: High-level pathology semantics (tumor grade, micro-environmental architecture) serve as domain-invariant representations robust for cross-center clinical applications, mirroring pathologists' diagnostic logic.

title: Autonomous Event-Driven Multi-Agent Orchestration for Enterprise AI at Scale
summary: Enterprise AI aims to move toward continuous event monitoring, detection, and action across specialist agents, yet existing multi-agent systems largely assume discrete request-response workflows and remain underexplored at enterprise scale. We evaluate DAG Plan and Execute and ReAct across 208 production-derived enterprise scenarios spanning Persona (<10 agents), Department (20-80), and Enterprise (200) scales, and introduce a Task Manager for continuous operation via priority inference, related-event merging, and preemption. Results show that scale, not task complexity, dominates orchestration performance: both architectures perform well at small scale but degrade at enterprise scale as agent discovery noise becomes the primary bottleneck, with simple tasks degrading more sharply than complex ones. DAG Plan and Execute offers higher precision and structured parallelization at smaller scales, but its higher overhead worsens at enterprise scale; ReAct is more robust by handling failures incrementally. The Task Manager reduces high-priority queue latency by 14-75% and improves related-event correctness by over 20 percentage points at enterprise scale.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates DAG Plan-and-Execute and ReAct multi-agent architectures across 208 enterprise scenarios at three scales (Persona, Department, Enterprise), finding that scale—not task complexity—is the primary factor degrading performance, and introducing a Task Manager that reduces high-priority latency by 14-75% and improves related-event correctness by >20pp at enterprise scale.
AI.motivation: Enterprise AI needs continuous event monitoring and multi-agent coordination, but existing systems rely on discrete request-response workflows and lack evaluation at realistic enterprise scales (up to 200 agents).
AI.method: The authors evaluate two architectures (DAG Plan-and-Execute and ReAct) on 208 production-derived scenarios across three scales (Persona <10, Department 20-80, Enterprise 200 agents), and propose a Task Manager that performs priority inference, related-event merging, and preemption for continuous operation.
AI.result: Scale dominates orchestration performance; both architectures degrade at enterprise scale due to agent discovery noise, with simple tasks suffering more. DAG offers higher precision and parallelization at small scales but higher overhead at scale, while ReAct is more robust incrementally. Task Manager reduces high-priority queue latency by 14-75% and improves related-event correctness by >20 percentage points at enterprise scale.
AI.conclusion: Agent discovery noise at scale is the primary bottleneck, not task complexity. The Task Manager effectively mitigates enterprise-scale degradation through priority-driven scheduling and event merging, making continuous multi-agent operation feasible.

title: SEAGAN: domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network for Dynamic Plant Processes
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) provide a flexible framework for learning from scientific data linked through physical, biological, or functional relationships. One promising domain is plant physiology, where measured responses often arise from multiple interacting processes whose exact separation remains difficult even with manual intervention. In plant physiology, a key example is the A-Ci curve, which relates net CO2 assimilation rate (Anet) to leaf intercellular CO2 concentration (Ci) and is used to estimate photosynthetic parameters in leaf and crop-canopy models. However, reliable estimation requires identifying the active biochemical limitation state at each curve point, which remains a major source of uncertainty. Here, we formulate limitation-state identification along A-Ci curves as a graph-based node classification problem, with curve points as nodes. Domain-specific graph representations are created using distance-based k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) and auxiliary-signal-guided (ASG) connectivity, with edge attributes encoding pairwise relations. The framework was evaluated against conventional learning baselines, graph-based architectures, and an automated fitting-based benchmark. Results on a large synthetic dataset with known ground-truth limitation states show that graph-based models improve classification, particularly near biochemical transition regions. The best-performing configuration, SEAGAN (domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network for Dynamic Plant Processes), integrates process-aware node features, edge attributes, kNN connectivity, and graph attention with weighted cross-entropy loss, achieving an F1-score of 0.857 and an accuracy of 0.882. The results show that representing A-Ci curves as graphs improves biochemical limitation-state analysis, with edge-aware attention over local kNN neighborhoods providing the most effective strategy.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a graph-based approach to identify biochemical limitation states along A-Ci curves in plant physiology, formulating it as a node classification problem. The best model, SEAGAN (graph attention network with domain-specific features and edge attributes), achieves 0.857 F1-score and 0.882 accuracy on synthetic data.
AI.motivation: Reliable estimation of photosynthetic parameters from A-Ci curves requires identifying the active biochemical limitation state at each curve point, which remains a major source of uncertainty even with manual intervention.
AI.method: The problem is formulated as graph-based node classification where A-Ci curve points are nodes. Two graph connectivity strategies are used: distance-based kNN and auxiliary-signal-guided (ASG) connectivity, with edge attributes encoding pairwise relations. The SEAGAN model integrates process-aware node features, edge attributes, kNN connectivity, and graph attention with weighted cross-entropy loss.
AI.result: Graph-based models outperform conventional learning baselines and automated fitting-based benchmarks, especially near biochemical transition regions. SEAGAN achieves 0.857 F1-score and 0.882 accuracy on a large synthetic dataset with known ground-truth limitation states.
AI.conclusion: Representing A-Ci curves as graphs improves biochemical limitation-state analysis, with edge-aware attention over local kNN neighborhoods being the most effective strategy.

title: CrossFlow: One-Step Generation Across Latent and Pixel Spaces
summary: Most diffusion and flow-matching generators define the prior, probability path, and prediction target in the same representation space. Latent diffusion improves efficiency by moving this path into an autoencoder latent space, but the final sample is still produced by a separately trained decoder. This separation creates a mismatch: the generator is optimized for latent-space prediction, while final quality depends on how the decoder handles generated latents that may differ from clean encoder outputs. We introduce CrossFlow, a cross-space flow formulation that maps noisy latent inputs directly to pixel-space images. The key technical step is a velocity-free one-step objective: the latent trajectory defines the training path, but the supervised prediction is an image rather than a latent displacement. This lets one model act both as a one-step latent-to-pixel generator and as a decoder replacement for latent diffusion pipelines. On class-conditional ImageNet-1k at $256\times256$, CrossFlow-XL achieves 1.62 FID with one function evaluation. Ablations show that the latent encoder and pixel-space perceptual and adversarial losses are important for fidelity. These results indicate that cross-space flow objectives can combine the efficiency of latent representations with direct pixel-space supervision, without requiring a separate decoder at inference.

AI.tldr: CrossFlow introduces a cross-space flow formulation that maps noisy latent inputs directly to pixel-space images, eliminating the need for a separate decoder at inference. Using a velocity-free one-step objective, it achieves 1.62 FID on ImageNet-1k 256×256 with a single function evaluation.
AI.motivation: Latent diffusion models suffer from a mismatch between the latent-space optimization objective and the final pixel-space quality, since the decoder is trained separately and must handle generated latents that differ from clean encoder outputs. CrossFlow aims to bridge this gap by directly supervising the generator in pixel space.
AI.method: CrossFlow proposes a cross-space flow where the latent trajectory defines the training path, but the supervised prediction target is an image rather than a latent displacement. This velocity-free one-step objective enables a single model to act as both a one-step latent-to-pixel generator and a decoder replacement. It incorporates latent encoder and pixel-space perceptual and adversarial losses.
AI.result: On class-conditional ImageNet-1k at 256×256, CrossFlow-XL achieves 1.62 FID with one function evaluation, demonstrating strong performance without a separately trained decoder at inference.
AI.conclusion: Cross-space flow objectives can combine the efficiency of latent representations with direct pixel-space supervision, eliminating the need for a separate decoder at inference time.

title: Process-Verified Reinforcement Learning for Theorem Proving via Lean
summary: While reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) typically has relied on a single binary verification signal, symbolic proof assistants in formal reasoning offer rich, fine-grained structured feedback. This gap between structured processes and unstructured rewards highlights the importance of feedback that is both dense and sound. In this work, we demonstrate that the Lean proof assistant itself can serve as a symbolic process oracle, supplying both outcome-level and fine-grained tactic-level verified feedback during training. Proof attempts are parsed into tactic sequences, and Lean's elaboration marks both locally sound steps and the earliest failing step, yielding dense, verifier-grounded credit signals rooted in type theory. We incorporate these structured rewards into a GRPO-style reinforcement learning objective with first-error propagation and first-token credit methods that balances outcome- and process-level advantages. Experiments with STP-Lean and DeepSeek-Prover-V1.5 show that tactic-level supervision outperforms outcome-only baselines in most settings, delivering improvements on benchmarks such as MiniF2F and ProofNet. Beyond empirical gains, our study highlights a broader perspective: symbolic proof assistants are not only verifiers at evaluation time, but can also act as process-level reward oracles during training. This opens a path toward reinforcement learning frameworks that combine the scalability of language models with the reliability of symbolic verification for formal reasoning.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that the Lean proof assistant can provide dense, fine-grained tactic-level feedback during RL training, outperforming outcome-only binary reward approaches in formal mathematical reasoning.
AI.motivation: RLVR relies on sparse binary verification signals, but formal reasoning environments like Lean offer rich structured feedback. The gap between unstructured rewards and structured reasoning processes motivates using proof assistants as process-level reward oracles during training.
AI.method: Parse proof attempts into tactic sequences; use Lean's elaboration to identify both locally sound steps and the earliest failing step. Incorporate these structured rewards into a GRPO-style RL objective with first-error propagation and first-token credit methods.
AI.result: Tactic-level supervision outperforms outcome-only baselines in most settings, with improvements on MiniF2F and ProofNet benchmarks using STP-Lean and DeepSeek-Prover-V1.5.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic proof assistants can serve not only as evaluation-time verifiers but also as process-level reward oracles during training, enabling RL frameworks that combine LM scalability with symbolic verification reliability.

title: MassSpecGym in the Wild: Uncovering and Correcting Evaluation Pitfalls in AI-Driven Molecule Discovery
summary: Reliable benchmarking is critical for developing machine learning models for tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS) based molecule discovery. Subtle issues in experimental design and model evaluation procedures can degrade the trustworthiness of such benchmarks and lead to erroneous conclusions. We conduct a thorough review of model evaluation issues in the recent MS/MS machine learning literature, using the standard MassSpecGym benchmark suite as a case study to illustrate the impact of these issues. We find evaluation issues in at least 17 of 26 papers reporting MassSpecGym benchmark results in the first year of its adoption. We isolate three classes of failures: (i) data leakage, (ii) shortcut learning, and (iii) implementation bugs and metric divergence. Through extensive experimentation and code replication, we quantify the impact of these issues and show how they corrupt the evaluation standards MassSpecGym was designed to enforce. We distill our findings into recommendations generalizable to MS/MS challenges, benchmarks, and custom evaluation setups. We also release MassSpecGym v1.5, an implementation of our recommendations in the MassSpecGym benchmarking suite which addresses the failure modes identified in this audit. MassSpecGym v1.5 is publicly available at https://github.com/pluskal-lab/MassSpecGym.

AI.tldr: This paper audits 26 papers using the MassSpecGym benchmark for MS/MS molecule discovery, finding evaluation issues in at least 17 of them. Three failure classes are identified: data leakage, shortcut learning, and implementation bugs/metric divergence. The authors release MassSpecGym v1.5 to address these issues.
AI.motivation: Reliable benchmarking is critical for developing ML models for MS/MS-based molecule discovery, but subtle issues in experimental design and model evaluation can degrade benchmark trustworthiness and lead to erroneous conclusions.
AI.method: The authors conduct a thorough review of model evaluation issues in MS/MS machine learning literature, using MassSpecGym as a case study. They perform extensive experimentation and code replication to quantify the impact of identified issues across 26 papers.
AI.result: At least 17 of 26 papers reporting MassSpecGym benchmark results contain evaluation issues. Three classes of failures are identified: (i) data leakage, (ii) shortcut learning, and (iii) implementation bugs and metric divergence.
AI.conclusion: The findings are distilled into generalizable recommendations for MS/MS challenges, benchmarks, and custom evaluation setups. MassSpecGym v1.5 is released to address the identified failure modes.

title: Vision-Reasoning-Guided Occlusion Removal from Light Fields
summary: Occlusion-robust scene recovery remains a major challenge in computational imaging, particularly in natural environments where dense foreground vegetation severely limits visibility. We propose a vision-reasoning-guided light field occlusion removal framework that combines the visibility recovery capability of light field integration (LFI) with the semantic reasoning capacity of vision-language models (VLMs). Multi-view observations are first integrated via LFI to suppress foreground occlusions and produce an initial visibility-enhanced representation. A VLM is then incorporated as a conditional semantic prior to restore degraded structures and recover fine details, guided by the observed measurements. To improve recovery consistency and reduce hallucination artifacts, we introduce a multi-sample fusion strategy that aggregates multiple generated hypotheses into a unified estimate. Experimental results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, achieving the highest average SSIM across four synthetic light field benchmark scenes (4-Syn) and strong generalization across structured and unstructured acquisition settings. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining physical imaging constraints with vision-language reasoning for robust perception under severe occlusion, with applicability to search-and-rescue and exploratory robotic navigation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a vision-reasoning-guided light field occlusion removal framework that integrates light field integration with vision-language models to recover scenes occluded by dense foreground vegetation, achieving state-of-the-art performance on synthetic and real datasets.
AI.motivation: Occlusion-robust scene recovery remains challenging in computational imaging, especially when dense foreground vegetation severely limits visibility in natural environments.
AI.method: The framework combines light field integration (LFI) to suppress foreground occlusions and produce an initial visibility-enhanced representation, then incorporates a vision-language model (VLM) as a conditional semantic prior to restore degraded structures and recover fine details. A multi-sample fusion strategy aggregates multiple generated hypotheses into a unified estimate to improve consistency and reduce hallucination artifacts.
AI.result: Achieved state-of-the-art performance with the highest average SSIM across four synthetic light field benchmark scenes (4-Syn), with strong generalization across structured and unstructured acquisition settings on synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: Combining physical imaging constraints (LFI) with vision-language reasoning (VLM) is effective for robust perception under severe occlusion, with applicability to search-and-rescue and exploratory robotic navigation.

title: Residual-Space Evolutionary Optimization via Flow-based Generative Models
comment: Accepted by ICML 2026 Workshop SPIGM, 5 pages, 3 figures
summary: Data editing with generative methods typically requires differentiable objectives and gradient-based search. However, these assumptions break down in flow-based settings, where edits are performed through forward and backward integration and often involve non-differentiable or black-box objectives. We introduce residual-space evolutionary optimization, a model-agnostic framework that addresses this gap by combining flow-based generative editing with evolutionary algorithms. Building on the observation that conditional flow matching (CFM) can disentangle condition-controlled factors from instance-specific residuals, our framework directly operates in residual space and separates two complementary search regimes: self-pollination performs local exploitation through feature-preserving residual refinement, and cross-pollination promotes broader exploration by recombining residuals across heterogeneous samples. As a proof of concept, we validate on MorphoMNIST, a benchmark dataset for counterfactual generation, and on crystal data, demonstrating that this exploration--exploitation decomposition provides a useful mechanism for balancing target alignment, instance preservation, and diversity, and extends beyond images to real-world scientific domains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces residual-space evolutionary optimization, a model-agnostic framework that combines flow-based generative editing with evolutionary algorithms to enable editing with non-differentiable or black-box objectives. It separates self-pollination (local exploitation) and cross-pollination (broad exploration) in residual space, validated on MorphoMNIST and crystal data.
AI.motivation: Existing generative editing methods require differentiable objectives and gradient-based search, which are incompatible with flow-based settings where edits involve forward/backward integration and non-differentiable or black-box objectives.
AI.method: The framework operates in residual space derived from conditional flow matching (CFM), which disentangles condition-controlled factors from instance-specific residuals. It combines two search regimes: self-pollination for local exploitation (feature-preserving residual refinement) and cross-pollination for broader exploration (recombining residuals across heterogeneous samples).
AI.result: Validated on MorphoMNIST (counterfactual generation benchmark) and crystal data, demonstrating that the exploration-exploitation decomposition balances target alignment, instance preservation, and diversity, extending beyond images to scientific domains.
AI.conclusion: The residual-space evolutionary optimization framework effectively bridges flow-based generative editing and evolutionary algorithms, providing a model-agnostic solution for non-differentiable objectives in scientific and image domains.

title: Hard or Just Unreached? Diagnosing the Sampling Blind Spot in Math-Reasoning Difficulty Estimation
comment: 9 pages of main paper, 4 figures and 5 tables in the main paper, with more in the appendix
summary: Math and science reasoning benchmarks rely on pass@k, the fraction of sampled chains that reach gold, as the canonical per-example difficulty signal. The same signal drives RL with verifiable rewards, math data curation, synthetic curricula, and verifier training. We show this proxy has a persistent blind spot on its hardest stratum: on the eight free-form math cells we test (GSM8K and MATH across four open-weight models), 10.3-22.9% of the examples that no sampling seed solves in six tries are instead solved at matched compute by a six-chain deterministic regime. These are greedy decoding plus five cheap residual-stream perturbations applied via activation grafting, while greedy alone solves at most 6% on these math cells. Recovery scales with the additional budget, across perturbations whose mechanistic distinctness we verify across all twelve cells (cross-kind fix-set Jaccard <= 0.47 in every setup). Activation grafting is used as an intervention on internal representations, not a decoding method; we use it purely as a diagnostic and diversification tool, and our recovered items show that the pass@k= 0 % stratum is structurally identifiable in the residual stream rather than that the unmodified model reaches them under ordinary inference.

AI.tldr: Pass@k, the standard difficulty metric for math reasoning benchmarks, systematically underestimates model capability on the hardest problems. Using activation grafting (perturbing residual stream representations) with six deterministic chains, the authors recover 10.3-22.9% of problems that standard sampling (pass@6=0%) fails to solve, showing these "unsolvable" items are structurally identifiable in the model's internal representations.
AI.motivation: Pass@k is widely used as a difficulty signal for RL with verifiable rewards, data curation, curricula, and verifier training, but it may have a blind spot on the hardest examples. The authors aim to expose and quantify this blind spot.
AI.method: Eight free-form math cells (GSM8K and MATH across four open-weight models) are tested. The authors apply six deterministic chains: greedy decoding plus five cheap residual-stream perturbations via activation grafting. They measure recovery rates on examples where pass@6=0% (no sampling seed solves in six tries) and compare mechanistic distinctness across perturbations using Jaccard similarity.
AI.result: 10.3-22.9% of pass@6=0% examples are solved by the six-chain deterministic regime. Greedy alone solves at most 6%. Cross-kind fix-set Jaccard ≤ 0.47 across all twelve cells, confirming mechanistic distinctness of perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Pass@k has a persistent blind spot on the hardest stratum; recovered items show this stratum is structurally identifiable in the residual stream, not reachable by the unmodified model under ordinary inference. Activation grafting is used as a diagnostic/diversification tool, not as a decoding method.

title: QG-MIL: A Gated Transformer Aggregator for Domain-Agnostic Multiple Instance Learning in Medical Imaging
summary: Attention-based Multiple Instance Learning aggregators in medical imaging are prone to attention concentration, producing overconfident and unstable predictions. We introduce QG-MIL, a gated transformer aggregator that addresses this through four synergistic architectural components: RMSNorm-based pre-normalization, per-head QK normalization, fine-grained attention output gating, and SwiGLU-style feed-forward modules. Together, these design choices stabilize training and distribute attention more uniformly across instances without auxiliary losses, masking, or multi-stage regularization. We evaluate QG-MIL across six benchmarks spanning whole-slide pathology and cell-level hematology, covering two fundamentally different MIL scales. The best-performing QG-MIL variants outperform leading baselines on all six benchmarks, with an average improvement of +6.1 mean macro F1 points. Attention overlays and attention mass analysis confirm more distributed instance weighting. Ablation studies show that while individual components can match the full model on specific datasets, the QG-MIL design provides the most consistent cross-domain performance and tightest variance when compared to selected baselines. We release a configurable implementation to support reproducibility at: https://github.com/unica-visual-intelligence-lab/QG-MIL

AI.tldr: QG-MIL introduces a gated transformer aggregator with four architectural components (RMSNorm pre-normalization, per-head QK normalization, attention output gating, SwiGLU feed-forward) to address attention concentration in MIL for medical imaging, achieving +6.1 mean macro F1 improvement across six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Attention-based MIL aggregators in medical imaging suffer from attention concentration, leading to overconfident and unstable predictions. The authors aim to stabilize training and distribute attention more uniformly across instances.
AI.method: QG-MIL employs four synergistic components: (1) RMSNorm-based pre-normalization, (2) per-head QK normalization, (3) fine-grained attention output gating, and (4) SwiGLU-style feed-forward modules. No auxiliary losses, masking, or multi-stage regularization is needed.
AI.result: QG-MIL outperforms leading baselines across six benchmarks (whole-slide pathology and cell-level hematology), with +6.1 mean macro F1 improvement. Attention overlays and mass analysis confirm more distributed instance weighting. The full model provides the most consistent cross-domain performance and tightest variance.
AI.conclusion: The QG-MIL design offers consistent cross-domain performance with tight variance. Individual components can match the full model on specific datasets, but the complete architecture is most robust overall.

title: Multi-Head Attention-Based Feature Extractor Integration with Soft Actor-Critic for Porosity Prediction and Process Parameter Optimization in Additive Manufacturing
summary: Additive manufacturing process optimization requires precise parameter control to minimize defects such as porosity. Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) approaches using discrete action spaces suffer from slow convergence and susceptibility to local optima, limiting their effectiveness for high-precision manufacturing tasks. This study addresses these limitations by employing a continuous action space combined with a novel architecture that integrates a multi-head attention mechanism with the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm. The attention-based feature extractor enhances the agent's ability to capture subtle variations in low-dimensional input features, enabling more effective exploration-exploitation balance for navigating value spaces with local minima. We validate our approach on porosity prediction and process parameter optimization in laser powder bed fusion, demonstrating faster convergence and higher final reward values compared to standard RL methods including DQN, PPO, TD3, and vanilla SAC. The proposed methodology achieves a convergence value of 322.79 within 14 episodes, outperforming existing approaches while maintaining stability throughout training.

AI.tldr: This study improves additive manufacturing process optimization by combining a continuous action space with a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm enhanced by a multi-head attention mechanism, achieving faster convergence and higher reward values in porosity prediction and parameter optimization for laser powder bed fusion compared to standard RL methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional RL approaches with discrete action spaces suffer from slow convergence and susceptibility to local optima, limiting their effectiveness for high-precision additive manufacturing tasks such as minimizing porosity defects.
AI.method: The method employs a continuous action space with a novel architecture integrating a multi-head attention mechanism as a feature extractor with the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm, enabling better capture of subtle variations in low-dimensional input features and improved exploration-exploitation balance.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves a convergence value of 322.79 within 14 episodes, demonstrating faster convergence and higher final reward values compared to DQN, PPO, TD3, and vanilla SAC, while maintaining training stability.
AI.conclusion: The attention-augmented SAC with continuous action space effectively addresses the limitations of discrete-action RL for additive manufacturing optimization, providing a more efficient and stable solution for high-precision parameter control.

title: Convex training of Lipschitz-regularized shallow neural networks
summary: In this work, we introduce a training procedure for shallow neural networks that promotes robustness against adversarial attacks. We solve a non-convex Lipschitz-regularized training program by introducing a convex restriction that can be efficiently solved to global optimality. Our approach can be employed as a post-processing step by taking a pre-trained network as an initial solution to then solving the convex program whose optimal network is guaranteed to be no worse than the initial one. We illustrate the improvements of our training procedure with experiments using real world datasets for regression tasks under an adversarial setting. We show numerically that solving our proposed convex program yields networks with lower objective values on the Lipschitz-regularized program compared to existing methods. Additionally, we show that on certain datasets, networks obtained using our convex training program are both more accurate and robust with respect to adversarial attacks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a convex restriction approach to solve a non-convex Lipschitz-regularized training program for shallow neural networks, improving adversarial robustness. The method can be used as a post-processing step from a pre-trained network and guarantees no worse performance. Experiments show improved accuracy and robustness on regression tasks under adversarial attacks.
AI.motivation: To develop a training procedure for shallow neural networks that promotes robustness against adversarial attacks by solving a non-convex Lipschitz-regularized program through an efficiently solvable convex restriction.
AI.method: Introduces a convex restriction of the non-convex Lipschitz-regularized training program that can be solved to global optimality. The approach can be used as a post-processing step, taking a pre-trained network as an initial solution, with a guarantee that the optimal network from the convex program is no worse than the initial one.
AI.result: The convex program yields networks with lower objective values on the Lipschitz-regularized program compared to existing methods. On certain datasets, the obtained networks are both more accurate and more robust with respect to adversarial attacks in regression tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed convex restriction approach effectively improves adversarial robustness for shallow neural networks, offering a guaranteed improvement over pre-trained networks and achieving better accuracy-robustness trade-offs on real-world regression datasets.

title: ReA-OVCD: Reliability-Aware Open-Vocabulary Change Detection via Semantic and Spatial Refinement
summary: Unlike traditional remote sensing change detection that relies on predefined categories, Open-Vocabulary Change Detection (OVCD) identifies land cover changes flexibly using arbitrary text prompts. However, existing methods suffer from an inherent trade-off when modeling changes: instance-level comparison overlooks fine-grained semantic variations (e.g., partial building extensions), while direct pixel comparison proves unreliable, yielding unstable responses and boundary artifacts due to semantic ambiguity and spatial inconsistency. To this end, we propose an efficient training-free Reliability-Aware Open-Vocabulary Change Detection (ReA-OVCD) framework. It first derives candidate change regions from pixel-wise semantic discrepancies to ensure flexible and detailed localization. To ensure reliability, it subsequently introduces a collaborative refinement strategy to explicitly model change validity from both semantic and spatial perspectives. Specifically, we develop a Semantic Change Reasoning (SCR) module that reassesses changes by jointly analyzing distributional divergence and response variation, enabling the suppression of incidental inconsistencies while preserving reliable semantic shifts. In addition, a Boundary-aware Change Refinement (BCR) module is designed to mitigate artifacts stemming from boundary misalignment and uncertainty through validating whether candidate regions are supported by reliable interior pixels. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets (LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, DSIFN, and SECOND) demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, achieving $\mathrm{F}_{1}^{C}$ improvements of 2.13\% to 9.75\% with higher computational efficiency. The code is publicly available at \https://github.com/Funny0101/ReA-OVCD

AI.tldr: ReA-OVCD is a training-free framework for Open-Vocabulary Change Detection that resolves the trade-off between instance-level and pixel-level change modeling through collaborative semantic and spatial refinement, achieving consistent state-of-the-art performance with 2.13%-9.75% F1_C improvements across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing OVCD methods suffer from an inherent trade-off: instance-level comparison misses fine-grained semantic variations (e.g., partial building extensions), while direct pixel comparison yields unstable responses and boundary artifacts due to semantic ambiguity and spatial inconsistency.
AI.method: Proposes a training-free ReA-OVCD framework with: (1) candidate change regions derived from pixel-wise semantic discrepancies; (2) Semantic Change Reasoning (SCR) module that reassesses changes via joint analysis of distributional divergence and response variation; (3) Boundary-aware Change Refinement (BCR) module that validates candidate regions using reliable interior pixels to mitigate boundary artifacts.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms SOTA methods on LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, DSIFN, and SECOND datasets, achieving F1_C improvements of 2.13% to 9.75% with higher computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The collaborative refinement strategy from both semantic (SCR) and spatial (BCR) perspectives effectively resolves the instance-level vs. pixel-level trade-off, enabling reliable and flexible OVCD without training.

title: ScaffoldAgent: Utility-Guided Dynamic Outline Optimization for Open-Ended Deep Research
summary: Open-ended deep research (OEDR) requires systems to acquire knowledge through multi-round retrieval and generate coherent long-form reports. The outline plays a central role as a structural scaffold that coordinates retrieval, evidence organization, and generation. However, existing methods either fix the outline before writing or refine it with local heuristics, leading to scaffold drift under continuous information accumulation and delayed feedback for evaluating outline modifications. We propose ScaffoldAgent, a utility-guided dynamic outline optimization framework for OEDR. ScaffoldAgent models outline evolution as a structured decision process with three operations: Expansion, Contraction, and Revision, enabling controlled updates to the report scaffold. It further introduces a utility-guided feedback mechanism that estimates the downstream value of each outline operation from retrieval gain, structural coherence, and trial-generation quality. The resulting utility signal guides node selection, operation scheduling, and termination during inference. Experiments on DeepResearch Bench and DeepResearch Gym show that ScaffoldAgent consistently improves long-form report generation and factual grounding over existing deep research agents.

AI.tldr: ScaffoldAgent is a utility-guided dynamic outline optimization framework for open-ended deep research that models outline evolution as structured decisions (expansion, contraction, revision) and uses utility signals to improve long-form report generation and factual grounding.
AI.motivation: Existing OEDR methods suffer from scaffold drift due to fixed outlines or local heuristic refinements under continuous information accumulation, and lack delayed feedback for evaluating outline modifications.
AI.method: ScaffoldAgent models outline evolution as a structured decision process with three operations (Expansion, Contraction, Revision), and introduces a utility-guided feedback mechanism that estimates downstream value of each outline operation from retrieval gain, structural coherence, and trial-generation quality to guide node selection, operation scheduling, and termination.
AI.result: Experiments on DeepResearch Bench and DeepResearch Gym show consistent improvements in long-form report generation and factual grounding over existing deep research agents.
AI.conclusion: Utility-guided dynamic outline optimization effectively addresses scaffold drift and delayed feedback issues in open-ended deep research, leading to better report quality and factual accuracy.

title: LOKI: Memory-Free Null-Space Constrained Lifelong Knowledge Editing
summary: Lifelong knowledge editing aims to efficiently and sequentially update language models over time, as new knowledge becomes available or when the model makes mistakes, while preserving acceptable performance on past knowledge. One unresolved challenge is that existing methods modify a fixed set of layers for all new knowledge samples, reducing flexibility and increasing catastrophic forgetting. Another is requiring access to previous knowledge and extensive pre-processing to obtain data statistics. To address these challenges, we introduce LOKI, a novel approach that uses dynamic layer selection based on the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion and projects gradient updates onto the null-space of the model weights, bypassing the requirement for previous knowledge access. We show that LOKI achieves superior performance to existing approaches across a wide variety of experiments, achieving up to a 14\% improvement in average accuracy.

AI.tldr: LOKI introduces dynamic layer selection using Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion and null-space gradient projection for lifelong knowledge editing, achieving up to 14% improvement in average accuracy without requiring access to previous knowledge.
AI.motivation: Existing lifelong knowledge editing methods suffer from two key limitations: (1) they modify a fixed set of layers for all new knowledge, reducing flexibility and increasing catastrophic forgetting; (2) they require access to previous knowledge and extensive pre-processing to obtain data statistics.
AI.method: LOKI employs (1) dynamic layer selection based on the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (HSIC) to adaptively choose which layers to modify for each new knowledge sample, and (2) projects gradient updates onto the null-space of model weights to avoid interfering with previously learned knowledge without needing explicit access to past data.
AI.result: LOKI achieves superior performance across a wide variety of experiments, with up to a 14% improvement in average accuracy compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic layer selection combined with null-space gradient projection effectively addresses catastrophic forgetting in lifelong knowledge editing while eliminating the need for previous knowledge access, offering a more flexible and efficient solution.

title: PU-UNet: Stable Multiplicative Interactions for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Many dense prediction networks rely on additive feature transformations and model higher-order feature interactions only implicitly. Product units provide an explicit mechanism for multiplicative feature modeling, but their logarithmic--exponential formulation can cause numerical instability, which has limited their use in deep dense prediction networks. In this work, we propose Product-Unit U-Net (PU-UNet), a residual U-Net that integrates stable product-unit residual blocks into rich low-resolution stages for medical image segmentation. The proposed formulation combines smooth positivity mapping with log-domain clipping, enabling stable multiplicative feature learning with negligible computational overhead. On ISIC 2018, Kvasir-SEG, and BUSI, PU-UNet achieves Dice scores of 0.942, 0.959, and up to 0.925, respectively. Compared with a matched Residual U-Net baseline, PU-UNet consistently improves Dice and IoU while keeping parameters, FLOPs, and inference latency nearly unchanged, and reduces the image-level false-positive rate on normal BUSI cases from 0.077 to zero. Ablation studies suggest that the gains are associated with product-unit interactions, are strongest under low-resolution placement, and benefit from the proposed stabilization design. These results suggest that stable product-unit residual learning can be an effective way to enhance U-Net-style segmentation networks with explicit multiplicative interactions.

AI.tldr: PU-UNet integrates stable product-unit residual blocks into low-resolution stages of U-Net for medical image segmentation, achieving Dice scores of up to 0.959 on ISIC 2018, Kvasir-SEG, and BUSI while keeping computational cost nearly unchanged and reducing false-positive rates to zero on normal cases.
AI.motivation: Existing dense prediction networks rely on additive feature transformations and only implicitly model higher-order feature interactions. Product units could provide explicit multiplicative modeling but their logarithmic-exponential formulation causes numerical instability, limiting use in deep networks.
AI.method: PU-UNet integrates stable product-unit residual blocks into low-resolution stages of a residual U-Net, using smooth positivity mapping with log-domain clipping to enable stable multiplicative feature learning with negligible computational overhead.
AI.result: Dice scores of 0.942 (ISIC 2018), 0.959 (Kvasir-SEG), and up to 0.925 (BUSI). Outperforms matched Residual U-Net baseline with nearly unchanged parameters, FLOPs, and latency. Reduces false-positive rate on normal BUSI cases from 0.077 to zero.
AI.conclusion: Stable product-unit residual learning effectively enhances U-Net-style segmentation networks with explicit multiplicative interactions, with strongest gains from low-resolution placement and the proposed stabilization design.

title: Learning to Prompt: Improving Student Engagement with Adaptive LLM-based High-School Tutoring
summary: LLMs can personalize education, although current static-prompt tutoring systems struggle to adapt to diverse academic disciplines. We develop and test a system with subject-aware prompting, based on 14 pedagogical features (e.g., tutor scaffolding, student understanding) extracted from raw transcripts. We first train a prompt routing model in a simulation environment, and then deploy it for online adaptation with actual high-school students. The simulation benchmark shows the router outperforming two static baselines ($0.694$ vs. $0.647$ and $0.64$, $p<0.001$). A/B testing ($N=656$ conversations from 359 students) shows sim-to-real transfer where the model switches from analytical to scaffolding learning strategies. Our adaptive prompt selection mechanism improves instructional efficiency, maintains pedagogical quality and reduces interactions by around 3 turns ($p=0.007$). While a greedy router achieves a comparable exercise conversion rate with the baseline ($19.1\%$ vs. $19.6\%$), a stochastic router that samples strategies leads to a higher conversion rate ($28.1\%$).

AI.tldr: The paper develops a subject-aware prompt routing system for LLM-based tutoring that adapts pedagogical strategies to different disciplines. It trains a routing model in simulation, then deploys it with real high-school students, showing improved efficiency (3 fewer turns) and that a stochastic routing strategy achieves higher exercise conversion (28.1% vs 19.6% baseline).
AI.motivation: Current static-prompt tutoring systems fail to adapt to diverse academic disciplines, limiting personalization in LLM-based education. The authors aim to bridge this gap by developing a system that dynamically selects pedagogical strategies based on subject context.
AI.method: The authors extract 14 pedagogical features (e.g., tutor scaffolding, student understanding) from raw tutoring transcripts. They train a prompt routing model in a simulated environment, then deploy it for online adaptation with actual high-school students. Both greedy and stochastic routing variants are evaluated.
AI.result: Simulation: router outperforms two static baselines (0.694 vs 0.647 and 0.64, p<0.001). Real deployment (N=656 conversations, 359 students): sim-to-real transfer observed with strategy shifts from analytical to scaffolding; adaptive selection reduces interactions by ~3 turns (p=0.007). Greedy router achieves comparable conversion (19.1% vs 19.6%), while stochastic router achieves higher conversion (28.1%).
AI.conclusion: Subject-aware prompt routing effectively adapts LLM tutoring to diverse disciplines. Stochastic strategy selection improves learning outcomes (conversion rate) over static or greedy baselines, demonstrating the value of exploration in pedagogical policy.

title: Multi-Granular Attention-Driven Reinforcement Learning Framework for Web Intelligent Enhancement Systems
comment: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS), 6 Pages
summary: From the past few years, web intelligent enhancement systems increasingly rely on heterogeneous and dynamic web data to deliver personalized, context-aware services. However, traditional machine learning, deep learning, and reinforcement learning models often struggle with semantic understanding, adaptability, and scalability in continuously evolving web environments. In this research, a Multi-Granular Attention-based Reinforcement Web Intelligent Enhancement System (MGAR-WIES) is proposed to address the challenges by integrating semantic graph modeling, attention mechanisms, and adaptive reinforcement learning. Initially, heterogeneous web data comprising structured, semi-structured and unstructured sources are collected and preprocessed for generating unified feature representations. These representations are transformed into a dynamic semantic graph, where entities and their relationships are modeled by using graph embeddings enhanced by attention mechanisms for capturing both local relevance and global contextual dependencies. Subsequently, an adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning strategy leverages the attention-aware semantic states to optimize personalized web actions like content recommendation, navigation optimization, and service adaptation. Finally, the continuous online feedback is further integrated to update graph representations and learning policies in real time by ensuring sustained adaptability and performance. The proposed MGAR-WIES acheived better results in terms of accuracy (80%) when compared with existing approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MGAR-WIES, a system that combines semantic graph modeling, attention mechanisms, and adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning to improve web intelligence enhancement tasks. It achieves 80% accuracy, outperforming existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Traditional ML, DL, and RL models struggle with semantic understanding, adaptability, and scalability in continuously evolving web environments when handling heterogeneous and dynamic web data.
AI.method: 1) Collect and preprocess heterogeneous web data (structured, semi-structured, unstructured) into unified feature representations. 2) Transform representations into a dynamic semantic graph with attention-enhanced graph embeddings to capture local and global dependencies. 3) Apply adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning on attention-aware semantic states for personalized web actions (recommendation, navigation, service adaptation). 4) Integrate continuous online feedback for real-time updates of graph representations and policies.
AI.result: MGAR-WIES achieves 80% accuracy, outperforming existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MGAR-WIES effectively addresses semantic understanding, adaptability, and scalability challenges in web intelligent enhancement through the integration of semantic graphs, attention mechanisms, and adaptive reinforcement learning with real-time feedback.

title: FUSE: Frequency-domain Unification and Spectral Energy Alignment for Multi-modal Object Re-Identification
