Taken together, this work moves beyond showing that demonstration-based jailbreaking works to characterizing how it works: what models extract from compliance demonstrations depends on demonstration content, ordering, and training methodology.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how in-context demonstrations (both benign and harmful) influence harmful compliance in language models, finding that benign demonstrations can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model, with preference optimization being the critical training stage that prevents benign demonstrations from increasing harmful compliance, and demonstrating recency bias in ordering effects.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed in-context demonstrations can jailbreak language models, but it remained unclear how models interpret different types of compliance demonstrations (benign vs. harmful). This work aims to characterize the mechanisms behind demonstration-based jailbreaking.
AI.method: The authors mixed benign compliance demonstrations (non-harmful request, helpful response) with harmful compliance demonstrations (harmful request, helpful response) and tested three hypotheses about how demonstration composition drives harmful compliance across four models.
AI.result: Benign and harmful demonstrations are not interchangeable; benign demonstrations can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model. Preference optimization is the critical training stage preventing benign demonstrations from increasing harmful compliance. Demonstration ordering exhibits strong recency bias. Models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context learning—some adopt demonstrated formatting even when refusing, while others override all in-context signals upon refusal.
AI.conclusion: What models extract from compliance demonstrations depends on demonstration content, ordering, and training methodology, moving beyond showing that demonstration-based jailbreaking works to characterizing how it works.

title: ADaPT: Token-Level Decoupling for Efficient Large Reasoning Models
summary: Large reasoning models rely on long chain-of-thought to achieve strong performance, but applying such reasoning uniformly incurs high computational cost. Existing efficiency-oriented methods attempt to shorten or mix reasoning strategies, yet often degrade reasoning capability. We identify the root cause as sequence-level coupling between efficiency incentives and correctness optimization, which implicitly penalizes long but correct reasoning trajectories. To address this issue, we propose Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT), a token-level dual-process framework that explicitly decouples efficiency and correctness signals during training. ADaPT introduces a mode-selection token to control fast and slow reasoning, applying efficiency-related rewards exclusively to this token to avoid penalizing correct long reasoning while encouraging efficiency when appropriate. Moreover, ADaPT enables precise and continuous control over the efficiency-performance trade-off at inference time: by adjusting the generation probability of the mode-selection token, a single trained model can smoothly move along the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ADaPT significantly reduces inference cost while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks.

AI.tldr: ADaPT is a token-level dual-process framework that decouples efficiency and correctness signals during training via a mode-selection token, enabling precise control over the efficiency-performance trade-off at inference time without degrading reasoning capability.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models incur high computational cost due to uniformly applied long chain-of-thought reasoning. Existing efficiency methods degrade reasoning because they couple efficiency incentives with correctness optimization, penalizing long but correct reasoning trajectories at the sequence level.
AI.method: Proposes ADaPT (Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking), a token-level dual-process framework that introduces a mode-selection token to control fast vs. slow reasoning. Efficiency-related rewards are applied exclusively to this token, decoupling efficiency from correctness optimization. At inference, adjusting the mode-selection token's generation probability enables smooth control along the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier.
AI.result: ADaPT significantly reduces inference cost while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks, and enables continuous, precise control over the efficiency-performance trade-off with a single trained model.
AI.conclusion: Token-level decoupling of efficiency and correctness signals is effective for reducing computational cost without sacrificing reasoning capability, and provides flexible inference-time control over the trade-off.

title: HilDA: Hierarchical Distillation with Diffusion for Advancing Self-Supervised LiDAR Pre-trainin
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Maciej and Jesper contributed equally
summary: Leveraging Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) for camera-to-LiDAR knowledge distillation offers a promising solution to the scarcity of annotated data needed to represent the immense geometric and kinematic diversity of real-world autonomous driving (AD). However, current approaches typically treat VFMs as black-box teachers, relying exclusively on frame-wise feature similarity. Consequently, they do not fully exploit the teacher's layer-wise semantic structure and global context, as well as the rich spatiotemporal information inherent in LiDAR sequences. We propose HilDA, a self-supervised pretraining framework for LiDAR backbones that better captures the semantic what and geometric where needed for driving tasks. HilDA combines hierarchical distillation comprising multi-layer distillation for progressive semantic alignment and global context distillation for scene-level semantics, with a temporal occupancy diffusion objective promoting spatiotemporal consistency. Models pre-trained with HilDA achieve state-of-the-art results on cross-modal distillation benchmarks and outperform models trained via prior distillation approaches on 3D object detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction. Code available at: https://maxiuw.github.io/hilda.

AI.tldr: HilDA is a self-supervised pretraining framework that leverages Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) for camera-to-LiDAR knowledge distillation, using hierarchical distillation (multi-layer + global context) and a temporal occupancy diffusion objective to achieve state-of-the-art results on 3D object detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of annotated LiDAR data for autonomous driving by better exploiting VFMs' layer-wise semantic structure, global context, and spatiotemporal information, which current frame-wise similarity approaches fail to utilize.
AI.method: HilDA combines (1) hierarchical distillation with multi-layer distillation for progressive semantic alignment and global context distillation for scene-level semantics, and (2) a temporal occupancy diffusion objective to promote spatiotemporal consistency.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on cross-modal distillation benchmarks; outperforms prior distillation approaches on 3D object detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction.
AI.conclusion: HilDA effectively captures both semantic what and geometric where needed for driving tasks, demonstrating that better exploitation of VFM structure and spatiotemporal information significantly improves LiDAR backbone pretraining.

title: Multi-LCB: Extending LiveCodeBench to Multiple Programming Languages
summary:
LiveCodeBench (LCB) has recently become a widely adopted benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) on code-generation tasks. By curating competitive programming problems, constantly adding fresh problems to the set, and filtering them by release dates, LCB provides contamination-aware evaluation and offers a holistic view of coding capability. However, LCB remains restricted to Python, leaving open the question of whether LLMs can generalize across the diverse programming languages required in real-world software engineering.
  We introduce Multi-LCB, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs across twelve programming languages, including Python. Multi-LCB transforms Python tasks from the LCB dataset into equivalent tasks in other languages while preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Because it is fully compatible with the original LCB format, Multi-LCB will automatically track future LCB updates, enabling systematic assessment of cross-language code generation competence and requiring models to sustain performance well beyond Python.
  We evaluated 24 LLMs for instruction and reasoning on Multi-LCB, uncovering evidence of Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial disparities in multilingual performance. Our results establish Multi-LCB as a rigorous new benchmark for multi-programming-language code evaluation, directly addressing LCB's primary limitation and exposing critical gaps in current LLM capabilities.

AI.tldr: Multi-LCB extends LiveCodeBench to 12 programming languages, revealing Python overfitting and significant multilingual performance gaps in 24 evaluated LLMs.
AI.motivation: LCB only evaluates Python, but real-world software engineering requires diverse programming languages. The paper aims to assess whether LLMs can generalize across languages.
AI.method: Transform Python LCB tasks into equivalent tasks in 12 languages while preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Multi-LCB is fully compatible with LCB and tracks future updates automatically.
AI.result: Evaluated 24 LLMs, finding evidence of Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial cross-language performance disparities.
AI.conclusion: Multi-LCB serves as a rigorous multilingual benchmark addressing LCB's primary limitation, exposing critical gaps in current LLM cross-language code generation capabilities.

title: Compositionality Emerges in a Narrow Depth-Connectivity Regime: Architecture Constraints and Solution Manifolds
summary: Compositionality is believed to be the foundation for generalization, enabling models to reuse meaningful primitives in novel combinations. Yet, models trained with standard gradient-based optimization rarely, and often only weakly, exhibit compositional internal structure, and it remains unclear how or why such compositionality forms. In this work, we show that compositionality emerges in a narrow connectivity-depth sweet spot. Along the connectivity axis, compositionality only appears in some specifically sparse networks, heavily depends on which connections remain rather than on weights' sparsity alone. Along the depth axis, compositionality emerges within a narrow, target-dependent regime, peaking at specific depths, while both shallower and deeper networks fail. When either the depth or connectivity condition is violated, gradient descent silently converges to fractured solutions rather than compositional ones. To discover and exploit this emergence, we introduce (i) similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity and (ii) a heuristic depth predictor to estimate where compositionality is most likely to appear. Finally, we support these empirical findings with a theoretical framework based on compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds, explaining why compositional solutions are reachable only in a narrow depth-connectivity regime.

AI.tldr: Compositionality in neural networks emerges only within a narrow sweet spot of specific sparse connectivity and target-dependent depth; outside this regime, gradient descent converges to fractured solutions. The paper proposes similarity-based pruning and a depth predictor to discover this regime, supported by theoretical analysis.
AI.motivation: To understand how and why compositional internal structure forms in neural networks trained with gradient-based optimization, given that such structure is rarely observed despite being critical for generalization.
AI.method: Empirical analysis revealing a connectivity-depth sweet spot for compositionality; introduction of similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity and a heuristic depth predictor; theoretical framework using compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds.
AI.result: Compositionality emerges only in specifically sparse networks (dependent on which connections remain, not just weight sparsity) and within a narrow, target-dependent depth range. Violating either condition leads to fractured solutions.
AI.conclusion: Compositional solutions are reachable only in a narrow depth-connectivity regime, explaining why standard gradient-based optimization rarely produces compositional internal structure.

title: Distill Once, Adapt Life-Long: Exploring Dataset Distillation for Continual Test-Time Adaptation
summary: Continual Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) aims to maintain model performance under evolving target domains by adapting online without labeled data. However, practical deployments often cannot retain the source dataset due to privacy or licensing constraints, and purely source-free CTTA methods tend to become unstable under long-term distribution shift, suffering from compounding self-training errors and catastrophic forgetting. We introduce DO-ALL (Distill Once, Adapt Life-Long), a plug-and-play framework that revisits source information in a compact and privacy-conscious form via Dataset Distillation (DD). Before deployment, DO-ALL performs DD to produce a small set of synthetic distilled anchors that summarize the source distribution. During adaptation, each target sample is matched with its most semantically aligned anchor, which provides a stable reference for various CTTA via source replay, representation alignment, and manifold-smoothing regularization. DO-ALL can be seamlessly integrated into existing CTTA algorithms, consistently improving long-term robustness across CIFAR100-C, ImageNet-C, and the CCC benchmark. This demonstrates the potential of leveraging DD to enable stable and continuous adaptation without retaining raw source data. The code is available at https://github.com/blue-531/DOALL.

AI.tldr: DO-ALL is a plug-and-play CTTA framework using Dataset Distillation to produce synthetic anchors summarizing source distribution, enabling stable long-term adaptation via source replay, representation alignment, and manifold-smoothing regularization without retaining raw source data.
AI.motivation: CTTA methods under source-free constraints suffer from instability, compounding self-training errors, and catastrophic forgetting under long-term distribution shifts due to the inability to retain source data for privacy or licensing reasons.
AI.method: Before deployment, perform Dataset Distillation (DD) to generate compact synthetic distilled anchors summarizing the source distribution. During adaptation, match each target sample to its most semantically aligned anchor, using it for source replay, representation alignment, and manifold-smoothing regularization.
AI.result: DO-ALL consistently improves long-term robustness across CIFAR100-C, ImageNet-C, and CCC benchmarks when integrated into existing CTTA algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging Dataset Distillation enables stable and continuous CTTA without retaining raw source data, offering a privacy-conscious solution to long-term distribution shifts.

title: FlowEdit: Associative Memory for Lifelong Pronunciation Adaptation in Flow-Matching TTS
summary: Flow-matching text-to-speech systems achieve remarkable zero-shot quality but remain static after deployment: pronunciation errors on out-of-vocabulary proper nouns persist unless the model is retrained. We introduce FlowEdit, a life-long adaptation framework for frozen flow-matching TTS that learns pronunciation corrections as latent conditioning edits rather than weight updates. When corrective feedback is provided, FlowEdit optimizes a token-level perturbation in the text embedding space, then stores the correction in a Modern Hopfield Network serving as content-addressable episodic memory. At inference, corrections are retrieved via soft attention with a similarity gate, enabling fuzzy morphological matching. On our curated benchmark of 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families, FlowEdit reduces target-word Phoneme Error Rate by 92.7% relative to the zero-shot baseline while maintaining identical general-speech quality. Corrections complete in approximately 15 seconds on a single GPU.

AI.tldr: FlowEdit is a life-long adaptation framework for frozen flow-matching TTS that corrects pronunciation errors on out-of-vocabulary proper nouns by learning latent conditioning edits stored in a Hopfield Network, reducing Phoneme Error Rate by 92.7% without retraining.
AI.motivation: Flow-matching TTS systems achieve high zero-shot quality but are static after deployment, making persistent pronunciation errors on out-of-vocabulary proper nouns that require costly retraining to fix.
AI.method: FlowEdit optimizes token-level perturbations in the text embedding space from corrective feedback, stores corrections in a Modern Hopfield Network as content-addressable episodic memory, and retrieves them at inference via soft attention with a similarity gate for fuzzy morphological matching.
AI.result: On a benchmark of 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families, FlowEdit achieves a 92.7% relative reduction in target-word Phoneme Error Rate with no degradation in general-speech quality, completing corrections in ~15 seconds on a single GPU.
AI.conclusion: FlowEdit enables efficient, life-long adaptation of frozen flow-matching TTS models through latent conditioning edits and episodic memory, offering a practical solution for correcting pronunciation errors without retraining.

title: Towards Graph-Based Deep Learning for Map Generalization: Insights from Building Footprints Simplification and Aggregation
summary: Map generalization remains one of the fundamental tasks in cartography, especially for the simplification and aggregation of complex building footprints. This study presents the first exploratory application of graph-based deep learning to both tasks, reformulating simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction within a unified graph learning framework. We evaluate representative graph neural network architectures (GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE) on multi-scale building datasets, showing that GraphSAGE demonstrates relative strengths in link prediction accuracy, while also revealing persistent challenges in precise node movement prediction. Beyond quantitative performance, the results highlight that aggregation poses greater complexity and challenges than simplification, underscoring the difficulty of capturing higher-level spatial relationships in map generalization with current deep learning approaches. Although limitations such as data imbalance and the need for post-processing remain, the study provides valuable insights and methodological directions for advancing automated map generalization with deep learning approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first exploratory use of graph-based deep learning (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) for building footprint simplification (node movement prediction) and aggregation (link prediction) in map generalization. GraphSAGE shows relative strengths in link prediction, but aggregation remains more challenging than simplification. Limitations include data imbalance and post-processing needs.
AI.motivation: Map generalization, particularly simplification and aggregation of complex building footprints, is a fundamental cartographic task that remains challenging. The authors aim to explore whether graph-based deep learning can address these tasks as a unified framework.
AI.method: The authors reformulate simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction within a unified graph learning framework. They evaluate three graph neural network architectures (GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE) on multi-scale building datasets.
AI.result: GraphSAGE demonstrates relative strengths in link prediction accuracy. However, there are persistent challenges in precise node movement prediction. Aggregation poses greater complexity and challenges than simplification.
AI.conclusion: The study provides valuable insights and methodological directions for advancing automated map generalization with deep learning, though limitations such as data imbalance and the need for post-processing remain.

title: Evaluation of Image Matching for Art Skills Assessment
summary: While some individuals possess a natural talent for drawing, mastering this skill requires dedicated training and practice. Determining one's skill in the art of drawing requires proper comprehensive assessment. In this paper, we propose a method to measure drawing skill by by matching the hand-drawn image with the original template. Existing techniques often involve complex processes. However, advancements in computer vision allow us to train computers to perform these comparisons at a human-like level, thereby resolving the tedious and overwhelming traditional process. Using computer vision applications, determining image similarity involves identifying the level of similarities in an image with a reference image. We have implemented and analyzed the SIFT feature and Siamese network to measure image similarity. Our results indicate that it is feasible to assess art skill levels. Through feature analysis, we found that SIFT-based key point matching provides a more effective means of detecting drawing skills.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a computer vision approach to assess drawing skill by measuring similarity between hand-drawn images and original templates, finding that SIFT-based keypoint matching outperforms Siamese networks for this task.
AI.motivation: Determining one's drawing skill requires proper comprehensive assessment, but existing techniques often involve complex and tedious traditional processes. The authors aim to automate this assessment using computer vision.
AI.method: The authors implemented and analyzed two computer vision approaches for measuring image similarity between hand-drawn images and original templates: SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform) feature matching and Siamese neural networks.
AI.result: SIFT-based keypoint matching provides a more effective means of detecting drawing skills compared to the Siamese network approach. The results demonstrate feasibility of assessing art skill levels through computer vision.
AI.conclusion: It is feasible to automatically assess drawing skill levels using computer vision techniques, with SIFT feature matching serving as a more effective method than Siamese networks for measuring similarity between hand-drawn and template images.

title: DeepSWIP: Quotient-WMC Counterfactuals for Neural Probabilistic Logic Programs
summary: Neurosymbolic systems such as DeepProbLog combine neural perception with probabilistic logic, but standard inference is associational. Counterfactual reasoning additionally requires a causal semantics for interventions and evidence. We introduce DeepSWIP, a single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog programs. Using neural materialization, we reduce fixed-context neural predicates to ordinary ProbLog choices, apply Single World Intervention Programs (SWIPs), and compute counterfactuals by weighted model counting (WMC) over a single transformed program. Under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions, DeepSWIP is exact relative to the learned materialized FCM. The standard quotient-WMC form of ProbLog conditionals identifies active neural probabilities and explains intervention cleaning, calibration sensitivity, and rare-evidence instability. Experiments on MPI3D confirm the transformation against a DeepTwin construction against 12,000 queries, as predicted and a 2.14$\times$ inference speedup from avoiding the Twin's endogenous duplication. A SUMO HOV experiment shows that neural calibration degradation biases plug-in estimates, while a correctly scoped randomized-policy AIPW estimator removes most first-order bias for population mean and ATE estimands. Code is at https://github.com/saibib/deep_SWIP.

AI.tldr: DeepSWIP introduces a single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog that uses neural materialization to reduce fixed-context neural predicates to ordinary ProbLog choices, enabling exact counterfactual computation via weighted model counting over a single transformed program.
AI.motivation: Standard inference in neurosymbolic systems like DeepProbLog is associational, lacking causal semantics needed for counterfactual reasoning under interventions and evidence.
AI.method: DeepSWIP applies neural materialization to convert fixed-context neural predicates into ordinary ProbLog choices, then uses Single World Intervention Programs (SWIPs) to compute counterfactuals via WMC over a single transformed program instead of dual (Twin) programs.
AI.result: Experiments on MPI3D show exact match against DeepTwin construction across 12,000 queries with 2.14× speedup, and SUMO HOV experiments demonstrate that AIPW estimators remove first-order bias from neural calibration degradation.
AI.conclusion: DeepSWIP provides exact, efficient counterfactual inference for DeepProbLog under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions, with practical benefits for causal estimation in neurosymbolic systems.

title: Low-Energy Reduced RISC-V Instruction Subset Processor for Tsetlin Machine Inference at the Edge
comment: 6 pages, 6 Figures, Accepted in IEEE ISVLSI Conference 2026
summary: Tsetlin Machine (TM) is a logic-based machine learning approach that relies on simple bitwise operations and finite-state automata, which makes it attractive for edge AI deployments. Recent work has focused on co-processor and accelerator designs based on Tsetlin Machines (TMs). Although these designs achieve high performance, they typically depend on tightly coupled interfaces, microcode-style programming, and external host processors, limiting flexibility and ease of programming. In this work, we present a domain-specific RISC-V microprocessor architecture and design flow tailored for TM inference. Leveraging the modular structure of RISC-V, we design a reduced instruction subset processor that retains programmability while targeting improved performance and lower energy consumption for TM workloads. Instruction profiling is employed to guide instruction reduction, followed by datapath and control path simplifications tailored to TM inference. Both the baseline RV32IM core and the proposed reduced core are evaluated across multiple datasets and compared with Binarized Neural Networks (BNNs), which serve as a hardware-efficient baseline due to their reliance on bitwise operations during inference. Results show that TM achieves comparable or higher accuracy (e.g., up to 88.18% on CIFAR-2 compared to 60.0% for BNN) while reducing execution time by up to 98% across multiple datasets. Furthermore, the proposed design achieves an average $29.7\times$ reduction in energy consumption, demonstrating its effectiveness for programmable and efficient edge AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a domain-specific RISC-V microprocessor architecture tailored for Tsetlin Machine (TM) inference, achieving up to 98% reduction in execution time and 29.7× energy savings compared to BNN baselines, while maintaining programmability for edge AI deployments.
AI.motivation: Existing TM accelerator designs rely on tightly coupled interfaces, microcode-style programming, and external host processors, limiting flexibility and ease of programming. A more programmable yet efficient solution is needed for edge AI.
AI.method: The authors leverage the modular RISC-V architecture to design a reduced instruction subset processor (instruction profiling-guided reduction) with simplified datapath and control path specifically for TM inference. Evaluation compares TM vs BNN across multiple datasets.
AI.result: TM achieves comparable or higher accuracy (e.g., 88.18% on CIFAR-2 vs 60.0% for BNN), reduces execution time by up to 98%, and achieves an average 29.7× reduction in energy consumption across multiple datasets.
AI.conclusion: A domain-specific RISC-V processor for TM inference provides an effective balance of programmability and efficiency, making it a promising solution for programmable edge AI systems.

title: DeepForestVisionV2: Ecology-Driven Taxonomy Expansion for Camera-Trap Monitoring in African Tropical Forests
comment: Accepted at ICPR 2026 - Computer Vision for Biodiversity Monitoring and Conservation Workshop
summary: Camera-trap monitoring in African tropical forests increasingly extends beyond closed-canopy interiors to riverbanks, clearings, and park edges. Among available open tools for African forest camera-trap classification, DeepForestVision is the only one providing a matched offline workflow for both photographs and videos, and previous work showed that it outperformed other available baselines on a comparable benchmark. However, it was designed for closed-canopy, ground-level forest interiors and uses a 35-class prediction space that becomes too coarse when deployments encounter arboreal primates, birds, semi-aquatic taxa, or human-associated confounders such as livestock. We present DeepForestVisionV2, an ecology-driven expansion from 35 to 64 prediction classes (61 animal classes plus human, vehicle, and blank) designed to address three recurrent deployment gradients: vertical stratification, scene openness, and anthropogenic interfaces. DeepForestVisionV2 retains the same offline workflow and is trained on 1,535,010 photographs and 243,354 videos from multi-country African tropical-forest projects. Evaluation combines a cross-country cropped-photo validation set, used to assess robustness across sites and camera-trap settings, with three held-out Uganda video benchmarks spanning the targeted gradients. On the validation set, DeepForestVisionV2 reaches 0.86 accuracy, 0.82 macro-F1, and 0.81 balanced accuracy. On the deployment benchmarks, it preserves or improves baseline accuracy despite its harder classification task, while increasing the number of identified taxa from 22 to 29 in forest-interior videos and from 4 to 9 at riverbanks. In the park-edge use case, it raises accuracy from 0.62 to 0.86 and reduces false alarms from 11 to 0. These results show that DeepForestVisionV2 materially improves field utility while preserving robustness across sites, habitats, and camera-trap settings.

AI.tldr: DeepForestVisionV2 expands the classification space of its predecessor from 35 to 64 classes (61 animal + human, vehicle, blank), targeting vertical stratification, scene openness, and anthropogenic interfaces in African tropical forests. Trained on 1.5M+ photographs and 243K+ videos, it achieves 0.86 accuracy on validation, improves taxonomic detection from 22→29 (forest interior), 4→9 (riverbanks), and raises park-edge accuracy from 0.62→0.86 while eliminating false alarms.
AI.motivation: The original DeepForestVision was limited to closed-canopy forest interiors with a 35-class scheme that was too coarse for deployments involving arboreal primates, birds, semi-aquatic taxa, and human-associated confounders like livestock. Camera-trap monitoring is expanding to riverbanks, clearings, and park edges, necessitating a richer classification space.
AI.method: Expanded the model from 35 to 64 prediction classes (61 animal + human, vehicle, blank), trained on 1,535,010 photographs and 243,354 videos from multi-country African tropical-forest projects. Retained the same offline workflow. Evaluation used a cross-country cropped-photo validation set and three held-out Uganda video benchmarks targeting vertical stratification, scene openness, and anthropogenic interfaces.
AI.result: Cross-country validation: 0.86 accuracy, 0.82 macro-F1, 0.81 balanced accuracy. Forest-interior videos: identified taxa increased from 22→29. Riverbank videos: identified taxa increased from 4→9. Park-edge videos: accuracy rose from 0.62→0.86, false alarms reduced from 11→0.
AI.conclusion: DeepForestVisionV2 materially improves field utility across diverse deployment gradients while preserving robustness across sites, habitats, and camera-trap settings.

title: LedgerAgent: Structured State for Policy-Adherent Tool-Calling Agents
summary: Policy-adherent tool-calling agents in customer-service domains must maintain task states across turns while calling tools and obeying domain policies. Task states consist of relevant facts, identifiers, constraints, and conditions observed through user interaction and tool calls. In standard agents, task states are not represented separately. Observations, tool returns, and policy instructions are placed in the prompt, leaving agents to reconstruct the relevant states from the prompt each time they decide what to do next. This design makes state management implicit, creating two common failure modes. An agent may retrieve the right facts but later ground its decision in stale, missing, or incorrect information; and a syntactically valid tool call may still violate a domain policy that depends on the current task state. We introduce \textsc{LedgerAgent}, an inference-time method for tool-calling agents that maintains observed task states in a separate ledger and renders the states into the prompt. The ledger is also used to check state-dependent policy constraints before environment-changing tool calls are executed, blocking policy violations. Across four customer-service domains and a mixed panel of open- and closed-weight models, \textsc{LedgerAgent} improves average pass\textasciicircum{}k over a standard prompt-based tool-calling approach, with the largest gains under stricter multi-trial consistency metrics.

AI.tldr: LedgerAgent improves tool-calling agent compliance in customer-service domains by maintaining task states in a separate ledger and checking domain policies before tool execution, outperforming standard prompt-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Standard tool-calling agents lack explicit task state management, leading to two failure modes: (1) grounding decisions in stale/missing/incorrect information, and (2) executing syntactically valid tool calls that violate state-dependent domain policies.
AI.method: LedgerAgent is an inference-time method that maintains observed task states (facts, identifiers, constraints, conditions) in a separate ledger, renders these states into the prompt, and checks state-dependent policy constraints before executing environment-changing tool calls.
AI.result: Across four customer-service domains and mixed open/closed-weight models, LedgerAgent improves average pass^k over standard prompt-based tool-calling, with largest gains under stricter multi-trial consistency metrics.
AI.conclusion: Explicit state management via a ledger reduces state-related failures and policy violations in tool-calling agents, particularly under strict consistency requirements.

title: Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing
summary: Reservoir computing offers a lightweight framework for forecasting dynamical systems but may struggle to capture long-range dependencies due to limited representational capacity. Conventional reservoir computing recurrently uses trainable reservoirs with hyperparameter sensitivity, while the next-generation reservoir computing removes recurrence at the cost of rapidly growing feature dimensions. Here, we develop Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing (KARC), which replaces reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem. We rigorously show that KARC is a lightweight design of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs), preserving the potential expressive capacity of KANs while admitting efficient closed-form training of reservoir computing. At comparable cost, KARC outperforms existing reservoir computing methods on challenging benchmarks including partial differential equations. It can also be integrated with generative diffusion models for text-to-image generation. This work thus establishes a principled bridge between reservoir computing and KANs, enabling efficient and high-fidelity dynamical system forecasting.

AI.tldr: KARC replaces traditional reservoir computing with Kolmogorov-Arnold basis-function expansions, offering closed-form training, superior performance on dynamical system benchmarks, and integration with diffusion models for text-to-image generation.
AI.motivation: Reservoir computing struggles with long-range dependencies due to limited representational capacity, and existing methods suffer from hyperparameter sensitivity (conventional) or rapidly growing feature dimensions (next-generation).
AI.method: KARC replaces reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, creating a lightweight design of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) that preserves expressive capacity while enabling efficient closed-form training.
AI.result: At comparable cost, KARC outperforms existing reservoir computing methods on challenging benchmarks including partial differential equations, and can be integrated with generative diffusion models for text-to-image generation.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a principled bridge between reservoir computing and KANs, enabling efficient and high-fidelity dynamical system forecasting.

title: Cinematic Compositing Using Character-Environment-Harmonized Video Generation Models
summary: Cinematic compositing aims to integrate green-screen characters into novel environments while maintaining physical and photometric realism. Previous methods often fail to capture the complex bidirectional interactions between characters and their surroundings, which we characterize as Character-to-Environment (C2E) physical interaction and Environment-to-Character (E2C) lighting harmonization. To address this, we propose an end-to-end video diffusion framework that jointly models C2E and E2C interactions, specifically handling the challenges of interactive props. Our approach introduces a tri-mask-guided architecture with RGB-D joint denoising to ensure physically consistent interactions among the character, props, and environment. We further develop an efficient prior-driven data curation pipeline to construct high-quality relighting pairs without expensive rendering. Finally, a reference-conditioned mechanism enables controllable environment synthesis and precise prop replacement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms existing methods in cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing.

AI.tldr: A video diffusion framework for cinematic compositing that jointly models character-environment physical interactions and lighting harmonization using tri-mask-guided architecture with RGB-D denoising, achieving state-of-the-art results in dynamic video compositing.
AI.motivation: Previous methods fail to capture complex bidirectional interactions between characters and environments, specifically Character-to-Environment (C2E) physical interaction and Environment-to-Character (E2C) lighting harmonization, especially for interactive props.
AI.method: Proposes an end-to-end video diffusion framework with: (1) tri-mask-guided architecture with RGB-D joint denoising for consistent physical interactions, (2) prior-driven data curation pipeline for high-quality relighting pairs, and (3) reference-conditioned mechanism for controllable environment synthesis and prop replacement.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms existing methods in cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively handles bidirectional character-environment interactions, enabling realistic cinematic compositing without expensive rendering.

title: How Do Instructions Shape Speech? Cross-Attention Attribution for Style-Captioned Text-to-Speech
summary: Style-captioned text-to-speech systems use natural language to control voice characteristics, but how individual words influence acoustic output remains unclear. Understanding this is critical for diagnosing failure modes and improving controllability in expressive TTS. We propose cross-attention attribution for speech diffusion models, adapting the DAAM framework to the speech domain for the first time, and apply it to CapSpeech-TTS. Our method extracts per-token heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps. We analyze 3,600 (style caption, text transcript) combinations comprising 120 style captions conditioning the generation of 30 text transcripts each, revealing how caption tokens shape waveforms. Results show: (1) style tokens have lower temporal variance than content/function tokens, confirming global conditioning; (2) style attention correlates with F0 and energy; (3) style conditioning peaks in early steps and deep layers; (4) attention entropy reaches its minimum at layer 17, co-occurring with the style importance peak, indicating maximal network selectivity at the most style-critical stage. This is the first study of how natural language influences cross-attention in speech diffusion models

AI.tldr: First study applying cross-attention attribution (DAAM) to speech diffusion models for analyzing how style caption tokens influence acoustic output in CapSpeech-TTS, revealing style tokens have lower temporal variance, correlate with F0/energy, peak in early steps/deep layers, and show maximal network selectivity at layer 17.
AI.motivation: Understanding how individual words in style captions influence acoustic output in TTS is critical for diagnosing failure modes and improving controllability in expressive text-to-speech.
AI.method: Proposed cross-attention attribution for speech diffusion models, adapting the DAAM framework to speech. Extracted per-token heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps, analyzing 3,600 (style caption, text transcript) combinations.
AI.result: (1) Style tokens have lower temporal variance than content/function tokens; (2) Style attention correlates with F0 and energy; (3) Style conditioning peaks in early steps and deep layers; (4) Attention entropy minimum at layer 17 co-occurs with style importance peak, indicating maximal network selectivity.
AI.conclusion: First study of how natural language influences cross-attention in speech diffusion models, providing insights into style-controlled TTS internals.

title: Activation- and Influence-Aware Ranks (AIR): Function-Preserving SVD Compression for LLMs
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Resource-Adaptive Foundation Model Inference (AdaptFM), Seoul, South Korea (non-archival)
summary: We present Activation- and Influence-Aware Ranks (AIR), an SVD-based LLM compression framework that guides each weight matrix's low-rank approximation with a backward-signal influence metric. Starting from the activation-aware optimum of SVD-LLM(W), AIR runs a single closed-form alternating least squares (ALS) sweep that integrates influence element-wise under a monotone-descent guarantee. AIR is layer-local and composes orthogonally with end-to-end methods: alone it exceeds ACIP, and AIR+LoRA outperforms it further. AIR improves perplexity over SVD-LLM(W) by >18% at <=60% parameter retention, matches its quality with ~90% less calibration data, and turns parameter savings into FLOP, peak-memory, and per-token latency gains.

AI.tldr: AIR is an SVD-based LLM compression framework that improves low-rank approximation by integrating influence-aware weights via a single ALS sweep, achieving >18% perplexity improvement over SVD-LLM(W) at ≤60% parameter retention while reducing calibration data needs by ~90%.
AI.motivation: Existing SVD-based LLM compression methods (e.g., SVD-LLM(W)) use activation-aware criteria but ignore backward-signal influence, leading to suboptimal low-rank approximations. The authors aim to incorporate influence information without sacrificing the closed-form efficiency of SVD.
AI.method: AIR performs a single closed-form alternating least squares (ALS) sweep after the activation-aware SVD optimum, integrating influence (backward-signal) metrics element-wise under a monotone-descent guarantee. It is layer-local and composes orthogonally with end-to-end methods like LoRA.
AI.result: AIR improves perplexity over SVD-LLM(W) by >18% at ≤60% parameter retention; matches SVD-LLM(W) quality with ~90% less calibration data; outperforms ACIP; and AIR+LoRA further improves performance. Parameter savings translate to FLOP, peak-memory, and per-token latency gains.
AI.conclusion: AIR is a more accurate and data-efficient SVD-based compression method that integrates influence-awareness with minimal overhead, and its gains are orthogonal to end-to-end fine-tuning approaches like LoRA.

title: BAFIS: Dataset + Framework to assess occupational Bias and Human Preference in modern Text-to-image Models
comment: Accepted at the IEEE Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision, WACV 2026
summary: Generative artificial intelligence has the potential to improve productivity and transform the production of creative content. However, existing research indicates that image generation models are significantly influenced by biases. This work investigates the inherent biases and language-induced biases present in text-to-image models within the context of occupation-related image generation, complementing established metrics with human preference feedback. We present a comprehensive evaluation of five current text-to-image models: Midjourney v6.1, Stable Diffusion 3 Medium, DALL-E 3, Playground v2.5, and FLUX.1-dev , focusing on gender and ethnicity bias, image quality, and prompt alignment. To facilitate this evaluation, we developed the "Battle-Arena for Fair Image Synthesis" (BAFIS), a platform designed to collect human feedback on bias in generated images. Furthermore, we created a dataset comprising 21,140 synthetic images generated using multilingual prompts, which serves as a basis for our analysis. We further place our results within a broader social context by comparing them to official statistics from the German Federal Employment Agency. Our findings reveal systematic biases in text-to-image models, with established evaluation metrics in partial correlation with subjective user ratings. Thus, our research emphasizes the need for including human preferences to develop fairer and more inclusive text-to-image models.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates gender and ethnicity biases in five text-to-image models (Midjourney v6.1, Stable Diffusion 3 Medium, DALL-E 3, Playground v2.5, FLUX.1-dev) using a newly developed platform (BAFIS) and a dataset of 21,140 multilingual-prompt images, finding systematic biases and limited correlation between automated metrics and human preference feedback.
AI.motivation: Existing research shows image generation models are significantly influenced by biases, but comprehensive evaluations combining established metrics with human preference feedback are lacking. The authors aim to fill this gap by investigating inherent and language-induced biases in occupation-related image generation.
AI.method: The authors developed BAFIS (Battle-Arena for Fair Image Synthesis), a platform for collecting human feedback on bias. They generated 21,140 synthetic images using multilingual prompts across five models, evaluated gender and ethnicity bias, image quality, and prompt alignment, and compared results against official German Federal Employment Agency statistics.
AI.result: Systematic biases were found across all text-to-image models. Established evaluation metrics showed only partial correlation with subjective user ratings, indicating that automated metrics alone are insufficient for capturing perceived bias.
AI.conclusion: The research emphasizes the necessity of incorporating human preference feedback to develop fairer and more inclusive text-to-image models, as current automated evaluation metrics are not fully aligned with human perceptions of bias.

title: Toward Calibrated Mixture-of-Experts Under Distribution Shift
summary: Calibration aligns a model's predictive uncertainty with the frequencies of its empirical outcomes and is important for understanding and trusting reported probabilities. Recent work shows that enforcing calibration at the level of individual predictors can improve ensemble accuracy and calibration, with mixture-of-experts (MoE) models showing strong empirical improvements in particular; however, the conditions under which calibration helps MoE are not well understood. In this work, we study how MoE models behave under distribution shift, focusing on how routing mechanisms interact with expert-level calibration. We show that expert calibration is sufficient to ensure calibration of the overall model under a broad class of distribution shifts in hard-routed models, but is insufficient for calibrating soft-routed models. To address this, we propose an adversarial reweighting that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift, and we demonstrate that it improves the accuracy-calibration tradeoff both on average and on difficult subsets of the data, across model classes, prediction tasks, and distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how expert-level calibration interacts with mixture-of-experts (MoE) models under distribution shift. It shows that expert calibration ensures overall calibration in hard-routed MoE under broad shifts but not in soft-routed MoE. The authors propose an adversarial reweighting method that penalizes calibration errors under distribution shift, improving the accuracy-calibration tradeoff across tasks and shifts.
AI.motivation: Recent work shows calibration at individual predictor level improves ensemble accuracy and calibration, especially in MoE models, but conditions under which calibration helps MoE under distribution shift are not well understood.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of hard-routed vs. soft-routed MoE under distribution shift; proposed adversarial reweighting that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift.
AI.result: Expert calibration is sufficient for overall model calibration under broad distribution shifts in hard-routed models but insufficient for soft-routed models. The proposed adversarial reweighting improves accuracy-calibration tradeoff across model classes, tasks, and shifts.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing calibration at the expert level interacts differently with routing mechanisms under distribution shift; adversarial reweighting可以有效缓解 soft-routed MoE 的校准问题并提升整体性能。

title: Connect the Dots: Training LLMs for Long-Lifecycle Agents with Cross-Domain Generalization Via Reinforcement Learning
comment: Work in progress; we will continuously update the codebase and arXiv version
summary: This work presents a general framework for training large language models (LLMs) to "Connect the Dots" (CoD), a meta-capability required by long-lifecycle agents: as an LLM-based AI agent gets deployed in an environment, it solves a long sequence of tasks while continuously exploring the environment, learning from its own experiences, and iteratively self-updating its context about the environment, thereby achieving progressively better performance on future tasks conditioned on the updated context. Major components of the CoD framework include: (1) algorithm design and infrastructure for end-to-end reinforcement learning (RL) with long rollout sequences interleaving solve-task and update-context episodes; (2) tasks and environments for incentivizing and eliciting the targeted meta-capability in LLMs during training, as well as for faithfully measuring progress during evaluation. We present proof-of-concept implementations of the CoD framework, including a GRPO-style RL algorithm with fine-grained credit assignment, as well as tasks and environments tailored to the targeted meta-capability (rather than domain-specific LLM capabilities or standard task-by-task RL). Empirical results validate the efficacy of end-to-end RL training in the CoD setting, and demonstrate the potential for out-of-distribution generalization -- within the training domains, across different domains, and from CoD to Ralph-loop settings -- of the elicited meta-capability. Our investigation of CoD connects several lines of prior works, and opens up new opportunities for advancing LLMs and AI agents. To facilitate further research and applications, we release our implementations at \url{https://github.com/agentscope-ai/Trinity-RFT/tree/research/cod/examples/research_cod}.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the "Connect the Dots" (CoD) framework for training LLMs to develop meta-capabilities needed for long-lifecycle agents—enabling them to solve sequential tasks while continuously exploring, learning from experience, and self-updating context. The framework uses end-to-end RL with long rollouts, fine-grained credit assignment via GRPO-style algorithms, and custom environments. Results show efficacy and out-of-distribution generalization across domains.
AI.motivation: Long-lifecycle AI agents deployed in environments need to solve sequences of tasks while continuously learning and updating their context from experience, but existing LLM training approaches lack mechanisms to develop this meta-capability of "connecting the dots" across tasks over time.
AI.method: The CoD framework consists of: (1) end-to-end RL with long rollout sequences that interleave solve-task and update-context episodes; (2) custom tasks/environments designed to incentivize meta-capability learning; (3) a GRPO-style RL algorithm with fine-grained credit assignment for training LLMs in this setting.
AI.result: Empirical results validate that end-to-end RL training in the CoD setting is effective, and the elicited meta-capability demonstrates out-of-distribution generalization—within training domains, across different domains, and from CoD to Ralph-loop settings.
AI.conclusion: The CoD framework connects prior work on LLM agents, RL, and continual learning, opening new opportunities for advancing LLM-based AI agents that can autonomously improve over long deployment horizons.

title: SPOT-E: Test-Time Entropy Shaping with Visual Spotlights for Frozen VLMs
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) often underperform on evidence intensive tasks because decisive visual evidence are small, localized, and easy to overlook, leading to failures in evidence readout even when high-level reasoning is intact. Prior inference-time visual interventions can improve grounding without retraining, but they are largely open-loop and lack a mechanism to verify whether highlighted evidence is actually used. We study answer-span prediction entropy as a model-internal feedback signal and show that naive entropy minimization is ambiguous, since low entropy may arise from evidence-grounded confidence or shortcut collapse. To resolve this ambiguity, we introduce low-entropy anchors and an entropy-shaping objective that reduces answer uncertainty while preserving baseline high-confidence tokens. We instantiate this principle in SPOT-E, a plug-and-play test-time method that produces question-conditioned spotlights, optimized per instance via light-weight tuning based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Across all benchmarks and different VLM families, SPOT-E yields consistent gains and improved robustness under visual corruptions. Code is publicly available at: \url{https://github.com/YinBo0927/SPOT-E}

AI.tldr: SPOT-E is a plug-and-play test-time method that uses entropy-shaping and GRPO to generate question-conditioned visual spotlights, improving VLM performance on evidence-intensive tasks by ensuring decisive visual evidence is actually used.
AI.motivation: VLMs fail on evidence-intensive tasks because small, localized visual evidence is often overlooked during inference, and existing open-loop visual interventions lack a feedback mechanism to verify whether highlighted evidence is actually used by the model.
AI.method: SPOT-E introduces low-entropy anchors and an entropy-shaping objective to resolve ambiguity in entropy minimization, then optimizes question-conditioned visual spotlights per instance via light-weight GRPO-based tuning.
AI.result: SPOT-E yields consistent gains across all benchmarks and different VLM families, with improved robustness under visual corruptions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed entropy-shaping approach effectively disambiguates evidence-grounded confidence from shortcut collapse, enabling reliable test-time visual interventions that improve VLM evidence readout.

title: StreamKL: Fast and Memory-Efficient KL Divergence for Boosting Attention Distillation
summary: Attention distillation, which trains one attention distribution to match another by minimizing their Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, is widely used in knowledge distillation, model compression, continual learning, and sparse-attention LLM training. However, existing approaches materialize both attention distributions before computing the KL reduction, incurring $O(N_QN_K)$ memory and IO costs that become prohibitive at long context lengths. We present StreamKL, the first fused GPU primitive for attention KL divergence that eliminates this quadratic materialization. StreamKL derives a novel online formulation for the coupled two-distribution KL reduction, enabling a single one-pass forward kernel that streams query-key tiles through on-chip SRAM. For the backward pass, StreamKL recomputes attention probabilities tile-by-tile, avoiding storage of quadratic intermediates. We further design and implement efficient GPU kernels with dedicated optimizations. Experiments show StreamKL delivers up to $43\times$ and $14\times$ speedups over baseline methods in the forward and backward passes, respectively. Most importantly, StreamKL reduces the extra HBM footprint of attention distillation from $O(N_QN_K)$ to $O(1)$, enabling long-context distillation on a single GPU.

AI.tldr: StreamKL is a fused GPU primitive that eliminates quadratic memory and compute costs in attention distillation by deriving an online formulation for KL divergence, achieving up to 43× forward and 14× backward speedups while reducing HBM footprint from O(N_Q N_K) to O(1).
AI.motivation: Existing attention distillation methods materialize both attention distributions before computing KL divergence, incurring O(N_Q N_K) memory and IO costs that become prohibitive at long context lengths, limiting applicability in long-context scenarios.
AI.method: StreamKL derives a novel online formulation for coupled two-distribution KL reduction, enabling a single one-pass forward kernel that streams query-key tiles through on-chip SRAM. The backward pass recomputes attention probabilities tile-by-tile, avoiding quadratic intermediate storage. Efficient GPU kernels with dedicated optimizations are implemented.
AI.result: StreamKL delivers up to 43× and 14× speedups over baselines in forward and backward passes respectively, and reduces extra HBM footprint from O(N_Q N_K) to O(1), enabling long-context distillation on a single GPU.
AI.conclusion: StreamKL makes attention distillation practical for long sequences by eliminating quadratic memory bottlenecks through online computation and tiled recomputation strategies, with significant speedups and minimal memory overhead.

title: Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification for Weakly Supervised Histopathology Segmentation
comment: Accepted to MICCAI 2026. This is the pre-review submitted version, not the camera-ready version. The final authenticated version will be available in the MICCAI 2026 proceedings
summary: Existing weakly supervised semantic segmentation (WSSS) methods in computational pathology rely on a multi-stage paradigm: class activation map (CAM) generation, offline pseudo-mask refinement, and fully supervised retraining. While established, this decoupled approach presents fundamental limitations. The multi-stage process not only incurs high computational training costs but also suffers from error propagation: local texture biases in shallow CNN layers generate false-positive artifacts that subsequent refinement steps often fail to correct. To address these persistent challenges through a simple yet highly effective approach, we propose the Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification (SSHR) framework. Rather than passively refining CAMs post-hoc, our method proactively purifies intermediate feature representations during the forward pass. We introduce a Hierarchical Feature Rectification Module (HFRM) that utilizes deep global semantic context to filter out local anomalies in shallow layers. This mechanism generates high-fidelity activation maps directly within a single training loop. Experiments on the LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS datasets demonstrate that SSHR outperforms state-of-the-art multi-stage methods. Furthermore, SSHR reduces training duration by 2 to 5 times. This efficiency minimizes computational overhead and accelerates clinical translation for large-scale histopathology workflows. The code is available at: https://github.com/trongduc-nguyen/SSHR

AI.tldr: SSHR is a single-stage weakly supervised semantic segmentation framework for computational pathology that uses a Hierarchical Feature Rectification Module (HFRM) to purify intermediate features during the forward pass, eliminating the need for offline CAM refinement and multi-stage retraining, while outperforming state-of-the-art methods and reducing training time by 2-5×.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-stage WSSS methods in pathology suffer from high computational costs and error propagation, where local texture biases in shallow CNN layers produce false-positive artifacts that downstream refinement steps cannot adequately correct.
AI.method: The SSHR framework introduces a Hierarchical Feature Rectification Module (HFRM) that leverages deep global semantic context to filter out local anomalies in shallow layers during the forward pass, enabling direct generation of high-fidelity activation maps within a single training loop without offline pseudo-mask refinement.
AI.result: On LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS datasets, SSHR outperforms state-of-the-art multi-stage methods while reducing training duration by 2 to 5 times.
AI.conclusion: SSHR demonstrates that a simple, single-stage rectification approach can simultaneously improve segmentation quality and computational efficiency, making it more suitable for large-scale clinical histopathology workflows.

title: VIMPO: Value-Implicit Policy Optimization for LLMs
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has become a central tool for improving the reasoning ability of large language models, but current methods face a trade-off between simplicity and credit assignment. Group-relative methods such as GRPO avoid training a critic, but typically assign a trajectory-level advantage to every token. Actor-critic methods provide denser learning signals, but require a learned value function with its own training instability. We introduce VIMPO, a critic-free policy optimization method that derives a policy-implied value function from the optimality conditions of KL-regularized reinforcement learning. For autoregressive generation, the resulting value recurrence can be written in terms of policy-reference log-ratios and anchored by the terminal condition that no future reward remains at the end of a trajectory. This gives a simple value loss that incorporates outcome-level verifiable rewards without training a critic. The same derivation also yields a critic-free actor advantage, allowing VIMPO to separate reward incorporation through the value loss from policy improvement through a PPO-style actor update. On mathematical RLVR benchmarks, VIMPO improves over GRPO across MATH-500, AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and OlympiadBench, with especially larger gains on competition-style evaluations. Under noisy rewards, VIMPO retains a consistent advantage over GRPO, suggesting that policy-implied value optimization can provide finer credit assignment while preserving the practical simplicity of critic-free training.

AI.tldr: VIMPO is a critic-free policy optimization method that derives a policy-implied value function from KL-regularized RL optimality conditions, enabling finer credit assignment without training a separate critic. It outperforms GRPO on math reasoning benchmarks, especially under noisy rewards.
AI.motivation: Current RLVR methods face a trade-off between simplicity (GRPO, no critic but coarse trajectory-level credit) and dense credit assignment (actor-critic methods, but require unstable learned value functions). The goal is to achieve finer credit assignment while preserving the simplicity of critic-free training.
AI.method: VIMPO derives a policy-implied value function from the optimality conditions of KL-regularized RL. For autoregressive generation, this yields a value recurrence expressed via policy-reference log-ratios, anchored by a terminal condition. A critic-free actor advantage is also derived, separating reward incorporation (via value loss) from policy improvement (via PPO-style actor update).
AI.result: VIMPO improves over GRPO on MATH-500, AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and OlympiadBench, with larger gains on competition-style evaluations. Under noisy rewards, VIMPO retains a consistent advantage over GRPO.
AI.conclusion: Policy-implied value optimization can provide finer credit assignment while preserving the practical simplicity of critic-free training, offering a better trade-off than existing methods.

title: U$^2$Mamba: A Two-level Nested U-structure Mamba for Salient Object Detection
summary: Mamba-based models have emerged as a promising alternative for salient object detection (SOD), offering significant advantages in modeling long sequences. However, existing models often fail to explore contextual information and the depth of the entire architecture. This paper introduces U$^2$Mamba, a powerful and innovative U-structured network for salient object detection. We propose multiscale Mamba U-blocks (MMUBs) that enhance the model depth to improve local feature extraction capabilities. Our newly developed nested U-structure, incorporating MMUBs, enables the network to integrate various receptive fields from shallow and deep layers, thereby collecting richer contextual information and longer-range data without being constrained by resolution. Instead of using the traditional deep supervision scheme and top-level supervised training, we propose a hierarchical training supervision method where the loss is computed at each level during the training process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that U$^2$Mamba achieves highly competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods. The source code is available at \url{https://github.com/JL021/U2Mamba}.

AI.tldr: U$^2$Mamba is a U-structured Mamba-based network for salient object detection that uses multiscale Mamba U-blocks (MMUBs) and a nested U-structure to enhance contextual information and long-range modeling, with hierarchical training supervision instead of traditional deep supervision, achieving competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Existing Mamba-based SOD models fail to fully explore contextual information and architectural depth for effective salient object detection.
AI.method: Proposes multiscale Mamba U-blocks (MMUBs) to increase model depth and local feature extraction; a nested U-structure integrating MMUBs to collect multi-receptive-field contextual information without resolution constraints; and a hierarchical training supervision method with loss computed at each level.
AI.result: U$^2$Mamba achieves highly competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods on salient object detection benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture effectively combines Mamba's long-sequence modeling with enhanced depth and multi-scale contextual information, outperforming existing approaches.

title: Self-Adaptive Scale Handling for Forecasting Time Series with Scale Heterogeneity
comment: This is the full version of the paper accepted by the IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP). The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/Meteor-Stars/ASTSF
summary: Current time series forecasting (TSF) research predominantly focuses on scale-homogeneous data, where different time series share similar numerical magnitude ranges. However, in real-world industrial scenarios such as financial product sales, different time series often differ by orders of magnitude (scale heterogeneity). Since these series share similar temporal patterns, joint modeling is desirable for better data utilization, yet existing scaling methods either compress low-scale signals (global normalization) or destroy semantic discriminability and amplify inverse-scaling errors (window-based scaling). This paper proposes a self-Adaptive Scale-handling (AS) module that learns adaptive scale factors tailored to each input, preserving semantic discriminability while reducing inverse-scaling errors. AS consists of Scale Calibrating (SC), which calibrates prior mean scaling factors through neural networks, and Scaling Selection (SS), which decides whether to apply calibration or retain the original factor, avoiding over-calibration. Experiments on real-world fund sales datasets from Ant Fortune and Alipay show that AS seamlessly integrates into popular TSF models and consistently improves their performance. The code and dataset are available at the link https://github.com/Meteor-Stars/ASTSF.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a self-Adaptive Scale-handling (AS) module for time series forecasting on scale-heterogeneous data, incorporating Scale Calibrating and Scaling Selection mechanisms to preserve semantic discriminability while reducing inverse-scaling errors, showing consistent improvements on real-world fund sales datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing TSF research focuses on scale-homogeneous data, but real-world industrial scenarios often involve scale-heterogeneous time series (differing by orders of magnitude). Current scaling methods either compress low-scale signals (global normalization) or destroy semantic discriminability and amplify inverse-scaling errors (window-based scaling).
AI.method: The AS module has two components: (1) Scale Calibrating (SC) calibrates prior mean scaling factors through neural networks, and (2) Scaling Selection (SS) decides whether to apply calibration or retain the original factor to avoid over-calibration.
AI.result: AS seamlessly integrates into popular TSF models and consistently improves their performance on real-world fund sales datasets from Ant Fortune and Alipay.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive scaling approach effectively handles scale heterogeneity in time series forecasting, offering a plug-and-play solution that enhances existing models without requiring architectural changes.

title: CMDS-AD: Cross-Modal Dual-Stream Decoupling for Few-Shot Anomaly Detection
summary: Few-shot anomaly detection remains challenging due to limited training data. Multi-modal anomaly detection (MAD) offers a viable solution, leveraging 3D geometric cues to enrich 2D RGB representations and compensate for this scarcity. However, existing MAD methods apply spatially uniform feature processing, conflating stable macroscopic structures with high-frequency localized defect signals, exacerbating cross-modal misalignment and inflating false-positive rates. To overcome this, we present CMDS-AD, a Cross-Modal Dual-Stream Anomaly Detection framework. A LoRA-guided diffusion model generates diverse RGB samples to mitigate extreme data scarcity. For 3D normal augmentation, we employ a pre-trained diffusion model as a normal estimator. Crucially, this estimator inherently acts as a non-linear low-pass filter, directly extracting low-frequency normal representations from RGB inputs. This establishes an auxiliary estimated stream of purely low-frequency information, anchoring robust structural templates and assisting the uncompressed real stream, containing coupled high- and low-frequency components, to precisely isolate micro-defects. A Coordinate-Aware Hierarchical Feature Mapper adaptively aligns cross-modal semantics, while a multiplicative scoring mechanism filters modality-specific noise. Under the extreme 1-shot setting, CMDS-AD achieves absolute performance gains of 5.7% (I-AUROC) and 2.0% (AUPRO) on MVTec 3D-AD, alongside 7.7% and 5.6% improvements on EyeCandies, establishing a new state-of-the-art.

AI.tldr: CMDS-AD is a cross-modal dual-stream anomaly detection framework that uses frequency decomposition (via a diffusion model as a low-pass filter) to separate normal structural signals from defect signals, achieving state-of-the-art few-shot anomaly detection on MVTec 3D-AD and EyeCandies benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-modal anomaly detection (MAD) methods apply spatially uniform feature processing, which conflates stable macroscopic structures with high-frequency localized defect signals, leading to cross-modal misalignment and inflated false-positive rates, especially under extreme few-shot settings.
AI.method: CMDS-AD consists of: (1) a LoRA-guided diffusion model for RGB data augmentation; (2) a pre-trained diffusion model used as a normal estimator that inherently acts as a non-linear low-pass filter to extract low-frequency normal representations; (3) a dual-stream architecture with an auxiliary estimated stream (purely low-frequency) and an uncompressed real stream (coupled high- and low-frequency); (4) a Coordinate-Aware Hierarchical Feature Mapper for cross-modal alignment; and (5) a multiplicative scoring mechanism for modality-specific noise filtering.
AI.result: Under 1-shot setting, CMDS-AD achieves absolute gains of 5.7% (I-AUROC) and 2.0% (AUPRO) on MVTec 3D-AD, and 7.7% and 5.6% improvements on EyeCandies, establishing new state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: The frequency-decomposition dual-stream paradigm effectively addresses cross-modal misalignment in few-shot MAD by separating normal structural signals from defect signals, significantly boosting detection performance under extreme data scarcity.

title: Hierarchical Control in Multi-Agent Games: LLM-based Planning and RL Execution
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved strong performance in sequential decision-making, yet scaling to complex multi-agent environments remains challenging due to sparse rewards, large state-action spaces, and the difficulty of learning coordinated strategies. We propose a hierarchical architecture where a pretrained large language model (LLM) acts as a centralized strategic controller that selects among specialized RL skill policies for a team of agents, while RL policies handle reactive low-level execution. We evaluate this hybrid system in a competitive 2v2 King of the Hill environment against behavior tree (BT) and \emph{``Flat''} RL (end-to-end training without skill decomposition) baselines. The LLM+RL system achieves task performance statistically equivalent to hand-crafted BT (46.4\% vs 51.5\% win rate, $p=0.103$) while both significantly outperform Flat RL trained without skill decomposition. A user study ($n=15$) reveals that 60\% of participants perceive LLM+RL agents as the most human-like ($p=0.027$), citing behavioral adaptability and tactical variability. These results demonstrate that pretrained LLM reasoning can effectively orchestrate pretrained RL skills, achieving competitive multi-agent coordination and superior perceived believability without manual rule engineering.

AI.tldr: A hybrid LLM+RL hierarchical system uses a pretrained LLM as a centralized strategic controller to select among specialized RL skill policies for multi-agent coordination, achieving task performance comparable to hand-crafted behavior trees (46.4% vs 51.5% win rate, p=0.103) and significantly higher perceived human-likeness (60% of participants, p=0.027) than Flat RL and behavior tree baselines in a 2v2 competitive environment.
AI.motivation: Scaling RL to complex multi-agent environments is challenging due to sparse rewards, large state-action spaces, and difficulty learning coordinated strategies; existing approaches lack effective hierarchical decomposition that leverages common-sense reasoning for strategic coordination.
AI.method: A hierarchical architecture combining a pretrained LLM as a centralized strategic controller that selects among specialized RL skill policies at a high level, while RL policies handle reactive low-level execution; evaluated in a 2v2 King of the Hill environment against BT and Flat RL baselines.
AI.result: LLM+RL achieves statistically equivalent win rate (46.4%) to hand-crafted BT (51.5%, p=0.103), both significantly outperforming Flat RL; 60% of participants in a user study (n=15) rated LLM+RL agents as most human-like (p=0.027), citing behavioral adaptability and tactical variability.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained LLM reasoning can effectively orchestrate pretrained RL skills for competitive multi-agent coordination and superior perceived believability without manual rule engineering.

title: CUPID: Reconstructing UV Texture Maps for Interpretable Person-of-Interest Deepfake Detection
summary: Deepfakes targeting a high-profile individual, known as Person-of-Interest (POI), are a threat to modern democracies and societies. Current POI deepfake detection methods still struggle to combine robustness to post-processing, efficiency and interpretability, focal aspects of modern deepfake detectors. In this paper we propose CUPID, a POI video deepfake detector that combines UV texture maps, a facial appearance representation derived from 3D face reconstructions, with the representation learning capabilities of the Masked Autoencoder (MAE). Our method does not require any deepfake videos in its training phase. Moreover, it does not even require to include a specific POI in the training set: the combination of UV texture maps extracted from real video frames and the MAE context-guided reconstruction yields a latent space that captures rich and discriminative facial features also for identities unseen during training. In the testing phase, the embeddings extracted from a query video depicting the POI can be matched against pristine reference videos to assess the video authenticity. Furthermore, operating in the UV space naturally provides an additional layer of interpretability. Specifically, we can extract decoded residual maps that highlight which facial regions of a test video deviate most from the identity representation of the corresponding POI. Experiments on four deepfake datasets show that CUPID outperforms current state of the art on most datasets and achieves the best overall robustness against strong downscaling and compression, providing also substantially faster inference. Our experimental code will be released at https://github.com/polimi-ispl/CUPID.

AI.tldr: CUPID is a POI video deepfake detector using UV texture maps from 3D face reconstructions combined with Masked Autoencoder (MAE) representation learning.
